FIGURE 1.15 ChoudWest Bank - Scenario 5.

In  aggregate,  in  common  with  many  initiatives  designed  to boost market share, ChoudWest suffers from both an NII and NIM perspective.

Total interest income remains constant at £972.5m ,  but interest expense  increases  to £542.5m (up  £31.0m).  Interest  earning  assets have  risen  to £16.5bn ,  so  both  NII  ( £430.0m ,  down  £31.0m)  and NIM ( 2.61% , down 0.28%) fall relative to the Base Scenario.

## Scenario 5 - Mortgage Growth (see Figure 1.15)

In this scenario, ChoudWest Bank is aggressively marketing its fixed and variable-rate mortgage products to boost market share. The new lending is  funded  by  a  combination  of  new  fixed  rate  retail  deposits  and  short-  term  inter-bank  deposits.  As  mortgages  are  generally low risk, the strategy is likely to boost the overall asset quality of the balance sheet. Whilst the new assets generate a return that is higher than the cost of the additional borrowing, and therefore boost overall income, the NIM is dragged down as the new business is not as profitable as the existing business in the Base Scenario.

Total interest income rises to £1,052.5m (up £80.0m) and interest expense increases to £582.0m (up £70.5m). Interest earning assets have  grown  to £17.50bn ,  so  although  NII  is  up  to £470.5m (up £9.5m), NIM at 2.69% is down 0.19% relative to the Base Scenario.

FIGURE 1.16

<!-- image -->

ChoudWest Bank - Summary of scenario results.

## NII and NIM Sensitivity

The main conclusions we can draw from the case study in Example 1.5 are that NII and NIM could be affected by movements in the general level of interest rates and changes in the composition of the balance sheet (the results are summarised in Figure 1.16). Focusing on the former, the reason why movements in interest rates affect the calculation of NII and NIM is because the assets and liabilities in ChoudWest's balance sheet are re-  pricing at different times. The term 're-  pricing' refers to the time when the interest rate applicable on an asset or liability changes. For example, if the interest rate on a 5-  year loan is linked to the 3-  month average of the overnight rate in the money markets (referred to as SONIA for GBP), then the loan would re-  price every 3 months. In the ChoudWest Bank example, the interest rate on variable-rate products is changing as soon as there is a movement in the general level of market rates, whilst the interest rate on fixed rate products remains the same until the fixed rate products mature or are repaid.

Another way of describing the behaviour of the assets and liabilities in the ChoudWest Bank balance sheet is to say they are re-  pricing off different points in the yield curve. In Chapter 4 we discuss yield curves in detail, but for this discussion we can describe a yield curve as a representation of the interest rates at which a given borrower is able to borrow funds or transact business, at a point in time, across a range of different maturities, in a given currency, for example, the £ sterling yield curve for the British Government or the £ sterling yield curve for a given bank. The yield curve is sometimes referred to as the 'term structure of interest rates', but strictly speaking the terms are not synonymous as the latter expression should be used to describe only a zero-  coupon yield curve.

Figure 1.17 shows a selection of yield curves for UK banks, taken as a collective, during 2017-2021. We notice that the level and shape of the yield curve changes over time.

Economists have devised a variety of theories to explain the shape of the yield curve, which is typically, but not always, upward sloping from left to

FIGURE 1.17 UK bank yield curves, 2017-2021. Source: Bank of England.

<!-- image -->

TABLE 1.9 Traditional theories explaining the shape of the yield curve.

| Theory               | Explanation                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                             |
|----------------------|---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| Market expectation   | The shape of the yield curve is based upon the market's view of future interest rates.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                  |
| Liquidity preference | It is riskier for lenders to commit funds for longer, so they need to be compensated with higher returns for doing so.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                  |
| Market segmentation  | Borrowers, lenders and investors have differing financial needs and time frames according to their business, cash flows and goals, leading to differing supply and demand at different points along the yield curve. For example, pension funds and infrastructure lending create supply and demand in the long end, while trade finance and cash surpluses influence the short end of the yield curve. |
| Preference habitat   | An extension of market segmentation. Investors have a preference for a certain investment horizon and demand a premium to commit funds outside this period.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                             |

right, such as market expectation theory, liquidity preference theory, market segmentation theory and preference habitat theory. These are discussed further in Chapter 4; we summarise them in Table 1.9.

The shape of the yield curve is important to banks, as it provides a guide to the rate at which existing business will re-  price and new business will be written. If assets and liabilities don't do this at exactly the same time, in the same volumes, then the bank in question will have an NII and a NIM sensitivity to movements in interest rates, as we have seen in the ChoudWest Bank case study. As a simple example, if a 5-  year fixed rate loan for £10,000 on which the customer is charged an interest rate of 10% is funded by a 1-  month deposit on which the bank pays 2%, the annualised income to the bank would be £800. However, if the level of interest rates were to increase so that the bank had to pay 4% for a 1-  month deposit, the bank's annualised NII would fall to £600, when the existing 1-  month deposit matures and is  replaced  but  the  fixed  rate  loan  remains.  Interest-rate  sensitivity  arises because the deposit and the loan are not re-  pricing at the same time. The management of this exposure, otherwise known as Interest Rate Risk in the Banking Book (IRRBB) is one of the roles of the Asset and Liability Management desk and is discussed further in Chapter 7.

## Managing the NIM

In Table 1.4 we discussed the factors influencing the level of a bank's NIM. Banks  can  take  actions  to  protect  their  NIMs  or  seek  to  increase  them. Examples of a range of possible initiatives are shown in Table 1.10.

TABLE 1.10 Examples of actions that can be taken to manage or protect a bank's NIM.

| Initiative                                                                                                                                                                                                  | Potential NIM outcome                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                      |
|-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| Increase the proportion of riskier assets in the balance sheet, e.g., sell government bonds buy corporate bonds.                                                                                            | The riskier assets will yield a higher interest rate, which may boost NIM in the short term (but maybe not in the longer term due to potential increased credit losses).                                                                                                                   |
| Distribute bank products to match the interest rate characteristics of assets/liabilities already on the balance sheet. For example, sell 2-year fixed rate deposits alongside 2-year fixed rate mortgages. | This action would reduce NII sensitivity and therefore protect the NIM.                                                                                                                                                                                                                    |
| Consciously engineer a mismatch in the re-pricing maturities of assets and liabilities.                                                                                                                     | As the yield curve is typically upward sloping, banks can often boost the NIM by bor- rowing for a shorter term and lending for a slightly longer term. However, as such an action would increase a bank's interest-rate risk, it would need to be taken within an approved risk appetite. |
| Enter into interest-rate derivative contracts, such as interest-rate swaps.                                                                                                                                 | Derivatives can be employed to create syn- thetic assets and liabilities to achieve the outcomes discussed in the two examples earlier.                                                                                                                                                    |
| Increase non-interest-bearing liabilities                                                                                                                                                                   | Engineering an increase in the level of non-interest-bearing current accounts, or retained earnings or equity through a rights issue would all tend to boost a bank's NIM.                                                                                                                 |
| Reduce non-interest-earning assets                                                                                                                                                                          | Any action to reduce the level of non-interest -earning assets, such as cash balances, in the balance sheet will likely lead to an increase in the NIM through reduced funding costs and interest expense.                                                                                 |

## Forecasting NII and the NIM

If we accept that NII/NIM is an important aspect of a bank's P&amp;L, it makes sense to generate forecasts of what it will be in future years to:

- ■ understand likely trends (e.g., to inform trading updates on likely performance by public companies to the market);

- ■ consider what management actions (tactical or strategic initiatives) are possible/necessary to improve the position, if income levels are expected to decline (or not grow as quickly as competitors);
- ■ provide a sound base from which to conduct scenario analysis and stress testing activity.

Forecasts can be developed using either a top-  down approach or a granular bottom-  up model, depending upon the size and sophistication of the bank. In a top-  down model, forecasts will be based upon an assessment of recent trends at an aggregate level, for example, for each product.

Banks pursuing such an approach would not normally have integrated systems to manage, forecast and stress test NIM. Instead, they would rely upon off-  line calculations/spreadsheets by divisions and business units.

Account level information would normally be used in a granular bottom-  up model. Although most of the required data is available from transaction systems (and from business plan assumptions), it is a substantial task to organise it. A hypothetical example of (some of) the data attributes required at account level is shown in Figure 1.18.

In simple terms, a granular NII or NIM forecasting model works by identifying key attributes for each asset and liability in the balance sheet such as amount, margin to reference rate, anticipated repayment profile and maturity - then using a forecast of key reference rates to generate expected future interest income or expense for each asset or liability in the existing book. The same information is collated for anticipated new business volumes, which enables NII for new business to be calculated. Combining the two elements produces a forecast of NII. Stressing the interest rates and reperforming all the calculations (possibly with different repayment and new business assumptions) enables NII sensitivity to interest rate movements to be assessed.

The  key  challenges  are  generally:  establishing  data  feeds  of  account level attributes from transaction systems to the NIM model and the quality of the account level data.

## CHAPTER SUMMARY

We defined banking as the act of accepting deposits, to be held in safe keeping and in some instances attracting interest, and of originating loans (all of which do accrue interest) to customers. Customers come in many different shapes and sizes, ranging from private individuals to governments and groups of countries.

The act  of  undertaking  creates  a  balance  sheet  of  loans  (assets)  and deposits  (liabilities),  and  the  non-  customer  share  of  the  liabilities  side  of the  balance  sheet  is  the  bank's  own  funds,  called  equity  or  capital. This balance sheet must be managed continuously to ensure that the various risks it faces - be it risks to capital, profit, liquidity and so on - do not render the bank unviable, as a going concern, at any time. A familiarity with balance sheets and the items on the bank's income statement, or P&amp;L statement, is a prerequisite to understanding managing balance sheet risk.

<!-- image -->

FIGURE 1.18 Example account level data attributes required for a NIM model.

<!-- image -->

Banks are part of the global money markets, the market in short-  term borrowing and lending, as well as the capital markets.

The  process  of  setting  the  interest  rate  on  a  customer  loan  follows orthodox principles of corporate finance, but there are variations on how loans are priced in different banks. The pricing inputs used in loan pricing are not universal.

We reviewed the products offered  by  banks  and  considered  whether they generate NII, fee and commission or other income. NII, calculated as the  interest  income  received  on  lending  less  the  interest  expense  paid  on liabilities,  has  been  shown  to  be  a  key  element  of  bank  profits,  typically accounting for 40-80% of total income.

The NIM, which is defined  as  NII  divided  by  a  measure  of  average assets, represents an important contributor to bank profitability. In the UK, Europe and USA, average bank NIMs range between 1% and 2.5% on an annualised basis. There is no optimal level for NIM, as it will depend upon the composition of individual bank balance sheets. However, the measure is  key to valuations performed by bank analysts, so banks should aim to preserve and maximise whatever NIM they have.

## APPENDIX 1.1 SUMMARY OVERVIEW OF BANK PRODUCT LINE

## DEFINITION OF BANKING

A definition of banking is available in a number of textbooks. For example, an accessible one is Modern Banking by Shelagh Heffernan (2005). The following are just two of many hundreds of definitions on the Internet:

Engaging in the business of keeping money for savings and checking accounts or for exchange or for issuing loans and credit, etc. www.wordnetweb.princeton.edu/perl/webwn

A bank is a financial  institution  that  accepts  deposits  and  channels  those  deposits  into  lending  activities.  Banks  primarily  provide  financial  services  to  customers  while  enriching  investors.

Government restrictions on financial activities by banks vary over time and location.

www.en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Banking

The author kept his definition of banking brief, as we noted on page 4.

## PRODUCT LINE

A summary of the main product types offered by banks can be found in the first edition (Choudhry, 2012), which is also reproduced on the accompanying website for the second edition. Not all banks offer all these products, but these instruments are available in virtually every banking market. More detailed information is available in the following books by the author: The Money Markets Handbook (Choudhry, 2005), Fixed Income Markets, Second edition (Choudhry, 2014) and Bank Asset and Liability Management (Choudhry, 2007).

## APPENDIX 1.2 OVERNIGHT REFERENCE RATES (RFR) 16

The  following  exhibits  illustrate  the  mechanics  of  overnight  average interest rates.

FIGURE A1.1 Future interest payments using RFRs.

<!-- image -->

16  This section was authored by Periklis Thivaios.

## Interest compounding options

Simple and compound averages reflect a technical difference in how interest is accrued

## Simple (arithmetic) interest

- ■ The additional amount of interest owed each day is  calculated  by  applying  the  daily  rate of interest to the principal borrowed, and the payment due at the end of the period is the sum of those amounts
- ■ Does not take into account unpaid interest that needs to be capitalised on the principal

## Compound (geometric) interest

- ■ The  additional  amount  of  interest  owed is calculated by  compounding  the  daily RFR to produce a compounded rate for the period, and applying the compounded rate to the principal

FIGURE A1.2 Interest compounding options.

Source: © Periklis Thivaios. Reproduced with permission.

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

## Where

d b = the number of business days in the interest period

d c = the number of calendar days in the interest period

r i = the interest rate applicable on business day i

n i = the number of calendar days for which rate r i applies (normally n i =1 for Monday to Thursday, n i =3 on Friday, n i =2 before a holiday)

N = the market convention for quoting number of days (US - N=360; UK - N=365)

## Practically

- ■ The  difference  between  the  two  approaches  is  typically quite small, especially at lower interest rates and over short periods of time

## Abstract

Banks are a key part of the national and global economy and their impact is felt across society. For this reason it is an imperative that they conduct their business along sound professional lines and displaying the highest ethical standards. To do this requires that banks consider all their stakeholders in the ordinary course of their business. A bank's customers are no less important a stakeholder than the bank's shareholders and regulators in this regard. This chapter describes a goodpractice approach to adopt to ensure high ethical standards are displayed by the bank in all its dealings, and the importance of professional ethics embedded in the bank's culture in ensuring good conduct, good customer service and minimal reputational risk.

T his chapter is mainly about doing the right thing. The right thing for the bank, and the right thing for the bank's customers. Originally the author wanted to call the chapter, 'Do Good Work' or 'Doing the Right Thing', but settled for the more orthodox sounding title we have here because the snazzier title might have come across as presumptuous. It's a very important topic, possibly the most important one of the 'principles' of banking. So why wasn't it covered at all in the first edition? Mainly because in his naivety the author had assumed that one could take this whole topic for granted, that everyone in a bank would always act in the best interests of all the bank's stakeholders, and particularly its customers. As ongoing events in  this  century  have  made  clear,  this  is  an  unrealistic  assumption.  But  in any case, can one actually teach sound values and ethics - let alone learn them - via a textbook? Perhaps. Perhaps not. But in this second edition we've deemed the subject to be one not just of importance but also worthy of exposition, so tarnished and sullied has the reputation and image of banks and banking become since 2008. Ultimately corporate behaviour is a reflection of the collective behaviour of a group of individuals, and corporate culture reflects the culture that those individuals espouse and follow.

The author defines ethics as:

Doing the right thing when no-  one is looking.

## Ethics, Conduct and Customer

## CHAPTER  2 Service

If an individual is not minded to follow this principle, then a reading of this chapter will make no difference to them.

This chapter is set out as follows: a discussion of the bank customer; 'know your  customer'  and  how  this  is  required  as  part  of  appropriate product offerings; ethics and customer service; ethics and conduct risk; and ethics and reputation risk.

## A BANK'S CUSTOMERS

It is straightforward to define a customer of a bank: a person or entity that has entered into a contractual relationship with the bank. As soon as the bank has opened up an account (loan or deposit) for a customer or entered into an agreement to provide any form of its services to the customer, that person or entity is a customer of the bank. From that point, and until the contractual relationship or agreement has expired, providing 'good' service to  the  customer  is  an  essential  prerequisite  of  the  principles  of  banking. More on that later. Global rules on anti-  money laundering (AML) requires bankers to 'know your customer' (KYC), and we will discuss that later as well. In essence, every customer must be known to the bank, and this must be the case for individual persons as well as for legal entities. This identity must be established before entering into any contractual agreement. 1

Note  that  compliance  rules  surrounding AML  and  KYC  are  a  large topic in their own right and are not covered in this book: interested readers should consult the titles referenced at the end of this chapter.

## Customer Relationship

The bank-  customer relationship is a straightforward one; in essence it is that of a debtor-  creditor one. Either the customer is the debtor or the creditor, or very commonly both across different transactions. A bank deposit is a loan from the customer to the bank, and the bank is the debtor, obliged to

1  When employed as an executive director at one particular bank, the author was

dismayed and disappointed to discover that this bank was still in the business of lending money to offshore domiciled 'special purpose vehicles' involved in property loan transactions, when many other banks in the UK had ceased lending to such SPVs for anti-  money laundering reasons. An SPV domiciled in an exotic far-  away location entering into a loan to purchase property in London adds to the complication of the 'know your customer' process - who exactly is the beneficial owner of the SPV? Sadly this bank saw no problem in undertaking such transactions.

repay the loan to the customer - often with interest - on demand from the customer under the terms of the contractual agreement. This may be immediately, as with a current account or call deposit account, or after some fixed period of time or notice of withdrawal. In a loan agreement the bank is the creditor and the customer is the debtor. The debt needs to be repaid - with interest - when due under the contractual terms. For loan products such as overdrafts there is no specified loan repayment date, although the facility often needs to be renewed annually and the terms may state repayment is required in 30 days on request from the creditor.

The  main  form  of  customer  relationship  in  banking  is  the  debtorcreditor one, but there are two other forms of relationship as well: these are the  agent-  principal  relationship  and  the  fiduciary  relationship.  Some  services offered by banks are not loan or deposit products; common examples of this are wealth management services and safe custody services. In these arrangements the bank is acting as an agent on behalf of the customer who is the principal. Another product offered by some banks is trustee services; in this case there is a fiduciary relationship with the customer, as the bank is acting as a trustee for a beneficiary (the customer), and owes a duty of care to the customer to act in its best interests. This particular function has a legal definition.

That said, the author would suggest that a bank has a duty of care to all its customers, and should act in the customer's best interest at all times. This objective is not limited to fiduciary relationships.

In  general  the  customer  relationship  is  ended  when  the  transaction expires, unless the customer has other accounts with the bank. A customer can terminate a relationship simply on request, at which point the account is closed. If it is a deposit account the bank will transfer any positive balance to another account held at another bank or by a banker's draft (rarer and rarer these days!). If it is a loan account with a continuing debit balance, the loan contractual payments must still be paid as and when due. Loan accounts generally close when the loan has been repaid.

A bank can close an account by providing written notice to the customer, usually 30 days' notice. There is no need for the bank to provide a reason; it may deem the deposit account holder to be a front for money laundering, or it may deem the credit card holder to be no longer an acceptable credit risk. There is quite often a sound reason for a bank wishing to close a customer's account. However, it is good practice, and good customer service principles, to provide a reason whenever doing so. Financial service products are very important to individuals and corporate entities alike, and closing them at 30 days' notice is often a cause for considerable stress to customers. Any communication about account closure should bear this in mind and also be written in 'human' terms rather than as bureaucratic platitude-  speak.

## Customer Types

We should be aware of the different types of customer that a bank may engage with, not simply for its own sake but also because different customers have different requirements when it comes to financial services. This is all part of 'know your customer'.

We can categorise customer types as follows:

- ■ Retail individual (personal) customers;
- ■ Sole traders;
- ■ Partnership entities;
- ■ Clubs, associations and societies;
- ■ Trustees;
- ■ Private limited liability corporate legal entities;
- ■ Public corporate listed limited liability entities;
- ■ Sovereign authorities.

We describe each briefly in turn.

- ■ Retail  customers: the  retail  customer  is  the  private  individual  and  for most  banks  their  most  numerous  customer  type.  Such  customers  will range from those requiring a few simple services (current account, savings account) to those requiring the full range of products a bank might offer. Retail customers under the age of 18, the age in most jurisdictions at which a person can enter into a contractual arrangement, are not allowed to open current accounts or loan accounts but are often allowed to open savings accounts.
- ■ Sole traders: this is a type of 'business' customer but comprised of one individual who has not set up a separate legal entity to conduct business through. Such customer relationships are conducted in a similar way as with retail customers.
- ■ Partnership entities: this  is  a  diverse  customer grouping. Traditionally certain professional service providers such as lawyers and accounting firms operated as partnerships, where the firm is owned by two or more partners (in some countries there is a limit placed on the number of partners in the firm). Some partnerships can be very large entities with hundreds of millions in turnover, and as such the customer relationship will be similar to that for a large corporate. Traditionally partners would be fully liable for all the business obligations of the partnership. This has led to the introduction of limited liability partnerships, which limits the liability of partners in the same way as that of shareholders of a conventional limited liability corporate entity. The customer relationship in

- either case is between the bank and the partnership entity, and not with individual partners of the firm.
- ■ Clubs, associations and societies: this a catch-  all category for those types of  partnerships  that  operate  in  a  not-  for-  profit  capacity.  The  author plays for an amateur football club in the West Sussex Football League, Newdigate FC, that comes into this category (it banks with NatWest, as a matter of interest!). The key information requirement for a bank is to know precisely which individuals in the society have authority to instruct operations on its bank account. This is as important for a current account product as it is with an overdraft facility: the responsibility of the relevant individual members of the society should be known and fixed. As with a corporate customer, a bank will wish to see the society's rules, articles, laws and constitution. The customer relationship will be with the society and not individual members.
- ■ Trustees: this is a category of 'corporate' customer because a trust is a separate and distinct legal entity in the same way as a corporate, and will have need of certain deposit products and in some cases loan products. It would report its income, for income tax purposes, in the same way as any corporate entity. For this reason this category of customer can be viewed as a subset of the category we describe next.
- ■ Private limited liability legal entities: these are 'corporate' customers and again this category represents a diverse group of entities. At the minimum all  limited  liability  companies  are  described  in  their  'Memorandum' and 'Articles of Association', although in practice these are very generic documents. There are defined rules - from the regulatory authorities - on what constitutes a 'small or medium-  sized enterprise' (SME) and what is otherwise a 'large' corporate. For example, when the author was Treasurer of the corporate banking division at the Royal Bank of Scotland the business  line  was  split  into 'Business  &amp;  Commercial'  and 'Corporate &amp; Institutional': the former comprised all corporate customers with an annual turnover of £50 million or less, and the latter those whose turnover exceeded £50 million. However, there is a world of difference between an entity whose turnover is £200,000, £1 million or £49 million. So even the B&amp;C division was dealing with very different types of customers, with different requirements. A limited liability company is a legal entity in its own right, and one that is separate from its owners, who are the company's shareholders. The liability of shareholders under any circumstances is limited to their paid-  up share capital. The interesting thing about such companies, particularly as they get larger and larger, is that while the company is owned by its shareholders, it is managed by directors who may or may not be shareholders. This is particularly pronounced in large companies, where the senior executives of the company are employees and not shareholders, but who in theory run the company for the ultimate benefit of the owners.

- ■ Public corporate listed entities: this category of customer is distinct from the private entity because it describes an entity whose shares are listed on a stock exchange, and hence may be traded at any time. There is a cross-  over  with  private  corporate  entities,  in  that  some  very  large  corporates may be privately owned and some minnows may be listed and owned publicly.
- ■ Sovereign authorities: national governments and their various departments, local authorities and associated public sector bodies all require financial services, both loan and deposit product. Hence this is a distinct  category  of  customer  for  some  banks,  particularly  larger  banks that have separate business lines or relationship management desks to cater for these customers.

## ANTI-  MONEY LAUNDERING AND KNOW YOUR CUSTOMER

There are uniform regulations worldwide, under various names such as AntiMoney Laundering and Anti-  Terrorist Financing Act (UK) that dictate the responsibilities of banks with respect to money laundering. The effectiveness of banks to police this aspect of global criminal activity is questionable, nevertheless government laws provide a baseline framework and process for all banks to follow. We do not cover this topic in  the  book,  and  only  introduce it. The references at the end of this chapter are recommended for interested readers.

## Anti-  M o n e y   L a u n d e r i n g   R e g u l a t i o n s

All  banks  worldwide  are  designated  by  their  supervision  authorities  as 'reporting  institutions'  hence  are  obliged  to  follow  legislation  regarding money laundering. Failure to be seen to be observing such regulation can have severe implications and penalties for banks.

What exactly is money laundering? Put simply it is the process of transforming 'dirty money' into seemingly 'clean money' via a series of transactions. Dirty money is of course the proceeds of criminal activity. By turning such proceeds into funds that appear to have been acquired legitimately, the criminal is able to enjoy the fruits of their illegal activities with less worry of being questioned by law enforcement authorities.

As anyone employed in the banking sector knows, from the countless number of times they have been required to pass AML 'online training', money laundering by the criminal involves three distinct stages:

- ■ Placement: the first stage where the criminal introduces the dirty money traditionally in the form of cash but today as likely to be in the form of 'crypto' currency - into the banking system. A common method would be to set up a legitimate business, such as a jewellery shop or a mobile phone repair shop, and bank the cash takings from that enterprise each week along with an amount of dirty money mixed in; another might be to set up an SPV in a tax haven or other offshore jurisdiction and then set up a bank account for the SPV in a recognised money centre jurisdiction, which is then used to introduce dirty money into a more respectable location.
- ■ Layering: the more complex process of entering into a series of transactions, perhaps involving cross-  border transfers, in order to create a more complex audit trail and make it harder for regulators and law enforcement agencies to trace the origin of any funds that appear questionable as to source.
- ■ Integration: using the now cleaner looking money to purchase legitimate assets, for example property in the West End of London or a football club, and there being no suspicion that the funds were the result of illegal activities.

Thence we might define money laundering as:

- ■ Entering  into  a  transaction  using  the  proceeds  gained  from  criminal activity, where the person or persons are aware that the funds have been realised illegally.
- ■ Transferring  dirty  money  across  bank  accounts,  or  using  it  to  acquire  assets.
- ■ Hiding the source of funds or attempting to persuade a bank that the source is legitimate.

There are further definitions but these are the core ones. In every jurisdiction engaging in money laundering is deemed to be a criminal activity, with fines and/or imprisonment being the punishment that can be expected if found guilty of the crime.

## Main Requirements in Money Laundering Regulation

In order to comply with money laundering regulations a bank has to implement formal procedures, overseen by the compliance officer, to minimise the chances that it is being used to launder money. In the UK, for example, there is a specific senior executive role known as the money laundering reporting officer (MLRO); this is designated a 'senior management function' within the country's senior management and certification regime (SMCR).

The essential requirements are as follows.

## Record Keeping

As banks are designated 'reporting institutions' they are required to keep detailed records of all transactions, on either side of the balance sheet, above a certain minimum size. This size differs by jurisdiction, and in some countries there is a lower level for cash deposits than electronic deposits. This will include data on:

- ■ date and time;
- ■ transaction size;
- ■ name and address (or registered address) of the person or entity who is instructing the transaction;
- ■ name and address (or registered address) of the person or entity that is the other side of the transaction;
- ■ the bank accounts involved;
- ■ the nature of the transaction (deposit, withdrawal, drawdown, foreign exchange, and so on);
- ■ the counterparty bank or banks involved in the transaction.

In most jurisdictions there are rules on which authorities (Inland Revenue, police, and so on) must be informed for certain types of transactions.

## Reporting Transactions

In today's market, banks will have set up warning triggers in their automated systems to generate alerts for all transactions that are outside certain specified parameters. Indeed in some cases banks have implemented machine learning systems 2  that predict when and where suspicious transactions will occur. Generally there will be a designated arm of the central  bank,  or  police  agency,  or  both  to  which  suspicious  transactions should be reported. A failure to report is itself a criminal offence in most jurisdictions.

2  Machine learning is a branch of what is commonly termed  'artificial intelligence'. The author's publication on this topic is referenced at the end of the chapter.

## Know Your Customer

Every bank must establish the identity of all its new customers, and in many jurisdictions on an ongoing basis of all its existing corporate customers. This identity and domicile must be confirmed before any transaction between the customer and the bank can take place. This verification of customer identity is the baseline requirement of the 'know your customer' principle, and is part of a bank's 'due diligence' on all its customers before it enters into a contractual relationship with them. At an absolute minimum the information that must be provided by the customer (and documentary evidence to the fact supplied alongside) includes:

- ■ name and address (registered address for corporate entities);
- ■ for retail customers identity number (such as passport number or driving  licence  number)  and  for  corporate  customers  company  registration number;
- ■ for retail customers date of birth and for corporate customers date of incorporation;
- ■ nationality and tax domicile.

For corporate customers there are further data requirements such as the Memo and Articles we described previously and the authorised signatories of the entity.

There is a class of customer that is designated 'higher risk', and for these customers the due diligence and data gathering required are more onerous. For example, for retail customers these might be 'politically exposed persons' or PEPs, described in Wikipedia as:

In financial regulation, a politically exposed person is one who has been entrusted with a prominent public function. A PEP generally presents a higher risk for potential involvement in bribery and corruption by virtue of their position and the influence that they may hold.

For corporates, a higher risk customer type might be a small business that deals predominantly in cash transactions.

Of course the due diligence process goes beyond the simple data requirements introduced above; for retail customers banks will require employment status and name of employer, or details on occupation if the customer is self-  employed. Spending patterns are also tracked on banking systems in order to detect any potential suspicious activity, as well as fraudulent activity on the account that the customer is not aware of.

## Reporting Suspicious Activity

AML and KYC processes are typically embedded within the Compliance function in a bank. In most banks this is part of the 'second line of defence' (2LoD) function, headed by the chief risk officer (CRO). 3  The head of compliance is ultimately responsible for ensuring that all suspicious transactions are reported to the designated authority.

In most jurisdictions suspicious transactions are described in a kind of watch list; any transaction that was one of these types, for example, should be considered as potentially suspicious:

- ■ a customer who deposits a large amount of cash at a bank branch when the norm is for this customer to use electronic transfers;
- ■ regular deposits of cash followed by transfers to bank accounts overseas;
- ■ sudden increase in making foreign currency transfers;
- ■ a  hitherto  out-  of-  pattern  inflow  of  funds  from  abroad  to  a  customer bank account, which are then withdrawn;
- ■ a hitherto out-  of-  pattern early repayment of a loan, when the amount repaid appears beyond the stated earnings capacity of the customer;
- ■ an  overpayment  of  a  credit  card  balance  turning  the  account  into a  surplus  balance, followed by a request from the customer shortly after  to  repay  the  surplus  credit  balance  to  a  new  current  account recently set up.

Banks should also be aware of their employees becoming involved in money laundering, for motivations that can be guessed at, by assisting criminals  particularly  with  the  placement  and  layering  stages. The  traditional 'warning signs' for this are employees who appear reluctant to go on holiday, or exhibiting a lifestyle that is at odds with their salary level. 4

3  This organisation arrangement is possibly for convenience reasons as much as any other reason. The author's belief is that Compliance and Risk (2 nd  LoD or 2LOD) are distinct and separate disciplines, and that the Compliance function is more appropriately defined as being part of the first line of defence (1 st  LoD or 1LOD). The rationale is that compliance is a rules-  based process, with compliance officers enforcing adherence to published rules. The 2 nd  LoD, on the other hand, is defined as the 'independent oversight, review and challenge' function, and this is distinct from Compliance. In other words, the role of Risk is to provide independent oversight, review and challenge, and not therefore to 'own' any risk type. This includes, for the author, compliance risk.

4  In the UK there is a regulatory requirement that all bank employees take at least one holiday of 10 consecutive working days duration each calendar year, as a deterrent against or detection mechanism for employee fraud.

## ETHICS AND CUSTOMER SERVICE

If a man has no personal integrity, then he has nothing of value.' (Col. John Boyd, US Air Force, 1927-1997)

The author's firm belief is that 'doing the right thing' for a customer is something that is inherent to, and intimately tied with, a person's character. Without personal integrity at the individual level, anything written in a chapter on ethics is meaningless. But it may be that one should not take ethics and good conduct towards customers as something that is simply known by everyone, right from the start, and this being the real world often there are shades of grey involved; it isn't always 'black and white'. In which case there is some value in writing about this topic here. Ideally, everyone reading this section will conclude that all the author has done is state the obvious.

## Trust and the Meaning of Ethics

Consumers' income and wealth is, if not necessarily always hard earned, certainly a key factor contributing to their well-  being and quality of life. For any consumer to entrust a corporate entity with any aspect of its financial services needs requires just that - trust. A bank is able to operate only if it holds the trust of the community within which it operates. All customers and potential customers have to feel that they can trust the bank to not only look after their own funds but also that they can rely on the bank to deal with their best interests at heart. This goes straight to the point of what good banking is: ensuring a safe store of value for a customer's deposits, and transacting only in financial services products that are appropriate to the customer's needs. If a customer suspects that their bank cannot be trusted to not misinform them, or feels that any product marketing targets them unfairly, then they will not feel comfortable dealing with the bank and that will be to everyone's detriment.

This is at the heart of good banking: be trustworthy.

Put simply, ethical good conduct is a baseline requirement for successful provision of banking services. Banks, and all bank employees, should always act in an ethical manner towards all customers. And by so doing, customers will have comfort that their bank will always treat them fairly. If banks do not behave ethically, and trust is lost, then everyone, and society as a whole, will be the poorer for it.

The author defined 'ethics' at the start of this chapter. We recognise that there are categories of ethics, including one's personal ethics, but we are concerned with corporate ethics and professional ethics. Corporate ethics  refer  to  the  ethical  standards  of  a  corporate  entity;  they  address  how the  company  behaves  towards  its  stakeholders,  including  customers  and employees, and the behavioural standards it expects employees to adhere to. Not all companies, in any industry, believe that serving the customer's best interests should be at the heart of everything they do, and so may foster a corporate culture that advocates profit maximisation. They may also not care overmuch about employee contentment, and encourage an aggressive atmosphere in the belief that this delivers 'results'. That's their lookout. In a bank, the corporate culture has to place the customer at the heart of everything it does, because that is the basis of the trust that customers implicitly place in it.

Professional ethics are very relevant to banks in particular, although of course they apply equally in other professions. The author's own professional association, the Chartered Institute for Securities &amp; Investment, of which he is a Fellow, has always placed professional ethics high on the list of what it expects from its members when conducting their 'day job'. In most countries there is a professional association for employees in the banking sector, and more often than not each will have issued its own guidelines on ethical behaviour for members.

The case for good ethics in banking is easily made: banks are the cornerstones of any economy, and economic development needs an efficient banking sector. But a prerequisite for efficiency in the banking system is trust in the banks themselves. For this reason, banks must not only conduct their business in a fair and ethical manner, they must also be seen to be doing so. Any well-  ordered banking sector requires customers to have trust in it.

## The Concept of Good Ethics

At this point it is well to state that while skills can be taught (and experience gained), this is not necessarily true of values (unless one is teaching children). It is not straightforward to inculcate values, in an individual or a collection of individuals, via a training course. Values reflect individual personality and character, which are functions of many factors. But we should be able to answer the question, 'What exactly do you mean by good ethics?' We might wish to answer as follows.

In banking, good ethics should be comprised of:

- ■ integrity;
- ■ honesty;
- ■ fair play;
- ■ transparency.

These characteristics should be reflected in corporate as well as individual  behaviour.  Integrity  is  perhaps  the  hardest  to  tie  down  explicitly, although as the old saying goes, we know it when we see it. The Oxford English Dictionary defines integrity as:

the quality of being honest and having strong moral principles.

So integrity already incorporates the next ingredient, that of honesty. In essence, integrity in banking is to act in a way that is required and expected by the customer, and in line with acceptable standards of behaviour. It means avoiding conflicts of interest, and not lowering standards for any reason. And it means being honest, which one defines simply as telling the truth and not telling lies. Honesty and integrity will ensure trust.

Fair play is easy to define and easy to recognise. It means observing stated rules, not favouring one person over another, and treating everyone in the same way. In banking, fair play means not marketing an inappropriate product to a customer, as well as more obvious things like not setting an unreasonable interest rate on a loan and paying a reasonable rate of interest on a deposit. It also means demonstrating empathy when responding to customer complaints.

Transparency is also simple to define and to follow. Banks should speak clearly and accessibly, by which we mean customer communications should be clear and accessible, and employees should be clear and avoid jargon when speaking or emailing customers. Some banks communicate with customers 'digitally', for example via a smartphone 'app'. The same principle applies. There is an additional aspect of transparency: making one's own interests clear from the start. A bank and its employees should make sure all relevant information pertaining to a transaction is passed to the customer from the start, and disclose their own interests as well. This is particularly important  when  it  comes  to  the 'small  print':  all  terms  and  conditions relating to the product should always be explained clearly and in full so that the customer is in a good position to make a decision. This aspect of good service also connects back with the 'know your customer' requirement we discussed earlier. A bank will not be in a position to recommend any product or service to a customer unless and until it has obtained all relevant information about that customer's financial standing, their needs and their objectives. Only then can it offer or recommend products that are best suited to helping the customer achieve those objectives, and be sure that it  is  not  pushing unsuitable products that the customer does not actually need or benefit from. Finally, when describing a product, the bank should ensure that any variables in the performance of the product are explained in advance and in full. A good example is a loan being take out at a floating interest rate: in this case the relationship manager (RM) or customer service officer describing the product should note to the customer that the interest rate may rise, under what circumstances, and what this will mean for the interest payment cash flows.

## Ethics and Corporate Governance

In Chapter 17 we discuss bank corporate governance in some detail. Here we will consider the ethical considerations that should both feed into, and be managed via, the governance framework in a bank.

Well-  established principles in the field of industrial economics describe the 'principal-agent' issue in corporate governance, which boils down to the fact that for large institutions the owners of the firm are not the managers. That is, the shareholders entrust the employees to manage the firm on a day-  to-  day  basis  in  a  way  that  enables the shareholders to realise their desired  return  on  capital  target.  This  can  sometimes  create  a  conflict  of interest between principal and agent, and the governance structure of the firm is, in theory at least, set up to address and minimise conflict risk.

In a bank, the customer is as important a stakeholder as the owners and employees. The governance structure in a bank should recognise the needs of its customers and ensure that these needs are protected against any unethical or unfair practice. To assist with this, good practice dictates that the corporate governance framework in a bank allows for:

- ■ alignment of the bank management's interests with those of customers;
- ■ an incentive structure that enables these interests to be aligned.

The culture surrounding corporate governance will influence the culture throughout the rest of the bank. We spoke earlier of corporate ethics; corporate culture is the set of ethical values that the company exhibits, and which are reflected in the behaviour of the company's employees. The culture of, and in, a company is influenced by a number of factors, and is one that develops over time; for these reasons any culture can be changed only slowly and over time. Culture also comes from the top down. This is why it is vital that the company's senior executives, from the Board of Directors downwards, lead by example and display the right ethical behaviours.

A number of regulatory authorities around the world use the expression 'due skill, care and diligence' in their guidelines on what is required of bank directors and executives (the UK regulator also states that such individuals must be 'fit and proper' persons in order to hold these positions). Certainly that is an unarguable requirement: a bank is entrusted by its customers with something that is very, very important (their financial well-  being) and so it is surely a minimum requirement of all bank employees that they act with due skill, care and diligence. The author's personal preference would be that bank employees, at any level of seniority, simply do the right thing.

## ETHICS AND CONDUCT

A Google search suggests that we define 'conduct risk' as:

any action of a financial institution or individual that leads to customer detriment,  or  has  an  adverse  effect  on  market  stability  or effective competition.

It  goes  without  saying  that  to  minimise  conduct  risk  a  bank  should strive to avoid any detrimental impact on its customers. Consumer protection laws, which are in place in many jurisdictions, can be seen as a minimum requirement and standard for customer service, but a bank should set up its own policies in this space designed to ensure good conduct towards customers.

Bank products available  for  customers  range  from  the  simple  to  the complex (although this is relative: one person's simple may be another's complex). So banks should always strive to ensure that all customers understand fully the terms and conditions of any product they are transacting in or any service they are being offered. The bank itself should have in place policies and procedures to ensure that customers are not 'mis-  sold' products, and that customer service officers and RMs take all necessary steps to understand fully every customer's needs and objectives. Both retail and corporate customers will expect, and should receive, fair treatment, which includes:

- ■ provision of all necessary information enabling them to make the most appropriate decision;
- ■ the right and proper advice regarding the suitability of any product recommended to them;
- ■ integrity in all dealings by their bank;
- ■ prompt response and fair treatment of any complaint or claim.

Table 2.1 summarises the fair treatment and customer protection process that should be embedded in the entire product life cycle.

TABLE 2.1 Product life cycle and customer fair treatment

| Life-cycle stage          | Conduct risk mitigant                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                              |
|---------------------------|------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| Product design            | Transparent and fair contractual terms and conditions Fair and equitable fees and charges Simple and clear descriptive language Approval by relevant executive committee                                                                                                           |
| Marketing and advertising | Clear and understandable advertising Avoidance of any misleading language Adequately trained and skilled customer-facing staff Bank accepts responsibility for ensuring that the product is suitable and appropriate for the customer given the under- stood needs of the customer |
| Transaction origination   | Full and clear disclosure of productT&C to customer Speedy settlement                                                                                                                                                                                                              |
| After-sales service       | Speedy response to customer follow-up enquiries Fair notice given to customer of expiry or renewal of a trans- action                                                                                                                                                              |
| Complaints                | Clear and established process for customer complaints Speedy response to and settlement of customer complaints                                                                                                                                                                     |

An ingredient of good customer service and fair treatment is transparency of the product itself. For example, one regulatory authority has stated:

Adequate and effective product disclosure and transparency [will] facilitate  consumers  to  be  more  active  in  safeguarding  their  own interests  while  exerting  market  discipline  on  financial  service providers.

This is an unarguable statement, but in today's environment banks have to be proactive in ensuring their customers have all the information they need, and that it is completely accurate information, so as to have comfort that the customer is making the right decision. The firm commitment and engagement of senior executives to this principle is an essential prerequisite of good disclosure and transparent practice. From the Board downwards, senior  executives  and  managers  should  provide  the  necessary  leadership, direction and supervision to ensure that these principles are adhered to.

On a related note: we haven't used this expression before, but 'market discipline' is a somewhat academic concept that suggests that (Google search again):

market participants, such as depositors and shareholders, monitor the risks of banks, and take action to limit excessive risk-  taking. This requires banks that have taken excessive risk to fail and for bank investors and depositors to share the losses when they do.

In reality, past history has shown that it is quite possible for banks to enter  into 'excessive'  risk  taking  and  still  attract  depositors  and  market counterparties right up until the moment that they become unviable as going concerns. Hence this author prefers not to rely on 'market discipline' in any way. A bank should recognise its own importance in the wider economy and its connection to all stakeholders in the community and use that awareness to manage its own 'risk taking'. One cannot rely on 'market discipline' in anything other than an aggregate way when discussing the stability of the banking sector. A bank should manage its own risk based on its assessment of good practice.

## Responsible Banking

All banks should practise responsible banking.  This is intimately connected with the 'know your customer' principles we discussed earlier. In essence, responsible banking covers all products, but is an important part of the lending policy of a bank. Down to the individual retail and corporate customer level, a bank will wish to ensure that debt does not become excessive. Traditionally there was an assumption that customers would be expected to make responsible borrowing decisions based on their self-  interest; while this is not an unreasonable assumption in today's environment, it is not sufficient to ensure responsible banking. Therefore a bank should, as well as supplying full and accessible information on any loan product, also ensure that any lending is both suitable and affordable for the customer. Determining affordability requires that the bank address the customer income and cash flow state at time of origination but also assess their repayment ability should their circumstances change (for example, unemployment, recession, loss of market, and so on).

Responsible banking practice extends into any default situation for any customer. Irrespective of the regulatory guidance on this, in any jurisdiction, a bank should always seek to treat customers fairly should they be unable to continue payments on their loan or other financing arrangements. A policy on responsible banking would include guidelines covering:

- ■ clear processes and procedures on fair treatment of customers who are unable to meet contractual requirements on a loan, due to unemployment, illness, loss of revenue, or other reasonable cause;
- ■ contacting a customer as soon as possible if the bank detects any sign of  impending  difficulty  in  meeting  debt  interest  payments  or  loan repayments. This  communication  should  also  highlight  what  options are  available  to  a  customer  experiencing  payment  problems,  such  as alternative repayment methods or a restructuring of the loan (any alternative payment profile or restructuring should not leave the customer worse off. This is why an interest payment holiday is not necessarily the best option for a customer experiencing temporary cash flow problems, unless interest is not rolled or the loan maturity date is extended: it may not ultimately benefit the customer);
- ■ sufficient information and early warning of what recovery actions the bank  may  be  expected  to  follow  through  with,  should  the  customer remain in default.

Some  banks  'outsource'  debt  collection  and  recovery  processes  to third-  party agencies. Where this is the case, a bank should remember that it is its reputation that is at stake in any recovery undertaking. Any outsourced provider should follow the same communication and fair dealing policy and approach that the bank itself follows.

## Customer Complaints

A bank should have in place an approved policy and procedure to handle any form of complaint from a customer. The procedure should be easy to follow and it should be made known to customers from the beginning, so that should a customer feel a need to complain about a product or any aspect of the bank's service they know exactly what to do right away. Generally a customer might be expected to complain to their RM, who may be able to deal with it to the customer's satisfaction. Should that not be the case, there should be a dedicated team or department that handles customer complaints. This will ensure sufficient independence of the process and assist in empathy for the customer. The author recommends that every bank has such a dedicated team or department.

In most jurisdictions the regulatory authority will have specified rules on customer complaints; for example the timescale within which complaints must be responded to and must be resolved. That said, a bank should strive to meet a timeline that is inside the regulatory requirement. Ideally, every complaint should be resolved to the satisfaction of the customer. This is not always  possible;  indeed  some  complaints  may  be  spurious  and  the  bank may reject them. But as far as practicable the objective is to address the complaint in a way that resolves the dispute to the customer's satisfaction. Where this is  not  possible,  the  bank  should  inform  the  customer  of  any higher authority that exists in the market (usually a government agency) that can look into the issue.

More and more banks in today's environment engage with their customers 'digitally', via a smartphone mobile app, and their customers never actually communicate with a human being. The principles regarding customer complaints and responsible banking remain unchanged. The operating model in place at a bank does not impact the importance of observing good practice when it comes to these principles.

## ETHICS, CONDUCT AND REPUTATION RISK

In  Part  VI  we  discuss  the  bank's  risk  management  framework,  incorporating its 'risk register'. One of the risk types on the risk register is reputational risk. In the author's view, reputation risk is very effectively minimised,  and  indeed  made  negligible,  if  the  bank  simply  follows  the principles  described  in  this  chapter  and  'does  the  right  thing'  by  its customers and all its  stakeholders.  Practising  good  ethical  banking  and observing good customer conduct is the best way for a bank to preserve a sound reputation.

The author would define 'reputation risk' as follows:

The risk of damage to the bank's name arising from any association, action or inaction which is viewed by stakeholders (including customers, clients, colleagues. shareholders, regulators, and media) to be inappropriate or unethical.

An alternative definition might be:

The  risk  of  loss  (possibly  indirectly)  resulting  from  undertaking business which, in the public's view, is seen to be below the standards expected of the bank. That is, the risk that a bank's activities could, if viewed as unethical, lead to negative publicity for the firm, thereby generating direct negative impact on the balance sheet.

Thus, by definition reputation risk is increased by any compromise on ethics and ethical behaviour, or indeed any perceived compromise. So ensuring the right culture, embedding sound ethics, is the first and last key ingredient in managing a bank's reputation risk.

Damage to a bank's reputation or 'image' can feed through directly to the balance sheet. Loss of reputation reduces the attractiveness of the bank to stakeholders (both customers and shareholders), which results in lower revenues, regulatory or legislative action, loss of existing and potential client business, reduced workforce morale and difficulties in recruiting staff. Hence it needs to be taken as seriously as more traditional bank risks such as credit risk or liquidity risk.

The tie-  up between ethics and reputation risk manifests itself in different ways, for example:

- ■ Failure to act in good faith and in accordance with the bank's values and stated ethics and customer code of conduct;
- ■ Failure (real or perceived) to comply with the law or any regulations, or association (real or implied) with explicitly illegal activity;
- ■ Failure  to  comply  with  internal  standards  and  policies,  particularly those relating to conduct towards customers;
- ■ Any association with controversial business sectors or clients;
- ■ Association  with  controversial  transactions,  projects, countries  or governments.

In  each  case,  the  risk  may  arise  from  failure  to  comply  with  either stated or expected good practice. Acceptable behaviour and standards may change over time, hence reputation risk management is not a static process. If a bank's observation of good ethical practice is not managed effectively,  stakeholder  expectations  of  responsible  corporate  behaviour  will not be met.

Ethics in themselves may be difficult to pin down as written rules. Similarly,  reputation  risk  is  by  definition  difficult  to  quantify,  requiring  more subjective judgement than more traditional bank risks. A bank can set up a formal risk management framework process addressing this risk, but the more difficult  challenge  is  to  ensure  that  employees'  values,  from  Board Directors  downwards,  adopt  the  various  rules  and  processes  in  any  risk framework and operate by them.

A bank's  risk  management  framework  should  incorporate  a  code  of conduct that sets out what it should mean to work at the bank and the standards and behaviours expected of all employees. Any such code ideally should provide examples of how the bank's values should be put into practice in decision-  making and highlights the responsibility of individuals to challenge poor practice whenever and wherever they observe it.

## CHAPTER SUMMARY

Banking is considered a profession. Many bankers study for the   professional qualifications  of  their  national  bank  trade  association;  for  example,  the author is a Fellow of the Chartered Institute for Securities &amp; Investment, and a Fellow of  the  London  Institute  of  Banking  &amp;  Finance.  Sredojevic and Brkovic (2022) provide a comprehensive listing of associations around the world. It is common for these professional bodies to include a paper on ethics, and customer conduct, in their examinations. They also require their  members  to  undertake  'continuing  professional  development'  in order to maintain currency and to enable continued fellowship with their association.

This is all to the good. This chapter has, hopefully, painted a sufficiently detailed picture of the key principles of good customer service, good conduct and level of professional ethics required of a bank and all its employees. In summary we would describe these as follows:

- ■ Know your customer: not because of anti-  money laundering requirements (although these do require it), but because without this it is simply not possible to deliver good and appropriate customer service. When we say 'know', we are not referring to name, proof of address, passport ID and utility bill (nonetheless these are of course very important) but rather to the financial services requirements of the customer, what precisely  their  needs  and  objectives  are. Armed  with  this  knowledge  the bank is in a position to provide good service.
- ■ Professional ethics and customer service: this refers to the need for banks and their employees to exhibit integrity and honesty in all their dealings with all their customers.
- ■ Ethics and conduct risk: as part of delivering good service, a bank should strive  consistently  to  deliver  fair  outcomes  for  its  customers.  This  is allied to the 'know your customer' principle: ensure that only the most appropriate products are offered to customers and work proactively to ensure lending, hedging and indeed all financial services provision are undertaken responsibly.
- ■ Ethics  and  reputation  risk:  providing  financial  services  on  an  ethical basis will minimise, if not eliminate entirely, the risk of negative impact on a bank's reputation.

It can be said that the words 'ethical basis' and indeed 'good customer service' are describing value-  based judgements: one person's 'good' may be another person's 'unsatisfactory'; it depends to some extent on whose viewpoint one takes. But that illustrates exactly the point that was made at the beginning of this chapter. Ultimately these principles can be said to be all values-  based. And that is the message of this chapter: one's personal values will drive one's own interpretation of what is required to demonstrate adequate standards of professional ethics and good customer service. And that is something that cannot really be learned by reading a book.

The final word on this subject will be left to a gentleman called George Rae, who pursued a career in banking in England in the 19th century. Upon his retirement he authored a book on banking, and we furnish some quotes from that book in Chapter 24. His words are surely the first and the last needed to be said on this subject.

## REFERENCES AND FURTHER READING

- Choudhry, M. (2018), 'Machine Learning in Banking: How to Transform both Balance Sheet Management and Customer Services Provision', European Financial Review , April-May, pp. 32-38.
- Dill, A. (2019), Bank Regulation, Risk Management, and Compliance: Theory, Practice, and Key Problem Areas , London: Informa Law from Routledge.
- Parkman,  T.  (2019), Mastering  Anti-  Money  Laundering  and  Counter-  Terrorist Financing:  A  Compliance  Guide  for  Practitioners ,  London:  FT  Publishing International.
- Ramakrishna,  S.  (2015), Enterprise  Compliance  Risk  Management: An  Essential Toolkit for Banks and Financial Services , Chichester: John Wiley &amp; Sons.
- Sredojevic, S. and Brkovic, M. (2022), Banking Associations: Their Role and Impact in a Time of Market Change , Berlin: De Gruyter.

## Abstract

In the era before bank regulation, all banks would have held a portion of their balance sheet liabilities in the form of their own funds, the shareholders' equity. The capital amount required to be held by banks today is given by a country's own legal rulings, and in virtually every country in the world follows guidance published  by  a  special  committee  of  the  Bank  for  International  Settlements. These guidelines are known as the Basel rules, and since the last century banks have seen three versions of them, known as Basel I, Basel II and Basel III. Each successive iteration of Basel guidance has increased the level of complexity and intrusiveness in national rulings.

I n Chapter 1 we noted the importance of capital to the basic business of banking. The ratio of a bank's total capital to its overall balance sheet is one of the hallmarks of a firm's overall financial health, and as the capital buffer is required to cover all (expected and unexpected) losses suffered by the bank, it is the target of bank regulators' rulings. Before we had official supervision and bank regulation - which was indeed the norm before the 20th century - banks would have held a share of their balance sheet liabilities in the form of their own equity, that is, shareholders' funds. Since the latter part of the 20th century the amount of this equity, or capital, to be held is governed by national law.

In this chapter we discuss bank regulatory capital, the structure of capital standards and the implications that they have for bank financial management. This includes a review of the Bank for International Settlements (BIS) regulatory capital guidance, known as the Basel rules, and their evolution from 1988 up to the time of writing.

The second part of the chapter looks at regulatory capital calculation.

## BANK REGULATORY CAPITAL REQUIREMENTS

Banks and financial institutions are subject to a range of regulations and controls; the primary one is concerned with the level and quality of capital

1  This chapter was co-  written with Periklis Thivaios, Faculty BTRM.

## CHAPTER  3

## Bank Regulatory Capital and the 'Basel Rules' 1

that a bank holds, and that this capital is sufficient to provide a cushion underpinning the risk-  taking activities that the bank enters into.

A bank's capital is, put simply, the difference between assets and liabilities on its balance sheet, and is the property of the bank's owners. It may be used to meet any operating losses incurred by the bank, and if such losses exceeded the amount of available capital, then the bank would have difficulty in repaying liabilities, which may lead to bankruptcy.

However, for regulatory purposes capital is defined differently; again in its simplest form regulatory capital is comprised of those elements in a bank's balance sheet that are eligible for inclusion in the calculation of capital ratios. The ratio required by a regulator will be that level deemed sufficient to protect the bank's depositors. Regulatory capital includes equity, preference shares and subordinated debt, as well as general reserves. The common element of these items is that they are all loss-  absorbing, whether this  is  on  an  ongoing  basis  or  in  the  event  of  liquidation. This  is  crucial to regulators, who are concerned that depositors and senior creditors are repaid in full in the event of the bank's bankruptcy.

The most recognised and internationally applied bank regulatory capital rules are the 'guidelines' published by the Basel Committee on Banking Supervision (BCBS), a supranational committee of central banks that is part of the Bank for International Settlements (BIS).

The Basel Committee, as the BCBS is commonly referred to, took its name from the town in Switzerland where the BIS is based. The BIS is not a regulatory body in itself, and its pronouncements carry no legislative weight; however, national authorities are keen to demonstrate that they follow the Basel rules, at a minimum to maintain investor and public confidence. Typically, an institution is subject to the local interpretation of the Basel requirements, but may also be subject to cross-  border requirements such as the European Union's Capital Adequacy Directive or the USA's complex web of regulations (including the Dodd-Frank Act (USC, 2010)). 2

The primary focus of this chapter is to discuss and analyse the evolution and structure of the Basel regulatory frameworks. Given that there are almost as many local implementations of the Basel rules as there are countries, the discussion of local law implementations is not addressed. The discussion will start, naturally, from the Basel I rules (issued back in 1988), all the way to the 'final' Basel III revisions published in 2017. 3

2  Just about every country in the world follows Basel guidance, in whole or in part, when drafting its own rules governing the supervision of banks. The author is aware of only three countries (other than North Korea) that were not following Basel III guidance (the latest form of the rules) in 2022, these being Cuba, Ethiopia and Viet Nam. All other countries in the world had implemented, in part or in whole, a form of the Basel Committee's latest guidance.

It is important to note when reading through the various frameworks that, while Basel I was superseded by Basel II, Basel II and Basel III are complementary frameworks; in other words, Basel III (and its revisions) build upon the core foundations of Basel II.
