As such, Basel II and III should be studied in tandem. The latest consolidated Basel framework can be accessed on the BIS website, at https://www.bis.org/basel\_framework/

Table 3.1 offers a high level summary of the Basel requirements, from Basel I all the way to 'Basel IV'.

TABLE 3.1 Evolution of Basel standards.

|                              | Basel I                              | Basel II                                                    | Basel III                                                   | Basel III final form (Basel 'IV')                       |
|------------------------------|--------------------------------------|-------------------------------------------------------------|-------------------------------------------------------------|---------------------------------------------------------|
| Risk types in scope          | Credit risk Market risk (since 1996) | Credit risk Market risk Operational risk                    | Credit risk Market risk Operational risk Liquidity risk     | Credit risk Market risk Operational risk Liquidity risk |
| Credit risk approaches       | 'Standard- ised'                     | Standardised Retail IRB Foundation IRB Advanced IRB         | Standardised Retail IRB Foundation IRB                      | Restrictions on use of A-IRB                            |
| Market risk approaches       | Standardised IMA                     | Standardised IMA                                            | Advanced IRB Standardised IMA                               | Standardised (FRTB revised) IMA (FRTB revised)          |
| Operational risk approaches  | None                                 | Basic Indicator Standardised / Alternative standardised AMA | Basic Indicator Standardised / Alternative standardised AMA | SMA                                                     |
| Explicit liquidity standards | None                                 | None                                                        | LCR NSFR                                                    | LCR NSFR                                                |
|                              |                                      |                                                             |                                                             | (Continued)                                             |

TABLE 3.1 ( continued )

|                 | Basel I                                             | Basel II                                            | Basel III                          | Basel III final form (Basel 'IV')   |
|-----------------|-----------------------------------------------------|-----------------------------------------------------|------------------------------------|-------------------------------------|
| Leverage ratio  | None                                                | None                                                | Yes                                | Yes Supplementary G-SIB LR          |
| Capital tiers   | Tier 1 Tier 2 Tier 3 (for market risk capital only) | Tier 1 Tier 2 Tier 3 (for market risk capital only) | Tier 1 Tier 2                      | Tier 1 Tier 2                       |
| Capital buffers | None                                                | None                                                | Countercyclical Conservation G-SIB | Countercyclical Conservation G-SIB  |
| Capital floors  |                                                     | Basel I based                                       | Basel I based                      | Standardised approach based         |

## MACROPRUDENTIAL REGULATION

Before we delve into the details of the Basel frameworks, we should introduce the concept of macroprudential regulation and the impact that it has had in the evolution of regulatory capital standards. While all Basel frameworks can be largely considered as 'microprudential' in nature (in other words, focusing on limiting the distress of individual firms), Basel II and especially Basel III have elements of 'macroprudential' parameters, aiming to limit the distress of the financial system as a whole.

The term 'macroprudential regulation' was first used by the Cooke Committee (the forerunner to the Basel Committee) at a meeting in 1979 (Clement, 2010) and has been more extensively incorporated in the regulatory  discourse  since  the  early  2000s. The  2008  financial  crisis  was  the trigger  for  macroprudential  regulation  to  be  elevated  in  significance  and attention (Borio, 2014).

According to Borio 'the objective of a macroprudential approach is to limit the risk of episodes of financial distress with significant losses in terms of the real output for the economy as a whole' (2003, p. 2). In other words, it can be contrasted with microprudential regulation, whose main objective is to prevent the failure of an individual financial institution (Hanson et al., 2010).

Table 3.2 summarises in a stylistic way the main differences between macroprudential and microprudential regulation (adapted from Borio, 2003).

TABLE 3.2 A comparison of macroprudential and microprudential regulations.

|                                                         | Macroprudential                            | Microprudential                                                                              |
|---------------------------------------------------------|--------------------------------------------|----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| Proximate objective                                     | Limit financial system- wide distress      | Limit distress of individual institutions                                                    |
| Ultimate objective                                      | Avoid output (GDP) costs                   | Soundness of individual institutions; consumer (investor/depositor/ policyholder) protection |
| Model of risk                                           | (In part) endogenous                       | Exogenous                                                                                    |
| Correlations and common exposures across institu- tions | Important                                  | Not relevant (except for deposit wholesale fund- ing runs)                                   |
| Calibration of prudential controls                      | In terms of system-wide distress; top-down | In terms of risks of individual institutions; bottom-up                                      |

Source: Borio (2003).

Macroprudential policies and their associated objectives can be characterised across a number of closely related dimensions, namely time, size, cross-  sectoral and structural (Borio and Drehmann, 2009; BIS, 2010; ECB, 2016). These dimensions, as well as the means by which they are (or are not) reflected in the Basel frameworks, are summarised in Table 3.3.

## BASEL I

Basel  I  (or  Basle  I)  was  introduced  in  1988  by  the  Basel  Committee  on Banking Supervision (BCBS) with two fundamental objectives, namely to 'strengthen the soundness and stability of the international banking system' and to provide 'a high degree of consistency in its application to banks in different countries with a view to diminishing an existing source of competitive inequality among international banks' (BCBS, 1988, p. 1).

The focus of the original Basel framework was on credit risk only. The 1988 framework was subsequently enhanced by the Market Risk Amendment (BCBS, 1996, 2005a) in 1996, in order to cover market risks embedded in a bank's trading book.

Even though Basel I is now outdated and superseded by the Basel II and Basel III frameworks, it is worth spending some time to understand the structure of the Basel I framework, as the concept of risk weights has flown through all the subsequent revisions, at least in the standardised approaches.

TABLE 3.3 Macroprudential dimensions in Basel frameworks .

| Dimension      | Discussion                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                           | Basel framework dimensions                                         |
|----------------|------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|--------------------------------------------------------------------|
| Time           | Aims to prevent the excessive build-up of risk, resulting from external factors and market failures, with the ultimate goal of smoothing the financial cycle. The policy tools employed to address this dimension primarily focus on the offsetting behaviour of prudential cushions (Borio, 2004; Borio and Shim, 2007) as well as the extension of the risk management time horizon (Borio and Drehmann, 2009).                                    | ▪ Countercyclical capital buffers ▪ Capital conser- vation buffers |
| Size           | Focuses on the potential reduction of nega- tive externalities associated with institutions that are perceived as 'too big to fail' due to their balance sheet size and interconnected- ness (BCBS, 2011a, 2011b). Measures include increasing the loss absorbing capacity of systemically significant institutions as well as the assessment of the extent of going-concern loss absorbency pertaining to them (FSB, 2010; BCBS, 2013a).            | ▪ G-SIB capital buffers ▪ G-SIB leverage ratio surcharge           |
| Cross- section | Concerned with how aggregate risk is distributed in the financial system at any point in time and aims to limit contagion effects. The main tools employed to limit cross-sectional effects have been capital requirements and insurance schemes (Kashyap et al. , 2008; Borio, 2009).                                                                                                                                                               | Not directly appli- cable (bank specific capital frameworks)       |
| Structure      | Aims to encourage a system-wide perspective in financial regulation. From a macroprudential perspective, while individual institutions may appear to be safe, the financial system as a whole is not (Acharya, 2001) because of 'the possibility that actions that are optimal from the perspective of individual institutions may result in undesirable outcomes for the system as a whole, through adverse feedback effects' (Borio, 2009, p. 33). | Tenuous link                                                       |

## Basel I Minimum Capital Requirements

The Basel I rules set a minimum ratio of total capital K total to risk weighted assets of 8%, in a formula that has remained largely unchanged throughout all the subsequent Basel iterations. In notation form:

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

where:

- ■ K total is the total capital requirement across the risk types in scope;
- ■ c is the minimum capital ratio (set to 8% under the Basel I framework);
- ■ RWA i are the risk weighted assets per risk type i (namely, market and credit risk), defined in the following subsections.

K total represents what one would call 'capital demand', in other words the capital required to be held against unexpected losses, given the bank's risk exposures. Capital demand should be compared to 'capital supply', which is the actual capital available to cover potential losses. Capital supply under Basel I had been defined across the following categories:

- ■ Tier  1:  perpetual  capital,  capable  of  absorbing  losses  through  the non-  payment of a dividend. This is shareholders' equity and also noncumulative preference shares.
- ■ Upper Tier 2: this is also perpetual capital, subordinated in repayment to other creditors; this may include, for example, undated bonds such as building society PIBS, and other irredeemable subordinated debt.
- ■ Lower Tier 2: this is capital that is subordinated in repayment to other creditors, such as long-  dated subordinated bonds.
- ■ The  1996  market  risk  amendment  introduced  a  third  tier  of  capital ('Tier  3'),  consisting  of  eligible  short-  term  subordinated  debt,  and which only applied to meeting a proportion of the capital requirements for market risks.

In other words, available capital (capital supply) should at no point fall below 8% of the sum of a bank's risk weighted assets. In addition, the composition of these capital ratios is constrained as follows, subject to a number of additional restrictions:

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

Under the original Basel rules, assets are defined as belonging to a bank's banking book or its trading book. The banking book essentially comprises the traditional activities of deposit taking and lending, with assets booked at original cost and not revalued.

Trading book assets, which include derivatives and other market risk exposures, are marked-  to-  market on a daily basis, with a daily unrealised profit or loss recorded. Such assets are risk weighted under the 1996 Market Risk Amendment.

## The Basel I Credit Risk Framework

Credit risk refers to 'the potential that a borrower or counterparty will fail to meet its obligations in accordance with agreed terms' (BCBS, 2000, p. 1). In other words, it is the risk of customer default, the impact of which is to erode part of the bank's capital base.

According to the Basel I rules, assets are scaled ('weighted') in terms of their assumed risk. Practically, each asset is assigned a risk weighting, which is 0% for low risk assets such as certain country government bonds, to 20% for inter-  bank lending, and up to 100% for the highest risk assets such as corporate loans. The Basel I risk weights are summarised in Table 3.4.

Practically, according to the Basel I rules, a loan in the inter-  bank market would be assigned a 20% weighting, while a loan of exactly the same size to a corporate would receive the highest weighting of 100%. So if a bank lent $1 million to another bank, in essence it would need to have at least:

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

of its own funds (its capital) on the balance sheet supporting this loan.

TABLE 3.4 Basel I credit risk standardised risk weights.

| Risk weight   | Example                                                                                                                                  |
|---------------|------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| 0%            | ▪ Cash ▪ Claims on central governments                                                                                                   |
| 10%           | ▪ Claims on domestic public sector entities (national discretion)                                                                        |
| 20%           | ▪ Claims on or guaranteed by banks incorporated in the OECD ▪ Cash items in the process of collection                                    |
| 50%           | ▪ Claims fully secured by mortgage on residential property                                                                               |
| 100%          | ▪ Claims on the private sector (ex. residential mortgages) ▪ Fixed assets ▪ Capital instruments issued by other banks ▪ All other claims |

## The Basel I Market Risk Amendment

The original 1988 Basel framework focused only on credit risk. However, the  Basel  Committee  soon  realised  that  internationally  active  banks  also held significant exposures in their trading books, 4  which were not captured and capitalised by the credit related accord. Therefore, in 1996, the BCBS introduced  an  amendment  incorporating  market  risks  (BCBS,  1996);  the 1996 amendment was subsequently refined in 2005 (BCBS, 2005a).

According to the BCBS, market risk is defined as 'the risk of losses in on- and off-balance sheet positions arising from movements in market prices' (BCBS, 2005a, p. 1). Market risk related capital charges are calculated  for  interest-  rate  related  instruments,  equities,  foreign  exchange  and commodities.

Despite the simplicity of the Basel I credit risk framework, the calculation  of  capital  requirements  for  trading  book  assets  is  more  convoluted, reflecting their greater complexity. Unlike the banking book where assets are primarily held at book value, and until expiry, the market risk measurement is based on mark-  to-  market valuations, using two prescribed methods.

The  Market  Risk Amendment  specified  two  different  approaches  to the estimation of market risk related capital requirements. The first method is the standardised approach , addressing a bank's general market risk and specific market risk exposures. General market risk captures a bank's exposure to the overall movements of the market; specific market risk captures exposures to specific issuers of debt securities or equities. 5  The standardised approach capital charge is the arithmetic sum of the risk measures obtained from interest rate, equity, foreign exchange, commodity risk and price risk in options of all kinds.

An alternative method offered was the internal models approach (IMA), whereby  banks  may  employ  internally  developed  'value-at-risk'  (VaR) models, subject to the explicit approval of their supervisory authorities. The scope of the IMA is similar to that of the standardised approach.

4  According to the market risk amendment, the trading book includes positions in

financial instruments (including positions in derivative products and off-  balance sheet  instruments)  which  are  intentionally  held  for  short-  term  resale  and/or which are taken on with the intention of benefiting in the short term from actual and/or expected differences between their buying and selling prices, or from other price or interest-  rate variations, and positions in financial instruments arising from matched principal brokering and market making, or positions taken in order to hedge risk on the trading book or banking book.

5  The scope of market risks has been significantly expanded and changed in subsequent market risk framework iterations.

## BASEL II

As a result of its simple, baseline approach (which to the author is actually one of its strengths!), the perceived shortcomings of the 1988 Basel capital accord  attracted  much  comment  from  academics  and  practitioners  alike, almost  as  soon  as  they  were  adopted. The  main  criticisms  included  that the requirements made no allowance for the credit risk ratings of different corporate borrowers, and that they were too rigid in their application of the risk weightings.

Shortly after the introduction of the 1988 credit risk framework, the BCBS commenced a lengthy revision process, with an aim to provide greater risk sensitivity and the incentive for banking institutions to better align the measurement of regulatory capital with their internal approaches. Following a number of consultative documents (BCBS, 2001, 2003), the revised Basel II framework was published in 2004 (BCBS, 2004). In 2006, the BCBS published a comprehensive version of the Basel II framework, incorporating the elements of the 1988 accord that were not revised, the 1996 Market Risk Amendment and the 2005 paper on the application of Basel II to trading activities and the treatment of double default effects (BCBS, 2005b). The development timelines for Basel II (and Basel I) are outlined in Figure 3.1.

The fundamental objective of Basel II was 'to develop a framework that would further strengthen the soundness and stability of the international banking system while maintaining sufficient consistency that capital adequacy regulation will not be a significant source of competitive inequality among internationally active banks' (BCBS, 2006a, p. 2).

Basel II is split into three 'pillars', summarised in Figure 3.2.

FIGURE 3.1 Basel I and Basel II timelines.

<!-- image -->

Pillar 1

Pillar 2

Pillar 3

## MEASURE - Minimum Capital Requirements

- Calculation of required capital for Credit, Market &amp; Operational Risk
- Permits selection of calculation method (subject to regulatory approval)

## MANAGE - Self Assessment &amp; Supervisory Review

- Establishment of Capital &amp; Liquidity adequacy assessment process (ICAAP &amp; ILAAP)
- A comprehensive assessment of risks (i.e. beyond Pillar 1) as well as a consistent supervisory assessment of banks' capital adequacy

## REPORT - Market Discipline

- Required risk disclosures in addition to existing statutory disclosure requirements
- Increased disclosure of risk-relevant information and thus fosters market discipline

FIGURE 3.2 Basel II pillars.

## Pill a r   1 :   T h e   M i n i m u m   C a p i t a l   R e q u i r e m e n t

The first pillar of the Basel II framework addresses the calculation of the total minimum capital requirements for credit, market and operational risk. The total Basel II minimum capital requirement K is  based  on  total  risk weighted assets (RWA) per risk type in scope and the minimum capital ratio.

Total risk weighted assets are calculated as follows:

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

where:

- ■ K market is the capital requirement for market risks;
- ■ K operational is the capital requirement for operational risks;
- ■ K credit is the capital requirement for credit risks;
- ■ 12.5 is the reciprocal of the minimum capital ratio c (defined as 8%; 12.5 = 1/0.08).

The total Pillar 1 capital requirement ('capital demand') for the Basel II framework is therefore calculated as:

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

Evaluation and review by Regulator

Assessed by Markets where:

- ■ K total is the total capital requirement across the risk types in scope;
- ■ c is the minimum capital ratio (8-10% in most countries);
- ■ RWA i are the risk weighted assets per risk type i (namely, market, credit and operational risk).

The definition of eligible capital ('capital supply') was refined slightly. Under Basel II, the composition is defined as:

- ■ Tier 1: paid up share capital / common stock and disclosed reserves.
- ■ Tier 2: undisclosed reserves, asset valuation reserves, general provisions /  general loan loss reserves, hybrid (debt / equity) capital instruments and subordinated debt.
- ■ Tier 3: short-  term subordinated debt, for the sole purpose of meeting a  proportion  of  the  capital  requirements  for  market  risks,  subject  to national authority discretion.

Under Basel II the total capital ratio must be no lower than 8% and Tier 2 capital is limited to 100% of Tier 1 capital. Subordinated term debt is limited to a maximum of 50% of Tier 1 elements, while Tier 3 capital is limited to 250% of a bank's Tier 1 capital that is required to support market risks.

Basel II introduced additional approaches for the calculation of regulatory capital requirements for credit risk, as well as explicit quantification of operational risk capital; the approaches available for market risk capital were left largely unchanged. Figure 3.3 indicates the various standardised and internal model approaches available for the calculation of the respective risk types, including the approaches that were subsequently removed by the 2017 Basel III revisions.

The  following  subsections  outline  the  approaches  available  for  the quantification of the respective risk types within the scope of Basel II.

## Credit Risk

Basel  II  credit  risk  capital  requirements  can  be  calculated  under  two approaches, the standardised approach and the internal ratings based (IRB) approach. Within the IRB there are three sub-  approaches, namely the Retail IRB  (R-  IRB)  approach,  the  Foundation  IRB  (F-  IRB)  approach  and  the Advanced IRB (A-  IRB) approach.

The  Basel II Standardised  Approach to Credit Risk The  Basel II standardised approach constitutes a refinement of the original Basel I credit risk  methodology  and  represents  the  most  straightforward  approach  for estimating credit risk capital requirements. Risk weighted assets under the standardised approach are assigned according to asset class and respective external credit ratings as follows:

FIGURE 3.3 Basel II standardised and internal models approach.

<!-- image -->

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

where:

- ■ EAD i,j is the exposure at default for asset class i and external rating j , accounting for undrawn limits and off-balance sheet items;
- ■ RWi,j is  the  respective  risk  weight  for  each  asset  class i and  external credit rating j .

Given that the Basel II risk weights have been superseded by the Basel III  refinements,  they  have  not  been  included  in  this  text.  For  any  practitioners  based  in  jurisdictions  that  have  not  moved  to  Basel  III,  the  risk weights applicable to each asset class and external rating (including also unrated exposures) can be found in section 2.II.A of the Basel II framework (BCBS, 2006a).

The Internal  Ratings  Based  (IRB) Approaches One of the  fundamental departures of Basel II from its predecessor was the introduction of internal ratings  based  (IRB)  approaches  to  the  calculation  of  credit  risk  capital requirements.

Under the IRB approaches, the BCBS allows banks to calculate capital requirements based on (primarily) the Probability of Default (PD), Loss Given Default (LGD) and Exposure at Default (EAD). All IRB approaches allow for the use of internal data estimates for the PD. However, only the Retail and Advanced IRB approaches allow for internal estimates of LGD and EAD, with the Foundation IRB approach using regulatory estimates for LGD and the Credit Conversion Factor (CCF) which converts current exposure to EAD. The Foundation IRB approach may be used as a stepping stone before the implementation of the Advanced IRB methodology, or be retained as a calculation method in its own right.

It is worth noting, albeit not relevant beyond an academic perspective, that the Basel II capital function has been modelled on the so-  called Asymptotic Single Risk Factor (ASRF) which, by definition, implies portfolio invariance (Gordy, 2003). Practically, within the Basel Committee's ASRF model, 'all systematic (or system-  wide) risks, that affect all borrowers to a certain degree, like industry or regional risks, are modelled with only one (the 'single') systematic factor' (BCBS, 2005c, p. 5). The implications of the ASRF approach are discussed later in this chapter.

To qualify for the use of the IRB approaches, banks must satisfy several operational prerequisites and have them approved by their relevant supervisory authority, including an appropriate length of internal data history.

Risk weighted assets for the IRB approaches are calculated as follows:

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

where:

- ■ EAD is  the  Exposure  at  Default,  based  on  internal  estimates  for  the Retail and Advanced IRB approaches, and based on supervisory estimates for Foundation IRB;
- ■ K credit is  the  capital  requirement  factor  for  retail  and  wholesale  exposures, calculated for each IRB approach as per the formulas below.
- ■ Note that a scaling factor is applied to the RWA amounts for credit risk assessed under the IRB approach, which was set at 1.06. 6

The IRB approaches differentiate between the Retail IRB for retail exposures and the Foundation and Advanced IRB for exposures to corporates, banks and sovereigns. The respective formulas are briefly summarised next.

6  The scaling factor was removed by the 2017 Basel III revisions (BCBS, 2017a).

- ■ G is  the  inverse  cumulative  distribution  function  for  a  standard  random variable;
- ■ PD is the probability of default estimated based on internal data;
- ■ R is the correlation factor which is:

- ■ 0.04 for qualifying revolving retail exposures.

where:

- ■ LGD is the loss given default estimated based on regulatory factors for the Foundation IRB and on internal data for the Advanced IRB;
- ■ N is  the  cumulative  distribution  function  for  a  standard  normal random variable;
- ■ G is  the  inverse  cumulative  distribution  function  for  a  standard  random variable;

- ■ R is the correlation factor which is calculated as follows.

Before continuing our discussion, it is interesting to study the behaviour of the capital requirement K based on the F-  IRB approach (as an illustration).  Assuming  an  LGD  of  45%  and  an  EAD  of  100,  Figure  3.4  maps expected losses (EL), unexpected losses (UL) and the sum of the two against a linearly increasing PD scale.

One can observe in Figure 3.4 that EL is, naturally, upward sloping. This  is  easy  to  comprehend  as  expected  losses  are  linearly  linked  to  the respective credit risk parameters (EL = PD * LGD * EAD).

However, one can also observe that UL is sloping upwards up until about a PD of 30%; afterwards it starts declining and it drops to 0% when PD tends to 100%. The explanation for the behaviour of the UL is thus: as we have already discussed, the Basel II capital function has been modelled on a single systematic factor (the ASRF), which assumes that only systematic  risk  affects  borrowers  (and  therefore  not  idiosyncratic  parameters). The larger the default probability of the counterparty, the more its unexpected losses are driven by idiosyncratic factors; therefore the systematicfactor  driven  UL  drops.  When  the  counterparty  has  defaulted  (PD ≈ 100%), the impact of systematic factors on UL is understandably zero.

The  rationale  behind  the  previously  noted  SME  adjustment  to  the correlation R (see Equation 3.12) is largely similar: the larger a firm, the greater  its  dependency  upon  the  overall  state  of  the  economy;  therefore, asset correlations increase with firm size. Smaller firms, on the other hand, are more likely to default for idiosyncratic reasons and hence the supposed need for an adjustment to their RWA calibration.

FIGURE 3.4 Expected and unexpected losses on PD.

<!-- image -->

## Operational Risk

A major departure from the structure of the Basel I framework was the introduction of an explicit 'operational risk' capital requirement.

Operational risk is defined as 'the risk of loss resulting from inadequate or failed internal processes, people and systems or from external events' (BCBS, 2006a, p.  144). The  Basel  definition  of  operational  risk  includes legal risk, which includes but is not limited to exposure to fines, penalties or punitive damages resulting from supervisory actions as well as private settlements, but excludes strategic and reputational risk.

Basel II offers three (effectively, four) different approaches for calculating the operational risk capital charge:

- ■ The Basic Indicator Approach (BIC). Banks using the basic indicator approach must hold capital for operational risk equal to the average over the previous 3 years of a fixed percentage (15%, denoted as alpha ) of positive annual gross income.

- ■ The Standardised Approach. Under the standardised approach, banks' activities are divided into eight business lines. 7  The capital charge for each business line is calculated by multiplying the respective business line's gross income by a factor (denoted as beta ) assigned to that business line. 8
- ■ The Alternative Standardised Approach (ASA). Under the ASA, the operational risk capital charge/methodology is the same as for the standardised approach, except for retail banking and commercial banking. For these business lines, loans and advances - multiplied by a fixed factor m -replaces gross income as the exposure indicator. The betas are unchanged. Banks can only use the ASA subject to national supervisory discretion.
- ■ The Advanced Measurement Approach (AMA). Under the AMA, the regulatory capital requirement will equal the risk measure generated by the bank's internal operational risk measurement system, subject to the quantitative and qualitative criteria outlined by the BCBS. Under Basel II, the spectrum of AMA models was arguably large, and this has been one of the reasons for the subsequent abolition of the AMA as part of the Basel III revisions.

## Market Risk

The 1996 Market Risk Capital Standards were left largely unchanged by the Basel II framework, whose main focus was on the refinement of the capital requirements for credit risk and the introduction of an operational risk capital charge. In 2005, the Basel Committee issued a number of Amendments to the Capital Accord to incorporate market risks (BCBS, 2005a), which once again did not materially affect the structure and approaches (standardised and IMA) available for the calculation of market risk capital.

The 2008 financial crisis initiated an extensive overhaul of the Basel market risk framework. The Market Risk Amendments were supplemented in 2009 by an incremental risk capital charge, which includes default risk as well as migration risk for unsecuritised credit products (BCBS, 2009a, 2009b).

7  The business lines identified by Basel II are corporate finance, trading &amp; sales, retail banking, commercial banking, payment &amp; settlement, agency services, asset management, and retail brokerage.

8  According to the Basel Committee's calibrations, beta serves as a proxy for the industry-  wide  relationship  between  the  operational  risk  loss  experience  for  a given business line and the aggregate level of gross income for that business line.

In addition, the requirement for a stressed value at risk (VaR) calculation  was  introduced,  intending  to  incorporate  periods  relating  to  significant losses as well as to reduce the procyclicality of the minimum capital requirement.

Lastly, most securitisation approaches were removed from internal models and are to be treated as if held in the banking book, in order to reduce regulatory capital arbitrage between the trading and the banking book.

These revisions were termed the 'Basel 2.5' reforms and represented an interim step, before the Fundamental Review of the Trading Book (FRTB) consultative process commenced in 2012. The FRTB process and final structure are discussed later in this chapter.

## Counterparty Credit Risk (CCR)

Counterparty Credit Risk has been part of the Basel framework since 1995 (BCBS, 1995). Counterparty Credit Risk refers to 'the bilateral credit risk of transactions with uncertain exposures that can vary over time with the movement of underlying market factors' (BCBS, 2005b, p. 3).

Even though CCR is fundamentally a credit risk, there are two main characteristics that differentiate it from 'normal' lending risk:

- ■ Uncertainty of exposure: the value profile of the exposure over the lifetime of the transaction is unknown (e.g., over-  the-  counter (OTC) derivative  values  may  go  up  or  down  depending  on  the  movement  of  the underlying risk factor).
- ■ Bilateral  nature  of  risk:  the  credit  exposure  for  a  product  subject  to counterparty credit risk can alternate between the bank and the counterparty to the transaction.

In a review of the CCR framework executed in 2005 (BCBS, 2005b), which was subsequently incorporated in the comprehensive version of the Basel II framework (BCBS, 2006a), the Basel Committee updated the treatment of OTC derivative transactions and extended the range of methods available for the quantification of this risk type. In addition to the existing Current  Exposure  Method  (CEM),  a  Standardised  Method  (SM)  and  an Internal Models Method (IMM) were also introduced.

The SM and the IMM approaches aimed to address some of the criticisms levied against the CEM framework, which calculates potential future exposure in a rather risk-  insensitive manner, based on the replacement cost of the transaction (mark to market) plus a regulatory defined add-  on. The CEM and SM have been replaced by the Basel III SA-  CCR approach; Basel III

also incorporated explicit capital charges for Credit Valuation Adjustments (CVAs), which were further refined in 2017.

## Pill a r   2 :   S u p e r v i s o r y   R e v i e w   P r o c e s s

The first pillar of the Basel II framework was complemented by a second pillar,  focusing  on  the  supervisory  review  process. The  main  objective  of Pillar  2  is  to  identify  risk  factors  not  captured  in  Pillar  1,  therefore  giving regulators discretion to adjust the minimum regulatory capital requirement. Pillar 2 requires banks to think about the whole spectrum of risks they might face (for example, interest-rate risk, systemic risk, pension risk, concentration risk, strategic risk, reputation risk, liquidity risk, legal risk), in addition to the explicitly quantified credit, market and operational risk capital charges.

The Pillar  2  supervisory  review  process  is  based  on  four  key  principles, 9  namely:

- ■ Principle 1: Banks should have a process that assesses their overall capital adequacy in relation to their risk characteristics, as well as a strategy for maintaining their capital levels.
- ■ Principle  2: Supervisors  should  review  a  bank's  internal  capital  adequacy assessments and follow up as needed. This was the inception of the annual internal capital adequacy assessment process (ICAAP), which is  an onerous ongoing capital planning, stress testing, assessment and demonstration of adequacy exercise.
- ■ Principle 3: Supervisors should specify their expectation for banks to operate above the minimum regulatory capital ratios.
- ■ Principle 4: Supervisors should intervene at an early stage to prevent capital from falling below the level required to support a bank's risk profile.

One needs to note that Pillar 2 requirements are highly dependent on the guidelines provided by local regulators. For example, the UK's Prudential Regulation Authority (PRA) provides stricter guidance on the quantification of  risks  under  Pillar  2  than  certain  other  national  regulatory  authorities (PRA, 2021).

9  The listed principles have been updated to reflect Basel Committee guidance post the Basel II framework.

## Pill a r   3 :   D i s c l o s u r e s

The main objective of Pillar 3 is to encourage market discipline by developing a set of quantitative and qualitative public disclosure requirements which will allow market participants to assess key pieces of information pertaining to the bank's capital position.

The Pillar 3 disclosures generally address:

- ■ Capital: the elements that make up the bank's capital, such as the types of instruments that make up Tier 1 and Tier 2 capital.
- ■ Capital adequacy: covers the amount of capital required against credit, market and operational risk, as well as capital requirements as a percentage of the total capital of the bank.
- ■ Risk  exposure: the  overall  risk  exposure  of  a  bank,  as  measured  by credit risk, market risk, operational risk, liquidity risk and so on. Hence, this would include a profile of the ALM book, including the maturity profile of the loan book, interest-  rate risk, other market risk, counterparty credit risk and, essentially, the sum of the exposures measured and monitored by a bank's risk management processes.

The Basel II Pillar 3 requirements have been updated a number of times (BCBS, 2015a, 2017b, 2018a, 2019a) and are currently part of the consolidated Basel framework. The Appendix to this chapter includes a table with all the Pillar 3 disclosures as at time of writing.

## BASEL III

The  2009 'Basel  2.5'  revisions  (BCBS,  2009a,  2009b)  were  shortly  followed by Basel III, consisting of the global regulatory framework for more resilient  banks  and  banking  systems  (BCBS,  2010a)  -  updated  again  in 2011 (BCBS, 2011c) - and the international framework for liquidity risk measurement,  standards  and  monitoring  (BCBS,  2010b).  The  Basel  III liquidity framework has been finalised in two documents, one addressing the  Liquidity  Coverage  Ratio  (LCR)  and  liquidity  risk  monitoring  tools (BCBS,  2013b)  and  the  second  one  addressing  the  Net  Stable  Funding Ratio (NSFR) (BCBS, 2014a, 2018b). Lastly, in December 2017, the Basel Committee  published  the  finalisation  of  the  post-crisis  Basel  III  reforms (BCBS, 2017a, 2017c), known as Basel III Final Form, including the results of the cumulative quantitative impact study (BCBS, 2017d) and the transitional arrangements. The Basel III development timelines are summarised in Figure 3.5.

FIGURE 3.5 Basel III timeline.

<!-- image -->

As previously discussed, Basel III is an expansion - rather than a replacement - of the Basel II rules. Therefore, the fundamental objectives, scope and structure of the framework have remained unchanged and scholars may refer to the consolidated Basel framework on the BIS website (https://www .bis.org/basel\_framework/).

The following subsections outline the most important additions introduced by the initial Basel III document, which were subsequently further enhanced in 2017.

## Capital Composition and Capital Buffers

Raising the quality, consistency and transparency of the capital base was one of the main objectives of Basel III. Therefore, the updated framework refined the definition of capital to Tier 1 (going concern capital) and Tier 2 (gone concern capital), and eliminated the category of Tier 3 capital instruments, which were previously accepted for covering market risks.

Tier 1 capital is made up of Common Equity Tier 1 (CET1) capital and Additional Tier 1. CET1 consists of the sum of the following elements:

- ■ Common shares;
- ■ Stock surplus (share premium) resulting from the issue of instruments included in CET1;
- ■ Retained earnings;
- ■ Accumulated other comprehensive income and other disclosed reserves;
- ■ Minority interests, subject to a number of criteria;
- ■ Regulatory adjustments applied in the calculation of CET1.

Additional Tier 1 capital consists of:

- ■ Instruments  issued  by  the  bank  that  meet  the  criteria  for  inclusion  in  Additional  Tier  1  capital  (and  are  not  included  in  Common Equity Tier 1);

- ■ Stock surplus (share premium) resulting from the issue of instruments included in Additional Tier 1 capital;
- ■ Instruments issued by consolidated subsidiaries of the bank and held by third parties that meet the criteria for inclusion in Additional Tier 1 capital and are not included in Common Equity Tier 1;
- ■ Regulatory  adjustments  applied  in  the  calculation  of Additional Tier 1 Capital.

Tier 2 capital consists of the sum of the following elements:

- ■ Instruments issued by the bank that meet the criteria for inclusion in Tier 2 capital (and are not included in Tier 1 capital), as per paragraph 58 of (BCBS, 2011c);
- ■ Stock surplus (share premium) resulting from the issue of instruments included in Tier 2 capital;
- ■ Instruments issued by consolidated subsidiaries of the bank and held by third parties that meet the criteria for inclusion in Tier 2 capital and are not included in Tier 1 capital;
- ■ Certain loan loss provisions;
- ■ Regulatory adjustments applied in the calculation of Tier 2 Capital.

A  really  good  book  that  describes  the  different  forms  of  capital  eligible under Basel III guidelines is Handbook of Basel III Capital by Juan Ramirez (2017).

In addition to redefining Tier 1 and Tier 2 capital, Basel III introduced 'macroprudential' capital buffers that are designed to be built up in benign cycles and can be drawn down in times of stress to limit procyclicality. 10  (In practice, the CCyB buffer - see explanation that follows - is just set by the regulatory authority and banks given a period of time, typically 12 months, to have that buffer amount in place.) The three buffers introduced are:

- ■ Capital  Conservation  Buffer  (CCB): designed  to  'ensure  that  banks build up capital buffers outside periods of stress which can be drawn down as losses are incurred' (BCBS, 2011c, p. 54). This buffer has been set  at  2.5%  and  sits  on  top  of Tier  1  capital,  composed  of  common equity, after the application of deductions such as deferred taxes. Practically,  a  bank  whose  capital  ratio  fails  to  stay  above  the  buffer  will be restricted from making payouts such as dividends, share buybacks and bonuses.

10 Procyclicality  refers  to  the  extent  that  capital  requirements  fluctuate  with  the business cycle, thus generating financial system instability and amplifying swings in the real economy. The procyclical behaviour of capital requirements had been one of the criticisms of Basel II (cf. Borio et al., 2001; Gordy and Howells, 2006).

- ■ Countercyclical  Buffer  (CCyB): aims  to 'take  account  of  the  macrofinancial environment in which banks operate' (BCBS, 2011c, p. 57). This buffer may be set at 0-2.5% of common equity or other full lossabsorbing capital. The aim of the buffer is to force banks to start building up additional capital cushions when supervisors see excessive credit in the system that threatens to spark loan losses later on. Banks would then tap the buffer to offset such losses without having to raise fresh capital  immediately.  During  the  Covid-  19  pandemic,  countercyclical buffer requirements were often reduced to 0%, demonstrating the ability of such measures to weather - to some extent - the business cycle.
- ■ G-  SIB Buffer (GCIBC): aims to increase the total loss-  absorbing capacity for banks that are designated as global systemically important (G-  SIBs).

The composition of capital is subject to a number of restrictions which have  been  rolled  in  over  time.  These  capital  minima  are  illustrated  in Figure 3.6.

Practically, the proportion of Core Equity Tier 1 capital (the best quality capital available) has been upped from 2% to 4.5%. In addition, total Tier 1 capital has been increased from 4% to 6%, with Tier 2 capital now only constituting 2% of the total capital composition. These changes, alongside  the  addition  of  the  countercyclical  and  capital  conservation  buffers, resulted in a material increase of the total loss absorbing capacity of financial institutions.

FIGURE 3.6 Regulatory capital minimums.

<!-- image -->

FIGURE 3.7 UK PRA capital buffers stack. © Dean Carter. Used with permission.

<!-- image -->

In  Figure  3.7,  we  show  this  combined  capital 'stack'  framework  as applied by the PRA for UK banks when there was a non-  zero CCyB buffer in place. 11

The introduction of capital buffers resulted in the following update to the total capital requirement formula:

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

## where:

- ■ K total is the total capital requirement across the risk types in scope;
- ■ c is the minimum capital ratio (8-10% in most countries);
- ■ RWA i are the risk weighted assets per risk type i ;
- ■ b CB is the capital conservation buffer, calculated based on total RWA;
- ■ b CCyB is the countercyclical buffer, calculated based on total RWA;
- ■ b GSIB is  the  buffer  for  global  systemically  important banks, calculated based on total RWA.

11    With thanks to Andrew Payton for sending me this illustration!

In addition to increasing the quantity and quality of capital, the BCBS issued  a  consultative  document  that  outlined  its  proposals  for  designing capital  floors  based  on  standardised,  non-  internal  modelled  approaches (BCBS, 2014b). The proposals have been incorporated in the consultative paper on the revisions of the standardised approach for credit risk, issued in December 2015 (BCBS, 2015b), and the updated minimum capital requirements for market risk, issued in January 2016 (BCBS, 2016a) and revised on a consultative basis in March 2018 (BCBS, 2018c).

Whilst the previous minimum capital requirements apply to all internationally active financial institutions, as per the scope of the Basel frameworks, Basel III has introduced higher loss absorbency requirements for global systemically important banks (G-  SIBs) (BCBS, 2011a, 2013a, 2018d). These requirements are expected to be further enhanced based on the Financial Stability Board's finalised proposals for the minimum Total Loss Absorbing Capacity (TLAC) of G-  SIBs (FSB, 2015). 12  Minimum TLAC is an additional requirement to minimum regulatory capital requirements as set out in Basel III and will be set in relation to the consolidated balance sheet of each resolution group.

## Li q u i d i t y   S t a n d a r d s

One of the most important Basel III enhancements was the introduction of liquidity. 13  The objective of the two explicit liquidity standards has been to mitigate excessive liquidity risk and maturity  transformation,  endogenous  risks  that  had  built  up  before  the

two minimum standards for funding 2008 financial crisis.

The  two  complementary  standards  introduced  by  Basel  III  are  the Liquidity Coverage Ratio (LCR) and the Net Stable Funding Ratio (NSFR). It is important to note that both standards apply in addition to adhering to the principles for sound liquidity risk management and supervision issued by the BCBS in 2008 (BCBS, 2008). 14

12    The FSB is an international body that monitors and makes recommendations

about the global financial system. It is hosted by the Bank of International Settlements (BIS) in Basel, Switzerland.

13    The liquidity standards introduced by Basel III focus on funding liquidity risk, which should not be confused with market liquidity risk. Funding liquidity risk is 'the risk that the firm will not be able to efficiently meet both expected and unexpected current and future cash flow and collateral needs without affecting either daily operations or the financial condition of the firm' (BCBS, 2006b, p. 1). Market liquidity risk is 'the risk that a firm cannot easily offset or eliminate a position without significantly affecting the market price because of inadequate market depth or market disruption' (BCBS, 2006b, p. 1).

## Liquidity Coverage Ratio (LCR)

The reliance on 'liquidity through marketability' (Turner, 2009) and wholesale funding resulted in excessive maturity transformation by the banking sector, with genuinely liquid assets making up about only 1% of total bank liabilities in 2008 (Goodhart, 2008).

Basel III introduced the LCR in order to 'promote the short-  term resilience  of  the  liquidity  risk  profile  of  banks'  (BCBS,  2013b,  p.  1). This  is achieved by ensuring that banking institutions have sufficient High Quality Liquid Assets (HQLA) that can be easily and immediately converted into cash in private markets under a 30 calendar day liquidity stress scenario.

The LCR is defined as:

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

where:

- ■ Stock of HQLA is  the value of the stock of unencumbered HQLA in stressed conditions (Level 1 and Level 2 assets).
- ■ Total net cash outflows over the next 30 calendar days are the net cash outflows  calculated  according  to  a  number  of  scenario  parameters. These are assumed outflow parameters and are designed by the regulator (usually following Basel III set guidance).

Practically,  the  ratio  implies  that  the  stock  of  HQLA  should  at  least equal total assumed net cash outflows.

## Net Stable Funding Ratio (NSFR)

The transformation of short-  term liabilities to long-  term assets constitutes a fundamental role of the banking system in the economy (BCBS, 2014a).

14    Unsurprisingly,  the  principles  for  sound  liquidity  risk  management  and  supervision  were  heavily  influenced  by  the  collapse  of  Lehman  Brothers  in September 2008.

Basel III introduced an explicit funding liquidity standard (the NSFR) with the objective to increase the use of sufficiently stable (longer-  term) sources of funding. The specification of the NSFR is as follows:

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

where:

- ■ Available amount of stable funding (ASF) is the amount of capital and liabilities expected to be reliable over a one-  year time horizon.
- ■ Required amount of stable funding (RSF) is the amount of stable funding  required  by  an  institution's  liquidity  characteristics  and  residual maturities of the various on-  balance sheet assets and off-  balance sheet exposures.

ASF and RSF factors are supplied in the Basel III guidance. Slightly differently to LCR, which takes an absolute (but real) number and divides it by a number based on a set of assumptions, NSFR takes a number based on a set of assumptions and divides it by a number based on a set of assumptions.

In theory, the NSFR aims to extend the stability of the bank funding horizon beyond the 30-  day requirement of the LCR.

Interestingly, and contrary to the BCBS's intentions, the Basel III liquidity  standards may result in competition for the types of liquid retail and wholesale funding required, therefore undermining rather than increasing financial stability (Hartlage, 2013). Similarly, the NSFR has been criticised for making deposits less stable, as banks will compete for this scarce source of  funding,  encouraging  maturity  transformation  activities  to  migrate to  the 'shadow'  banking  sector,  and  for  being  detrimental  to  economic growth, especially in emerging markets and developing economies (Gobat et al. , 2014).

## The Fundamental Review of the Trading Book (FRTB)

As part of the Basel III reform, the Basel Committee published its Minimum Capital Requirements for Market Risk in January 2019 (BCBS, 2019b), a document that is still euphemistically referred to as the Fundamental Review of the Trading Book (FRTB). In most jurisdictions, a bank that operates a designated Trading Book is required to set capital for it under FRTB rules. Certain jurisdictions, such as the PRA, exempt the book from FRTB rules if it is below a specified small size.

The 2019 Market Risk standard was the result of an extensive consultation approach that commenced in 2012 (BCBS, 2012). Following an additional two rounds of consultation (BCBS, 2013c, 2014c) and an interim impact analysis (BCBS, 2015c), the 'first final' standard for market risk was published in January 2016 (BCBS, 2016a, 2016b), only to be refined again two years later following a fourth consultation round in March 2018 (BCBS, 2018c).

The revised Market Risk standard focuses on four core areas:

1. A revised boundary between the banking book and the trading book to reduce incentives for regulatory capital arbitrage between the two. Banks may only include instruments in the trading book when there is no impediment against selling or fully hedging it. Such instruments must be fair valued daily and valuation changes must be recognised in the profit and loss (P&amp;L) account.
2. A  more  granular  model  approval  process  was  introduced  whereby internal models are approved for use at the trading desk level. In addition, separate, more stringent capital requirements were introduced for 'non-  modellable risk factors' (NMRFs), as well as new test metrics to discern poorly performing models.
3. A revised internal models approach substituting VaR with an Expected Shortfall  (ES)  approach, 15   accompanied  by  separate  NMRF  capital requirements and adjustments to capital requirements to address cliff effects.  Lastly,  a  number  of  constraints  were  imposed  on  the  capital reducing effects of hedging and portfolio diversification.
4. The standardised approach for market risk was revised to make it sufficiently risk sensitive and to serve as a credible fallback, as well as a floor, to  the  internal  models  approach. The  revised  standardised  approach for market risk introduces the sensitivities-  based method (SbM) which expands on the use of sensitivities (delta, vega and curvature risk factor sensitivities) 16 for market risk classes, including general interest rate risk (GIRR), foreign exchange (FX) risk, equity risk and commodity risk.

15    In  theory,  ES  constitutes  a  more  coherent  and  comprehensive  risk  measure that takes better account of tail risks and market illiquidity risk (Acerbi and Tasche, 2002a, 2002b).

16    Note that delta, vega and curvature risk measures are regulatory constructs and not the synonymous parameters encountered in market risk valuations, albeit conceptually similar to some extent. Delta is a risk measure based on sensitivities of a bank's trading book to regulatory delta factors. The vega risk measure is also based on sensitivities to regulatory vega risk factors. Lastly, curvature is a risk measure which captures the incremental risk not captured by the delta risk of price changes in the value of an option. Vega and curvature are only applicable to instruments with optionality.

## The Market Risk Standardised Approach

Under the original market risk amendment (BCBS, 1996, 2005a), the standardised measurement approach capital charge is the arithmetic sum of the risk measures obtained from interest rate, equity, foreign exchange, commodity risk and price risk in options of all kinds.

The revised standardised approach for market risk (BCBS, 2016a) introduces three components for the capital requirement for non-  securitisation and securitisation exposures in the trading book.

- ■ The sensitivities-  based  method ,  which expands the use of sensitivities (delta,  vega  and  curvature  risk  factor  sensitivities)  for  the  following risk classes:
- ■ General interest-rate risk (GIRR);
- ■ Credit Spread Risk (CSR): non-  securitisation;
- ■ CSR: securitisation;
- ■ CSR: securitisation correlation trading portfolio (CTP);
- ■ Foreign exchange (FX) risk;
- ■ Equity risk;
- ■ Commodity risk.
- ■ The standardised default risk charge (DRC), which is intended to capture  jump-  to-  default  (JTD)  risk.  In  parallel,  the  DRC  is  expected  to reduce the potential discrepancy in capital requirements for similar risk exposures across the banking book and the trading book. The default risk charge applies to the following risk classes:
- ■ Default risk: non-  securitisation;
- ■ Default risk: securitisation;
- ■ Default risk: securitisation correlation trading portfolio (CTP).
- ■ The residual risk add-  on is introduced to capture risks beyond the main risk  factors  captured  by  the  previous  two  charges.  Risk  weights  are applied to the notional amounts of instruments with non-  linear payoffs.

The overall structured of the standardised approach for market risks is illustrated in Figure 3.8.

In  order  to  facilitate  adoption  of  this  standard  for  banks  other  than those that are large and internationally active, the Basel Committee published a consultative document with its proposals for a simplified alternative to the market risk standardised approach (BCBS, 2017e). The reduced sensitivities-  based method (R-  SbM) is intended to address implementation challenges for banks that maintain smaller or simpler trading books and is made up of three components: (i) the risk charges under the R-  SbM; (ii) the default risk charge; and (iii) the residual risk add-  on. As such, it removes

FIGURE 3.8 Structure of the standardised approach for market risks.

<!-- image -->

the capital requirements for vega and curvature risks, simplifies the basis risk calculation and reduces the risk factor granularity and the correlations scenarios applied.

## Internal Models Approach (IMA)

Upon the explicit approval of a bank's supervisory authority, the use of an internal model for the measurement of market risks is permitted, subject to explicit  qualitative  and  quantitative  standards. At a minimum, banks are required to calculate an expected shortfall (ES) on a daily basis for the bankwide internal model, as well as for each trading desk, at a 97.5th percentile, one-  tailed confidence interval. The ES for a liquidity horizon must be calculated from an ES at a base liquidity horizon of 10 days with scaling applied as follows:

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

where:

- ■ ES is the regulatory liquidity adjusted ES;
- ■ T is the length of the base horizon, i.e. 10 days;

- ■ ES T (P) is the ES at horizon T of a portfolio with positions P = (p i ) with respect to shocks to all risk factors that the positions P are exposed to;
- ■ ES T (P, j) is the ES at horizon T of a portfolio with positions P = (p i ) with respect to shocks for each position p i in the subset of risk factors Q(p i , j) with all other risk factors held constant;
- ■ the ES at horizon T , ES T (P) must be calculated for changes in the risk factors, and ES T (P, j) must be calculated for changes in the relevant subset Q(p i , j) of risk factors, over the time interval T without scaling from a shorter horizon;
- ■ Q(p i , j) j is the subset of risk factors whose liquidity horizons for the desk where p i is booked are at least as long as LH j ;
- ■ the time series of changes in risk factors over the base time interval T may be determined by overlapping observations;
- ■ LH j is the liquidity horizon j .

In Chapter 25 (the guide to the book's website) we include a set of slides that illustrate these principles in greater detail.

## The Leverage Ratio

Basel III was a response to the 2008 Global Financial Crisis which was, to a certain extent, triggered by the 'build-  up of excessive on- and off-  balance sheet leverage in the banking system' (BCBS, 2014d, p. 1). 17  Therefore, in addition to the risk-  based approaches to calculating capital requirements, the BCBS introduced a non-  risk based leverage ratio with an objective to restrict the build-  up of leverage in the banking system and to act as a 'backstop' against model risk embedded in the risk-based approaches.

The leverage ratio is defined as the Tier 1 capital of Basel III over the total exposure measure:

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

where:

- ■ Capital measure is the Tier 1 capital of Basel III as defined in paragraphs 49-96 of the Basel III framework (BCBS, 2011c);
- ■ Total exposure measure is the sum of:

17    In fact the causes of the 'GFC' were many and varied, and not due simply to the factors noted in the BCBS paper. For a more accurate and complete analysis of what culminated in the crash (and best exemplified by the failure of Lehman Brothers, Royal Bank of Scotland and Citigroup, amongst others, in 2008) see the author's book The Future of Finance (Choudhry and Landuyt, 2010).

- On-  balance sheet non-  derivative exposures, net of specific provisions or accounting valuation adjustments;
- Derivative exposures;
- Securities Financing Transaction (SFT) exposures;
- Off-  balance sheet (OBS) items.

The Tier 1 leverage ratio was set at 3%. Practically, this means that banks will be limited to lending 33 times their capital, a value lower than that observed at some banks before the GFC.

In  December  2017,  the  Basel  Committee  introduced  a  leverage  ratio surcharge  for  the  largest,  systemically  significant  banks  (BCBS,  2017a) which will be discussed later.

It is worth noting that, even though the leverage ratio was designed as a backstop to the build-  up of leverage, it did not come without its practical drawbacks. Given the huge injections of liquidity in the banking sector as a response to the Covid-  19 crisis in 2020, it ended up becoming a constraint on bank lending, without a doubt an unintended consequence.

## THE 2017 BASEL III REFORMS ('BASEL IV')

In December 2017, the Basel Committee published further refinements to the Basel III framework (BCBS, 2017a) which complement the initial phase of  the  Basel  III  reforms. The  main  objective  of  the  2017  revisions  was  to reduce the excessive variability of risk weighted assets (RWAs) that have been observed as part of the BCBS Regulatory Consistency Assessment Programme (RCAP), and therefore improve the comparability of banks' capital ratios.

The 2017 revisions can be summarised as follows:

1. Enhancing  the  robustness  and  risk  sensitivity  of  the  standardised approaches for credit risk, credit valuation adjustment (CVA) and operational risk.
2. Constraining the use of internally modelled approaches by placing limits  on  certain inputs used to calculate capital requirements under the internal ratings-  based (IRB) approach for credit risk and by removing the use of the internal model approaches for CVA risk and for operational risk.
3. Introducing a leverage ratio buffer to further limit the leverage of global systemically important banks (G-  SIBs).
4. Replacing the existing Basel II output floor with a more robust risksensitive floor based on the Committee's revised Basel III standardised approaches.

Let's discuss each of the revisions in more depth now.

## Standardised Approach for Credit Risk

Even though large banks in developed jurisdictions are likely to rely on the IRB approaches for their estimation of credit risk capital requirements, we should remember that the vast majority of banks around the world employ the standardised approach. As a consequence, and in order to both improve the  granularity  as  well  as  the  consistent  application  of  the  standardised approach,  the  2017  refinements  introduced  further  enhancements  to  the applicable risk weights.

In  addition,  and  even  though  the  original  Basel  III  rules  already attempted  to  reduce  the  mechanistic  reliance  of  financial  institutions  on external ratings, the 2017 revisions went a step further. The standardised approach was enhanced first by requiring banks to conduct sufficient due diligence of external ratings and second by developing a more granular nonratings-  based approach for jurisdictions that cannot or do not wish to rely on external credit ratings.

Lastly, in an effort to improve the comparability of internally modelled capital requirements across banks (and consequently develop a 'level playing field'), the revised standardised approach is designated as the 'output floor' for the IRB approaches, accompanied by related disclosures.

The 2017 revisions to the standardised approach for credit risk can be summarised as follows:

- ■ Unrated exposures: a more granular approach has been developed for unrated exposures to banks and corporates.
- ■ Jurisdictions where the ratings exposure is not permitted: the more granular approach to unrated exposures also applies for rated exposures in jurisdictions where the use of credit ratings is permitted.
- ■ Exposures to banks: some of the risk weights for rated exposures have been recalibrated. In addition, the risk weighted treatment for unrated exposures is more granular than the previous flat risk weight.
- ■ Covered  bonds: a  standalone  treatment  for  covered  bonds  has  been introduced.
- ■ Exposures to corporates: a more granular look-  up table has been developed. A specific risk weight applies to exposures to SMEs.
- ■ Specialised  lending: standalone  treatment  for  exposures  to  project finance, object finance and commodities finance.
- ■ Residential  real  estate: the  existing,  single  risk  weight,  approach  has been  refined  to  introduce  risk  weights  varying  based  on  the  loan  to value (LTV) ratio of the mortgage.
- ■ Retail exposures: a more granular treatment distinguishes between different types of retail exposures (e.g., revolving facilities where credit is

- typically  drawn  upon  versus  transactors  where  the  facility  is  used  to facilitate transactions rather than a source of credit).
- ■ Commercial real estate exposures: more risk sensitive approaches than the flat risk weight which applied previously.
- ■ Subordinated  debt  and  equity  exposures :  more  granular  risk  weights relative to the flat risk weight which applied previously.
- ■ Off-  balance sheet items: the  credit  conversion factors (CCFs) used to convert off-  balance sheet exposures to loan equivalents have been made more  risk-  sensitive,  including  the  introduction  of  positive  CCFs  for unconditionally cancellable commitments (UCCs).

The detailed approach is described in the Basel paper and is recommended to be studied in full for those involved in their bank's capital strategy and capital planning process.

## I n t e r n a l   R a t i n g s -   B a s e d   A p p r o a c h e s   f o r   C r e d i t   R i s k

The internal ratings-  based approaches introduced by Basel II had been heralded as a significant  step  towards  better  risk  sensitivity,  allowing  banks to  utilise  internal  estimates  of  credit  risk  parameters  in  their  calculation of regulatory capital requirements. However, just over 10 years later, such approaches were considered to carry a number of shortcomings, especially with regards to the lack of robustness in the modelling of certain asset classes (and the associated lack of capacity by regulators to review all such models), as well as the large divergence in banks' internally modelled IRB requirements, as indicated by the Regulatory Consistency Assessment Programme.

To address this, the Basel Committee introduced the following revisions to the IRB approaches:

- ■ Application  of  the  Advanced  IRB  approach  for  certain  asset  classes: the 2017 revised IRB framework removed the ability to use the A-  IRB approach for exposures to large and mid-  sized corporates, and for exposures to banks and other financial institutions. For all such exposures, the F-  IRB approach (whereby LGD and EAD parameters are based on supervisory estimates) becomes the fall-  back approach. In addition, all IRB approaches for credit risk exposures to equities were removed. A comparison of the approaches available for the various portfolios under Basel II and the revised Basel III is summarised in Table 3.5.
- ■ Specification  of  input  floors: the  2017  revised  IRB  framework  introduced or updated minimum floor values for PDs (under both the F-  IRB and A-  IRB approaches), and LGDs and EADs (for the I-  IRB approach). The floors for corporate and retail asset classes, across credit risk parameters, are summarised in Table 3.6.

TABLE 3.5 Application of approaches to portfolios.

| Portfolio / exposure                                                  | Basel II approaches        | Basel III approaches          |
|-----------------------------------------------------------------------|----------------------------|-------------------------------|
| Large and mid-sized corporates (consolidated revenues > €500 million) | A-IRB, F-IRB, SA           | F-IRB, SA                     |
| Banks and other financial institutions                                | A-IRB, F-IRB, SA           | F-IRB, SA                     |
| Equities                                                              | Various IRB approaches     | SA                            |
| Specialised lending                                                   | A-IRB, F-IRB, slotting, SA | A-IRB, F-IRB, slotting, SA 18 |

TABLE 3.6 Input and output floors.

| Portfolio      | PD              | LGD unsecured   | LGD secured                                                                                                                    | EAD                                                                                                                                |
|----------------|-----------------|-----------------|--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| Corporate      |                 |                 |                                                                                                                                |                                                                                                                                    |
| All classes    | 5 basis points  | 25%             | Varying by collateral type: ▪ 0% financials ▪ 10% receivables ▪ 10% commercial or residential real estate ▪ 15% other physical | Floor is the sum of ▪ The on-balance sheet exposure ▪ 50% of the off- balance sheet exposure using the standard- ised approach CCF |
| Retail         |                 |                 |                                                                                                                                |                                                                                                                                    |
| Mortgage QRRE  | 5 basis points  | N/A             | 5%                                                                                                                             | Floor is the sum of ▪ The on-balance                                                                                               |
| trans- actors  | 5 basis points  | 50%             | N/A                                                                                                                            | sheet exposure                                                                                                                     |
| QRRE revolvers | 10 basis points | 50%             | N/A                                                                                                                            | ▪ 50% of the off- balance sheet                                                                                                    |
| Other retail   | 5 basis points  | 30%             | Varying by collateral type: ▪ 0% financials ▪ 10% receivables ▪ 10% commercial or residential real estate ▪ 15% other physical | exposure using the standardised approach CCF                                                                                       |

- ■ Specification of further parameter estimation practices: in order to further reduce RWA variability, the 2017 revisions introduced further specifications in the estimation of credit risk parameters. Such adjustments included increasing the haircuts that apply to the collateral and reducing the LGD parameters for exposures secured by non-  financial collateral and, for unsecured exposures, reducing the LGD parameter for exposures to non-  financial corporates to 40% (from 45%).

In light of these adjustments, as well as the introduction of the aggregate output floor, the Basel Committee removed the 1.06 scaling factor that was previously applied to RWAs determined by the IRB approaches to credit risk.

As expected, most of these revisions were met with criticism by some banks  and  other  commentators.  The  criticisms  focused  primarily  on  the reduction  in  the  risk  sensitivity  of  the  IRB  approaches  and  the  reduced incentive for banks to improve their internal modelling capabilities. With the application of an output floor determined by the standardised approach, the motivation for banks to move towards the IRB approaches is naturally curtailed,  while  the  operational  and  regulatory  burdens  associated  with such approaches remain cumbersome. This notwithstanding, the revisions went ahead and were published as part of the 2017 update. From a process implementation perspective we believe there is a strong incentive to remain on the standardised approach for all but the largest banks.

## Standardised Approach for CVA

The  initial  phase  of  the  Basel  III  reforms  introduced  an  explicit  capital charge for Credit Valuation Adjustment (CVA) risk. CVA risk pertains to the potential mark-  to-  market losses of derivative instruments resulting from the deterioration in the creditworthiness of the derivative counterparty. CVA losses were amongst the largest losses observed at some banks during the 2008 Global Financial Crisis.

As part of the 2017 revisions, the Basel Committee updated the CVA framework in order to:

- ■ Take into account the exposure component of CVA.
- ■ Remove the previously available use of an internally modelled approach to CVA (IMA-  CVA), allowing only the standardised approach (SA-  CVA) or  the  basic  approach  (BA-  CVA).  Banks  with  an  aggregate  notional amount of non-  centrally cleared derivatives less than or equal to €100 billion may calculate their CVA capital charge as a simple multiplier of their counterparty credit risk charge.

- ■ The SA-  CVA and BA-  CVA approaches were calibrated to be consistent with the approaches used in the revised market risk framework. In particular,  the  SA-  CVA  approach,  like  the  market  risk  approaches,  is based on fair value sensitivities to market risk factors and the BA-  CVA is benchmarked to the SA-  CVA approach.

Despite the focus on CVA, please note that other valuation adjustment parameters (particularly 'DVA', 'FVA' and other so-  called 'xVAs') are not discussed or applied in the context of the Basel III frameworks.

## The Standardised Measurement Approach (SMA) t o   O p e r a t i o n a l   R i s k

The Basel II framework introduced the advanced measurement approach (AMA) to  operational  risk  related  capital  requirements.  Similarly  to  the advanced approaches for market risk and credit risk, the AMA allowed the use  of  internal  data  and  estimated  parameters.  However,  the  insufficient loss histories and the high severity, low frequency nature of operational risk events made the Basel Committee reconsider its support for this approach.

The 2017 revisions to Basel III replaced the AMA, as well as the existing three available standardised approaches to operational risk, with a single standardised measurement approach (SMA) to be used by all banks.

The operational risk capital under the SMA is the product of the business indicator component (BIC) and the internal loss multiplier (ILM), the latter being a crude remnant of some sort of risk sensitivity to the framework.

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

where:

- ■ BIC is the business indicator component, calculated as the sum of the interest,  leases  and  dividend  component  (ILDC),  the  services  component  (SC)  and  the  financial  component  (FC) averaged  over  the  past three years ;
- ■ ILM is the internal loss multiplier, which is determined based on the loss component (LC) and the BIC.

The BIC is an increasing function of the BI depending on the buckets as defined by the BCBS (2016c), and is based on the 3-  year average of the following parameters:

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

where:

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

SC avg
