The ILM is defined as:

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

where the  Ln exp 1 1 0 541 /g11 /g12 /g16 /g11 /g12 /g124 . represents the lower bound of the ILM.

The loss component reflects the operational loss exposure of a bank that can be inferred from its internal loss experience and distinguishes between loss events above €10 million and €100 million, and smaller loss events.

Despite these seemingly complex formulas, the SMA is a rather mechanic calculation approach that, despite its stated intentions to provide some sort of risk sensitivity to the operational risk capital charge outputs, is primarily driven by regulatory parameters and calibrations.

## Leverage Ratio Framework

The 2017 revisions to the leverage ratio have been two-  fold. First, an explicit buffer for G-  SIBs has been introduced, which should be met with Tier 1 capital, and is set at 50% of a G-  SIB's risk weighted higher loss absorbency requirements (buffers). Practically, a G-  SIB subject to a 2% risk-  weighted higher-  loss absorbency requirement would be subject to an additional 1% leverage ratio buffer requirement.

In addition to the G-  SIB buffer, the Basel Committee introduced a number of refinements to the definition of the leverage ratio exposure measure. These  refinements  include,  amongst  others,  modifying  the  way  in  which derivatives are reflected in the exposure measure and updating the treatment of off-  balance sheet exposures to ensure consistency with their measurement in the standardised approach to credit risk.

## Output Floor

Output floors were not in fact a Basel III 'innovation'. Basel II had already introduced an output floor calibrated at 80% of the Basel I capital requirements. In order to avoid the inconsistent application of the previous output floor, the Basel Committee introduced a revised output floor based on the revised Basel III standardised approaches.

The revised output floor is set at 72.5% of the total RWA, calculated as per the standardised approaches. In other words, when the total RWA based on the supervisory approved approaches (including both standardised and internal model-  based approaches) falls below 72.5% of the equivalent standardised approach calculation, the higher of the two RWA measures will apply.

According to the Basel Committee, such an output floor 'helps to maintain  a  level  playing  field  between  banks  using  internal  models  and  those on the standardised approaches' while also supporting 'the credibility of banks' risk-  weighted calculations' (BCBS, 2017c, p. 11). However, and as previously discussed, the output floor has been criticised as a disincentive for  moving towards the more advanced approaches to risk measurement and capital estimation. This may, or may not, be a bad thing.

## FROM 2017 TO THE FUTURE

Regulators and industry practitioners alike have accepted the fact that there  will  be  no  'final'  Basel  guidance;  instead,  banks  have  to  deal

TABLE 3.7 Basel revisions implementation dates.

| Revision                              | Original implementation date   | Updated implementation date   |
|---------------------------------------|--------------------------------|-------------------------------|
| Standardised approach for credit risk | 1 Jan 2022                     | 1 Jan 2023                    |

with  a  continuously  evolving  set  of  standards. At  the  time  of  writing, a  number of regulatory items remain open and are summarised in the Appendix to the chapter. For a full list of open and closed items, see the respective BIS web page (https://www.bis.org/basel\_framework/timeline .htm?type=all).

## Covid-  19 Related Measures

The  2017  revisions  to  the  Basel  III  framework  came  with  transitional arrangements pertaining to the implementation dates of the standards. Since 27 March 2020, the transitional implementation dates were extended by one year to account for the impact of the Covid-  19 pandemic and the need for banks to focus on supporting the economy (BCBS, 2020).

The  original  and  revised  implementation  timeline  is  summarised  in Table 3.7.

Judging from past experience, the  forthcoming  changes  marked  here will likely only constitute a fraction of the future changes expected. As the financial services landscape changes, so will regulations. Whether these will focus on further enhancing current standards or expanding their scope (e.g., to the non-  regulated 'shadow banking' sector) remains to be seen.

## CRYPTOASSETS

It is to be expected that, at some point, prudential regulation would address cryptoassets. 19  In June 2021 the Basel Committee on Banking Supervision issued a consultative document on the prudential treatment of cryptoassets exposures which - due to industry pushback - is expected to be further revised (see BCBS, 2021a).

The consultative  document  proposes  determining  the  minimum  riskbased capital requirements for credit and market risk according to the following classification:

- ■ Group 1a cryptoassets: tokenised traditional assets (for example, bonds, loans, deposits, equities, commodities or cash held in custody).
- ■ Group  1b  cryptoassets:  tokenised  assets  that  include  a  stabilisation mechanism  (for  example, 'stablecoins'),  where  their  value  does  not exceed 10 bps of the value of the underlying traditional asset(s) more than three times over a one-  year period.
- ■ Group 2 cryptoassets: cryptoasset exposures (such as Bitcoin) that do not meet any of the classification conditions required for Group 1 cryptoassets, namely:
- ■ Legal enforceability and settlement finality.
- ■ Operational risk mitigation and management.
- ■ Entities that execute redemptions, transfers, or settlement finality of the cryptoasset are regulated and supervised.

Tokenised cryptoassets (Group 1) are proposed to be subject to at least equivalent risk-  based capital requirements as set out in the existing Basel capital  framework  for  non-  tokenised  traditional  assets.  In  addition,  for Group 1b cryptoassets, there is new guidance on the application of current rules to capture the risks relating to stabilisation mechanisms with further consideration for capital add-  ons.

Group 2 cryptoassets are proposed to be subject to a 1250% risk weight on the absolute value of the aggregate long positions and the absolute value of the aggregate short positions to which the bank is exposed. Practically, exposure to cryptoassets such as Bitcoin are deducted from capital, with the additional punitive measure that short positions are also deducted, albeit there may be no balance sheet asset to deduct.

Note that central bank issued digital currencies (CBDCs) are out of the scope of the proposed prudential treatment.

19    Cryptoassets are defined as private digital assets that depend primarily on cryptography and distributed ledger or similar technology (FSB, 2020).

In  addition  to  the  minimum  credit  and  market  risk-  weighted  rules, including CVA risk, cryptoasset exposures regardless of group should be:

- ■ included in the leverage ratio exposure measure according to their value for financial reporting purposes;
- ■ subjected to large exposure limits to each specific counterparty or group of connected counterparties;
- ■ subjected to the LCR and NSFR liquidity ratios . Group 2 cryptoassets in particular must be subject to a 0% inflow for the LCR, while cryptoasset liabilities must be subject to a 100% outflow. In addition, Group 2 cryptoassets must be subject to a 100% required stable funding factor (RSF) for the NSFR, while cryptoasset liabilities must be subject to a 0% available stable funding factor (ASF).

The proposed Pillar 1 requirements are further subject to Pillar 2 internal adequacy and supervisory review processes. Banks with direct or indirect exposures to any form of cryptoasset should ensure that risks attributable to operational and cyber risk, the underlying technology and money laundering / the financing of terrorism should be appropriately assessed, managed and mitigated.

Despite the BCBS's stated principle of 'same risk, same activity, same treatment', the prudential standards for Group 2 cryptoassets in particular were criticised by industry bodies such as ISDA, who have argued that excessively  penal  treatment  may  result  in  regulatory  fragmentation  and increase the risk of concentration outside of the regulatory perimeter (IIF, ISDA and CoDC, 2021).

On the back of the criticism, the Basel Committee indicated in November 2021 that it would review its proposals.

## CLIMATE CHANGE RELATED FINANCIAL RISKS

In November 2021 the Basel Committee issued a consultative paper on the principles for the effective management and supervision of climate related financial risks (BCBS, 2021b).

The 18 principles proposed seek to achieve 'a balance in improving practices related to the management of climate-  related financial risks and providing a common baseline for internationally active banks and supervisors,' while maintaining sufficient flexibility given the degree of heterogeneity and evolving practices in this area.

Principles 1 through 12 provide banks with guidance on the effective management of climate-  related financial risks and can further be classified under the following categories:

- ■ Corporate governance;
- ■ Internal control framework;
- ■ Capital and liquidity adequacy;
- ■ Risk management process;
- ■ Management monitoring and reporting;
- ■ Comprehensive management of credit risk;
- ■ Comprehensive  management  of  market,  liquidity,  operational  and other risks;
- ■ Scenario analysis.

The  principles  under  each  of  the  categories  aim  to  capture  climate change related financial  risks  across  various  time  horizons,  as  such  risks may materialise in horizons longer than a bank's traditional 2-3 year capital  planning  timeframe,  as  well  as  beyond  the  maturity  of  longer  dated positions.

Principles 13 through 18 provide guidance for prudential supervisors along the following two categories:

- ■ Prudential regulatory and supervisory requirements for banks;
- ■ Responsibilities, powers and functions of supervisors.

The proposed principles are intended to be applied on a proportionate basis depending on the size, complexity and risk profile of the bank or banking sector for which the authority is responsible. For scenario analysis and stress testing, the principles have been formulated with a view towards application  to  large,  internationally  active  banks  and  to  supervisory  and other  relevant  financial  authorities  in  Basel  Committee  member  jurisdictions. However, smaller banks and authorities in all jurisdictions are encouraged to consider similarly structured approaches.

## CHAPTER SUMMARY

An international harmonised approach to setting minimum capital levels for banks, to address the credit risk they faced arising from ordinary business, was introduced with the Basel standards in 1988, for implementation in 1992. These were quite basic in their approach to setting credit risk levels and in the way the capital level was calculated, although in today's era of FRTB, IFRS9, CCPs, xVAs and so on (these acronyms are deliberately not explained here, to illustrate ever-  increasing complexity without necessarily increasing market stability by a commensurate amount), these might not be seen to be negative characteristics.

The simplicity of the original Basel guidance (now referred to as Basel I) resulted in the Basel II framework in 2006, which might be summarised with the description that they allowed for more granular approaches and theoretically  more  accurate  data  inputs  to  calculating  credit  risk  capital required. Basel II also introduced the requirement for an annual 'capital adequacy' process, which today is an involved and onerous stress testing procedure often conducted monthly at some banks for their own internal motivations.

Basel III was introduced a little later and essentially raised the minimum capital  requirement  not  by  changing  the  original  8%  level,  now  referred to as 'Pillar 1 capital', but by adding other capital 'buffers', a universal one of which was the capital conservation buffer set at 2.5%. In practice, every bank's minimum capital level today is 10.5% of their 'risk weighted assets' (RWAs), being the 8% level of Pillar 1 and 2.5% level of the CCB. 20 Other buffers are applicable depending on the national regulator policy and the size of the bank. Basel III Final Form ('Basel IV') - which is the current form of guidance as at the time of writing - introduced additional features still, addressing topics such as FRTB, interest-  rate risk in the banking book (IRRBB), minimum required eligible liabilities (MREL), and so on and on and on.

The impact of new and/or changing environmental circumstances, such as the rise in 'crypto' assets and climate change, will bring forth further regulatory guidelines from the Basel Committee.

Finally, we should note that not every national regulator has followed every aspect of Basel III and Basel III Final Form when setting its supervision rules. It is perfectly acceptable to adopt only those parts of the guidance that are best suited to the banking market in question.

20    This statement is accurate because not every bank has to set an additional 'Pil-

lar 2a' capital level. If their analysis shows that their additional exposures are already covered by their Pillar 1 calculation, there is no Pillar 2a add-  on. The author has come across such banks, although they are in a minority around the world!

| RE commercial - property CF eligible                     | 80%                    | 80%                    | 100%        | 130%        | 130%         | 130%  |

TABLE A3.4 Updates to the Basel framework since 2017.

| Standard                                                            | Description                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                  | Publication date   | Effective date   |
|---------------------------------------------------------------------|--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|--------------------|------------------|
| RBC20 Calculation of minimum risk based capital standards           | Paragraph CRE20.4(2) amended to remove reference to transi- tional provisions that expire by 1 January 2028 (as announced on 27 March 2020). Also, cross-references to the securitisation chapters updated to include a reference to the chapter on NPL securitisations (CRE45) published on 26 November 2020.                                                                                               | Nov 2020           | Jan 2028         |
| RBC20 Calculation of minimum risk based capital standards           | Changes to output floor and approaches available to calculate credit and operational risk capital requirements, as set out in the December 2017 Basel III publication, including revised implementation date announced on 27 March 2020. Also, cross-references to the securitisation chapters updated to include a reference to the chapter on NPL securitisations (CRE45) pub- lished on 26 November 2020. | Nov 2020           | Jan 2023         |
| DIS10 Definitions and applications                                  | Updated Excel format tables to take account of DIS26 becoming effective, changes to DIS40, changes set out in the December 2018 Pillar 3 disclosure publication, the revised implementa- tion date announced on 27 March 2020 and updated cross- references due to the introduction of CRE45 announced on 26 November 2020.                                                                                  | Nov 2020           | Jan 2023         |
| DIS20 Overview of risk management, key pru- dential metrics and RWA | Updated to include the disclosure of: (i) leverage and capital ratios that exclude the output floor in the computation of RWA (Tem- plate KM1); and (ii) the level of the output floor and the result- ant floor adjustment (Template OV1). Updated to take account of new implementation date as announced on 27 March 2020.                                                                                | Mar 2020           | Jan 2023         |

| Standard                                                                                                                                    | Description                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                               | Publication date   | Effective date   |
|---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|--------------------|------------------|
| DIS21 Comparison of modelled and standard- ised RWA                                                                                         | First version in the format of the consolidated framework, updated to take account of new implementation date as announced on 27 March 2020. Also, cross-references to the securitisation chapters updated to include a reference to the chapter on NPL securitisations (CRE45) published on 26 November 2020.                            | Nov 2020           | Jan 2023         |
| DIS26 Capital distribution constraints                                                                                                      | Updated to include disclosure requirements for G-SIBs and the new implementation date announced on 27 March 2020.                                                                                                                                                                                                                         | Mar 2020           | Jan 2023         |
| DIS40 Credit risk                                                                                                                           | Updated to include additional disclosure requirements related to the prudential treatment of problem assets (Table CRB-A), updated cross-references and other changes to take account of changes in the credit risk standard in the December 2017 Basel III publication. Reflects revised implementation date announced on 27 March 2020. | Mar 2020           | Jan 2023         |
| CRE20 Standardised approach: individual exposures CRE22 Standardised approach: credit risk mitigation CRE34 IRB approach: RWA for purchased | Changes due to the December 2017 Basel III publication and the revised implementation date announced on 27 March 2020. Also, cross-references to the securitisation chapters updated to include a reference to the chapter on NPL securitisations                                                                                         | Nov 2020           | Jan 2023         |
|                                                                                                                                             | (CRE45) published on 26 November 2020.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                    |                    |                  |
| receivables                                                                                                                                 |                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                           |                    |                  |

TABLE A3.4 (Continued)

| Standard                                                      | Description                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                           | Publication date   | Effective date   |
|---------------------------------------------------------------|-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|--------------------|------------------|
| CRE40 Securitisation: general provisions                      | The 1.06 scaling factor has been removed to reflect its removal in the December 2017 publication of Basel III. The simple, trans- parent and comparable criteria related to credit risk of underly- ing exposures has been adapted to reflect the credit risk asset classes as defined in the December 2017 Basel III standardised approach for credit risk. The revised implementation date is as announced on 27 March 2020.A reference to the treatment of exposures to securitisations of non-performing loans (CRE45) is introduced in CRE40.48. | Nov 2020           | Jan 2023         |
| CRE45 Securitisations of non-performing loans                 | First version in the format of the consolidated framework, intro- duced to give effect to the treatment of exposures to securitisa- tions of non-performing loans published on 26 November 2020.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                      | Nov 2020           | Jan 2023         |
| CRE60 Equity investments in funds                             | Consequential changes resulting from changes to scope of internal ratings-based approach for credit risk that come into effect due to the December 2017 Basel III publication and the revised implementation date announced on 27 March 2020.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                         | Mar 2020           | Jan 2023         |
| MAR10 Market risk terminology                                 | Reflects revisions in the standardised and internal models approach for market risk, including the shift to an expected shortfall measure. Also reflects the revised implementation date announced on 27 March 2020.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                  | Mar 2020           | Jan 2023         |
| MAR20 Standardised approach: general provisions and structure | Introduces a revised approach based on expanded use of sensitivi- ties as set out in the January 2019 market risk publication and updated to take account of the revised implementation date announced on 27 March 2020.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                              | Mar 2020           | Jan 2023         |
| MAR30 Internal models approach: general provisions            | More rigorous model approval process that enables supervisors to remove internal modelling permission for individual trading desks. Updated to take account of the revised implementation date announced on 27 March 2020.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                            | Mar 2020           | Jan 2023         |

| Standard                                                                  | Description                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                               | Publication date   | Effective date   |
|---------------------------------------------------------------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|--------------------|------------------|
| MAR40 Simplified standardised approach                                    | First version in the format of the consolidated framework updated to take account of the revised implementation date announced on 27 March 2020. Also, cross-references to the securitisation chapters updated to include a reference to the chapter on NPL securitisations (CRE45) published on 26 November 2020.                                        | Nov 2020           | Jan 2023         |
| MAR50 Credit valuation adjustment framework                               | Changes to give effect to the target revisions to the CVA frame- work published on the 8 July 2020.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                       | Jul 2020           | Jan 2023         |
| OPE10 Definitions and application                                         | Reflects revised standardised approach introduced in the December 2017 Basel III publication (including the revised implementation date announced on 27 March 2020) and removal of internal model approaches.                                                                                                                                             | Mar 2020           | Jan 2023         |
| LEV10 Definitions and application                                         | Reflects revised standardised approach introduced in the December 2017 Basel III publication (including the revised implementation date announced on 27 March 2020) and removal of internal model approaches.                                                                                                                                             | Mar 2020           | Jan 2023         |
| LEV20 Calculation                                                         | Calculation frequency specified for reporting and disclosure purposes. Also takes account of the revised implementation date announced on 27 March 2020.                                                                                                                                                                                                  | Mar 2020           | Jan 2023         |
| LEV30 Exposure management                                                 | Refinements particularly affecting derivatives and off-balance- sheet exposures and a national discretion on the treatment of central bank reserves, as set out in the December 2017 publica- tion of Basel III and the June 2019 publication on client cleared derivatives. Takes account of the revised implementation date announced on 27 March 2020. | Mar 2020           | Jan 2023         |
| LEV40 Leverage ratio requirements for global systemically important banks | First version in the format of the consolidated framework, reflects the requirements introduced in the December 2017 Basel III publication and the revised implementation date announced on 27 March 2020.                                                                                                                                                | Mar 2020           | Jan 2023         |

I had the good fortune to have grown up under several outstanding leaders who had given me a lot of hands-  on experience with people and technically complex missions. I didn't have innate talent, so I surrounded myself with smart people and relied on them to work with me as a team to get the job done. My credo: always hire people who are smarter and better than you are, and learn from them.

-  Gene Kranz, Failure Is Not an Option: Mission Control from Mercury to Apollo 13 and Beyond (New York: Berkley Books, 2000)

P A R T

I

## Bank Asset and Liability Management

P art I was a primer on banking, the elements of which all feed into the essence  of  banking  discipline,  which  is  asset-liability  management (ALM). In Part II we are in a position now to introduce ALM itself.

ALM is an art that is required to be practised at every bank in every country. This is a simple statement of fact. The act of undertaking banking, offering loans and deposits to customers, creates a balance sheet of assets and liabilities that need to be managed effectively, and ideally optimally, in perpetuity. Unlike topics in banking and finance that attract much attention  from  business  media  and  academia  (particularly  academia),  such  as exotic derivatives, options valuation, CDO structuring, credit derivatives, traded market risk hedging, and so on, ALM is universal to every bank in the world. It applies to every bank. Period. So naturally we will spend some time on this topic.

In Part II of the book we review the main strands of the ALM discipline, covering liquidity risk, funding risk, banking book market risk and FX risk,  and  then  look  at  the  role  of  the ALM  Committee  (ALCO)  and ALCO reporting. The ALCO is the most important executive committee in the bank, bar none. Our discussion covers the terms of reference of the ALCO, a look at the main drivers of the ALCO agenda, and the management information (MI) content essential to effective ALCO governance. It also includes a look at the organisation of the Group ALCO (GALCO) function in a multinational or multi-  entity bank.

But we start with a look at the yield curve.

<!-- image -->

The long-term government bond yield is the single most important variable  in  any  market  economy,  setting  the  tone  for  equity  and house prices, governing business investment decisions and establishing the discount rate for pension liabilities and insurance policies.

-Anatole Kaletsky, ''Economic View'', The Times , 22 February 2005

© Bloomberg L.P. All rights reserved. Reproduced with permission.

<!-- image -->

## Abstract

The yield curve is the key determinant in all banking and finance operations, being the baseline driver for all pricing and valuation processes. It is also an important analytical tool in its own right, as its shape and structure at any time provides pointers on market interest rate expectations. The chapter considers the different types of yield curve and the explanations behind their shape, as well as the concept of discount factors and the orthodox approach to constructing the discount function. We address the information content of the curve, and summarise the process for fitting a bank's internal yield curve.

## THE YIELD CURVE

T he art of banking, indeed all of finance, revolves around the yield curve. Understanding and appreciating the curve is important to all financial market  participants.  It  is  important  to  commercial  bankers,  debt  capital market  participants,  institutional  investors,  and  especially  important  to bank ALM practitioners. So if you are reading this book it is safe to assume that the yield curve is a very important subject.

This is a long chapter but well worth the close attention of all bankers, irrespective of their function or seniority. In it, we discuss the main concepts behind the yield curve, as well as its uses and information content. An ability to interpret the yield curve is vital for all market practitioners. We discuss the zero-  coupon (or spot) and forward yield curves, and present the main theories that seek to explain their shape and behaviour. We will see that the spread of different curves to another, such as the swap yield curve compared to the government yield curve, is also noteworthy and so we seek to explain the determinants of these spreads in this chapter.

We begin with an introduction to the curve and interest rates in general.

1  This chapter was co-  authored with Polina Bardaeva, Faculty BTRM and Ioannis Rigopoulos, Faculty BTRM.

## CHAPTER  4

## The Yield Curve 1

## I M P O R TA N C E   O F   T H E   Y I E L D   C U R V E

As we noted in Chapter 1, banks deal in interest rate and credit risk, as well  as  liquidity  risk.  These  are  the  fundamental  tenets  of  banking,  as important today as they were when banking first began. The first of these, interest  rates,  is  in  effect  an  explicit  measure  of  the  cost  of  borrowing money, and is encapsulated in the yield curve. For bankers, understanding the behaviour and properties of the yield curve is an essential part of the risk management process. The following are some, but not all, of the reasons that this is so:

- ■ changes in interest rates have a direct impact on bank revenue; the yield curve captures the current state of term interest rates, and also presents the current market expectation of the future state of the economy;
- ■ the interest-  rate gap reflects the state of bank borrowing and lending; income levels that are a function of gaps along the term structure are sensitive to changes in the shape and slope of the yield curve;
- ■ current and future trading strategy, including the asset allocation and credit policy decision, will impact interest-  rate risk exposure and therefore must take into account the shape and behaviour of the yield curve;
- ■ the  balance  sheet  itself,  from  both  an  asset  sensitivity  and  a  liability sensitivity viewpoint, is sensitive to changes in the shape, level and slope of the yield curve;
- ■ balance  sheet  management  and  valuation  requires  an  accurate  yield curve, reflecting a liquid market.

We see, then, that understanding and appreciating the yield curve is a vital part of banks' balance sheet management operations, at both a strategic and tactical level. This chapter provides a detailed look at the curve from the banker's viewpoint. It is divided into three sections - the first two sections focus on interest rates and interpreting the yield curve and other curves; the third section looks at fitting the curve.

## THE MONEY MARKET AND CAPITAL MARKET YIELD CURVE

Financial market participants often examine the relationship between the yields on money market deposits and bonds that are in the same class; plotting yields of cash flows that differ only in their term to maturity produces what is known as a yield curve . This curve is an important indicator and knowledge  source  of  the  state  of  a  debt  capital  market.  It  is  sometimes referred to as the term structure of interest rates, but strictly speaking the two terms are not synonymous, and the latter term should be reserved for the zero-  coupon yield curve only.

Much of the analysis and pricing activity that takes place in the capital markets revolves around the yield curve. This curve describes the relationship between a particular redemption yield and that yield's maturity. Plotting the yields along the term structure will give us our yield curve. It is very important that only rates from the same class of issuer or with the same degree of liquidity are used when plotting the yield curve; for example, a curve may be constructed for UK gilts or for AA-  rated sterling Eurobonds, but not a mixture of both, because gilts and Eurobonds are bonds from different class issuers. The primary yield curve in any domestic capital market is the government bond yield curve, so for example in the US market it is the US Treasury yield curve. In the eurozone, in theory any euro-  currency government bond can be used to plot a euro yield curve. In practice, only bonds from the same government are used, as for various reasons different country bonds within euro-  land trade at different yields (the de facto euro benchmark is the German bund).

Outside  the  government  bond  markets  yield  curves  are  plotted  for money  market instruments, Eurobonds, swap contracts, futures contracts - in fact, all debt market instruments. Money market instruments trade on a simple yield basis, as the cash market is comprised essentially of bullet interest payment securities. So the money market yield curve is simple to construct. The traditional base for money market yield curves was the 'Libor curve'. In some jurisdictions the reference rate is still based on the 'Libor' concept but the term used today is IBOR. In certain currencies Libor was done away with and replaced with a reference rate based on transactions undertaken in the money market, hence as a concept the 'Libor curve' is now defunct, and instead we have the 'RFR' curve.

We  show  in  Figure  4.1  the  Bloomberg  screen  for  a  broker's  screen (ICAP) for US dollar interest-  rate swaps. This page enables one to interpolate a long-  dated yield curve. A widely used money markets yield curve is the exchange-  traded futures curve for short-  dated deposits; for instance, the Eurodollar curve or the short-  sterling curve. This is taken as the most reliable and liquid indicator of expected money market rates. Figure 4.2 shows the Eurodollar curve as at 30 June 2020.

Figure 4.3 shows the money market curve for USD based on overnight index swap (OIS) rates, going out to one year tenor, as at 28 July 2020.

The  principles  behind  the  money  market  yield  curve  are  exactly  the same as those behind the longer dated bond market yield curve. Later in this chapter we will consider the YTM yield curve and how to derive spot and forward yields from a current redemption yield curve.

FIGURE  4.1 Bloomberg  screen  ICAU2,  ICAP  broker's  price  screen  for  US  dollar interest-  rate swaps, 30 June 2020.

<!-- image -->

FIGURE 4.2 Eurodollar futures yield curve, 30 June 2020.
