## CHAPTER SUMMARY

The organisation of the ALM function and its placing within a bank are important to get right if a bank wishes to optimise its balance sheet structure. The most common operating model has the ALM desk operating as part  of Treasury  or  Finance,  although  for  a  number  of  reasons  a  strong argument can be made for ALM to be a standalone department reporting to the CEO direct (it is called 'Internal Treasury' at some banks, separate from the 'External Treasury').

The Treasury function itself can be set up as a middle-  office or frontoffice  cost  centre  or  profit  centre  respectively.  The  framework  for  both Treasury and ALM department should be appropriate to the bank in question, and its own operating model and objectives. The organisation structure should be reviewed at least once every two years to ensure it is still fit for purpose.

## REFERENCES AND FURTHER READING

- Bardaeva,  P.  (2021), Bank  Asset  Liability  Management  Best  Practice:  Yesterday, Today and Tomorrow , The Moorad Choudhry Global Banking Series, Berlin: De Gruyter.

BCBS (2016), Standard on Interest Rate Risk in the Banking Book , BIS, April. EBA (2018), Guidelines on Internal Governance ,  EBA/GL/2017/11, EBA, 21 March.

<!-- image -->

## ALCO Governance Framework and Terms of Reference

## Abstract

The asset-  liability committee (ALCO) in a bank is the governance forum responsible for managing the bank's balance sheet. As a robust and long-  term viable balance sheet is essential for the survival of the bank in perpetuity, the ALCO itself must be organised such that it has the requisite expertise and authority necessary to fulfil its mission. We review the role of ALCO and provide a good-  practice template for the ALCO governance framework and terms of reference. This is suitable for most commercial banks, irrespective of their jurisdiction.

T he question as to what is the most important management body in a bank  might  be  considered  to  be  an  uncontentious  one.  Most  people would answer, 'The board of directors; after all, the board has the ultimate responsibility for the continued viability and good governance of the bank.' Others might reply, 'The CEO-  chaired executive committee or 'Exco'; it has delegated authority from the Board to run the bank on a day-  to-  day basis, in line with the Board's overall long-  term objectives.' Either answer has merit.

The  author's  reply  to  this  question  is  unequivocal:  the  most  important forum in a bank, in any bank, is its asset-  liability committee (ALCO). Events throughout banking history have shown that the  balance  sheet  is everything : get the balance sheet wrong in any way and the result is always serious, often catastrophic. Most bank failures, again throughout history, can be sourced to getting the capital management and/or the liquidity management wrong. Even multi-  billion dollar fines levied on banks for all kinds of transgressions such as sanctions busting, product mis-  selling or market rigging, have not sunk banks. But a broken balance sheet usually will.

Thus, it is clear. The most important committee in a bank is the one charged with owning the balance sheet, and for managing it such that it is always, at all times, in robust shape and short- and long-  term viable. But isn't the Board of Directors and the Exco responsible for just that? Yes, they are. But the Board will delegate this responsibility, and the Exco will always have a slight bias towards profit and return on capital objectives. A viable balance sheet requires profit and safety. The role of the ALCO is to split its bias evenly, exactly 50-  50, between profit and safety.

We devote an entire chapter to the bank's ALCO. Because many banks around  the  world  still  organise  their ALCO  along  the  lines  of  the  more 'traditional' approach to ALM governance, we begin with a look at how ALCOs were originally structured and operated. The traditional approach is still valid and covers an important area, so being still current it is worth being  aware  of.  We  then  consider  recommended  good-  practice  for  the 'modern day' ALCO. We also look at requirements of ALCO management information (MI) reporting.

## TRADITIONAL ALCO MODEL AND POLICY

The ALCO is responsible for setting and implementing ALM policy. Its composition  varies  in  different  banks  but  usually  includes  the 'C-  suite'  and heads of business lines, plus their deputies. Often there is a representative from the '3rd line of defence' and sometimes the research or economics department, as well as director-  level staff such as the finance director. 1

The ALM process is delegated to the Treasury desk. It is responsible for most MI reporting to the ALCO. The ALCO will consider the report in detail at regular meetings, usually monthly. Traditionally, the main points of interest in the ALCO report include variations in interest income, the areas that experienced fluctuations in income and the latest short-  term income projections. The ALM report will link these three strands across the group entity  and  also  to  each  individual  business  line.  That  is,  it  will  consider macro-  level factors driving variations in interest income as well as specific desk-  level factors. The former includes changes in the shape and level of the yield curve, while the latter will include new business, customer behaviour and so on. Of necessity, the ALM report is a detailed document, but should

be kept as succinct as possible. 2

Table 9.1 is a summary overview of the responsibilities of the ALCO as they were traditionally; that is, as might be observed in the 1980s or the early 21st century. It is essentially a banking ALM overview.

1  The ALCO is known by other acronyms in some banks; for example, Balance

Sheet Risk and Management Committee (BRMC), Asset-Liability Policy Committee (ALPC) or Asset, Liability and Capital Management Committee (ALCMCO) has been observed by the author. However, the most common is ALCO.

2  Even today, brevity as a rule is not always followed. In the old days the author has come across ALCO decks that were over 100 pages long, double-sided A4 in Word, in some cases much over. This is rarer today but 60-70 page slide decks ful,  provided  the  bulk  of  the  pack  is  appendices!  More  on  this  topic  later  in

for the ALCO MI pack are not unusual. This in itself is not necessarily unhelpthe chapter.

TABLE 9.1 Traditional ALCO main mission.

| Mission                          | Components                                                                                                                                                                             |
|----------------------------------|----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| ALCO management and reporting    | FormulatingALM strategy Management reporting ALCO agenda and minutes Assessing liquidity, gap and interest-rate risk reports Scenario planning and analysis Interest income projection |
| Asset management                 | Managing bank liquidity book (CDs, Bills) Managing the government bond and FRN book Investing bank capital                                                                             |
| ALM strategy                     | Yield curve analysis Money market trading                                                                                                                                              |
| Funding and liquidity management | Liquidity policy Managing funding and liquidity risk Ensuring funding diversification Managing lending of funds                                                                        |
| Risk management                  | Formulating hedging policy Interest-rate risk exposure management Implementing hedging policy using cash and derivative instruments                                                    |
| Internal treasury function       | Formulating transfer pricing system and level Funding group entities Calculating the cost of capital                                                                                   |

Like most management committees the ALCO would meet on a regular basis, usually monthly. During stressed or crisis periods it would meet more frequently. The composition of the ALCO varies by institution, but is typically comprised of the CEO, the heads of the business lines, of Treasury, and Risk Management, as well as the finance director. Representatives from the credit committee and loan syndication may also be present. A typical agenda would consider all the elements listed in Table 9.1. Thus the meeting will discuss and generate action points on the following:

- ■ Management  reporting :  this  will  entail  analysing  the  various  management reports and either  signing  off  on  them  or  agreeing  to  items for  actioning. The  issues  to  consider  include  lending  margin,  interest income,  variance  from  last  projection,  customer  business  and  future business. Current business policy with regard to lending and portfolio management will be reviewed and either continued or adjusted.
- ■ Business planning : existing asset (and liability) books will be reviewed, and future business direction drawn up. This will consider the performance of existing business, most importantly with regard to return on

- capital. The existing asset portfolio will be analysed from a risk-  reward perspective, and a decision taken to continue or modify all lines of business. Any proposed new business will be discussed and if accepted in principle will be moved on to the next stage. 3  At this stage any new business will be assessed for projected returns, revenue and risk exposure.
- ■ Hedging policy : overall hedging policy will consider the acceptance of risk exposure, existing risk limits and the use of hedging instruments. The latter also includes use of derivative instruments. Many bank ALM desks find that their hedging requirements can be met using plain vanilla products such as interest-  rate swaps and exchange-  traded short-  money futures contracts. The use of options, and even vanilla instruments such as FRAs, 4  is much less common than one might think. Hedging policy takes into account the cash book revenue level, current market volatility levels and the overall cost of hedging. On occasion, certain exposures may be left unhedged because the cost associated with hedging them is deemed prohibitive (this includes the actual cost of putting on the hedge as well as the opportunity cost associated with expected reduced income from the cash book). Of course, hedging policy is formulated in coordination with overall funding and liquidity policy. Its final form must consider the bank's views of the following:
- ■ expectations on the future level and direction of interest rates;
- ■ balancing the need to manage and control risk exposure with the need to maximise revenue and income;
- ■ the level of risk aversion, and how much risk exposure the bank is willing to accept.

The ALCO is dependent on management reporting from Treasury, Risk, Finance and other departments. The reports are usually produced by the MO. The main report is the overall ALM report, showing the composition of the bank's ALM book. Other reports will look at specific business lines, and will consider the return on capital generated by these businesses. These reports  will  need  to  break  down  aggregate  levels  of  revenue  and  risk  by business line. Reports will also drill down by product type, across business lines. Other reports will consider the gap, the gap risk, the VaR or DV01

3  All new business should follow a formal approval process, typically involving all the relevant front-  , middle- and back-  office departments of the bank, and culminating in a 'New Products Committee' meeting at which the proposed new line of business will be either approved, sent back to the sponsoring department for modification or rejected.

4  See Chapter 6.

FIGURE 9.1 ALCO reporting input and output.

<!-- image -->

(interest-  rate risk) report and credit risk exposures. Overall, the reporting system must be able to isolate revenues,  return  and  risk  by  country  sector,  business  line  and  product  type. There  is  also  an  element  of  scenario planning; that is, expected performance under various specified macro- and micro-  level market conditions.

Figure 9.1 illustrates the traditional reporting concept.

## ALCO Operating Model

It is important that the ALCO function be set up and run with a governance structure and authority that befits its importance. For this reason, its terms of reference (ToR) must be articulated clearly to all bank management. Regular attendance by members of the ALCO must also be stressed. A common point of discussion is the frequency with which the ALCO should meet. At the minimum it should meet once every four weeks, ideally at the same time and on the same day each month. This establishes a pattern and ensures that the meeting is embedded in the firm's risk and management culture. If for any reason a discussion is required ahead of the next scheduled meeting, for example during periods of market stress or because of a firm-  specific issue of urgency, then certainly an extraordinary meeting should be able to be called at short notice.

We present here, still current, an orthodox ALCO ToR that would be consistent  with  the  traditional  approach  to  organising ALCO,  and  some sample agendas.

## ALCO Terms of Reference

In the first edition of this book we presented on the companion website a recommended sample template for an ALCO ToR. This has been replaced with what the author considers a more appropriate, fit for purpose ToR template better suited to the post-  Basel III era. This revised version is certainly appropriate for any commercial bank, large or small. With only minor edits it is also suitable for a multinational or Group entity.

At the risk of sowing confusion, in this section we continue with our description of the 'traditional' ALCO, because this traditional approach is still observed commonly around the world.

The most significant element of ALCO organisation is its membership and reporting line, which reflects its status as a high-  level management committee and key policymaking body. Table 9.2 is an extract from the ToR template that was presented in the first edition. This is the criteria recommended for ALCO membership in a medium-  sized bank. What one observes from Table 9.2 is the seniority as well as the slant of the ALCO, which is ultimately a risk management mechanism. Note the following:

- ■ the committee is chaired by the CFO or Finance Director, or in case of absence, the Head of Treasury, and not by the CEO or any of the business line heads (where the Treasury desk is a profit centre and not a cost centre, the Head of Treasury is less likely to act as chair, but if no alternative is possible, this person must be mindful to not allow any conflict of interest whenever required to act as chair in the CFO's absence);
- ■ the head of each business line must be represented, as must the head of risk management (more often now termed the Chief Risk Officer);
- ■ selected members of staff of relevant departments can also be invited to attend as guests; for example, the head of money markets or ALM (who reports to the head of Treasury). Where this occurs, the ToR must make clear that such persons have voting powers in the absence of their department head. For effective management and decision-  making, it is recommended that deputies be given such authority, so that the bank can function correctly in the absence of key senior individuals.

TABLE 9.2 Traditional ALCO membership and attendees.

## ALCO Membership

## Members

CEO or Deputy CEO

Chief Financial Officer (Chair)

Head of Treasury (Deputy Chair)

Head of Corporate Banking

Head of Retail Banking

Head of Private Banking

Head of Research or Chief Economist

Chief Risk Officer

## Guests

Head of ALM/Money Markets

Head of Market &amp; Liquidity Risk

Head of Valuation Control/Product Control

Head of Financial Institutions Group

## Secretariat

Treasury Business Manager or Liquidity Risk Team Member

It can be argued that the Head of Credit, or a senior person from the Credit  Committee,  should  also  attend  ALCO,  and  certainly  some  banks do follow such an arrangement. However, credit decisions, including riskrelated issues such as expected losses, forecasts and loss provisions, are better handled at the Executive Credit Committee level, and because the Credit department does not have a day-  to-  day involvement, from either an operational or policy level, with asset-liability issues it obviates the need to have it represented at the ALCO level. That said, the author believes there is considerable merit in their attendance at ALCO. We discuss this further in the section entitled 'The Modern Day ALCO', later in the chapter.

The membership of ALCO should be reasonably stable, but also flexible enough to allow for additional persons and expertise as and when necessary; for example, technical experts by invitation.

The ToR is a formal statement of the primary aims and objective of the ALCO. It should be a succinct document. We observe that its remit covers every aspect of asset, liability, liquidity and capital management of the bank's operations. We show in Figure 9.2 an extract from the original template ToR that was presented in the first edition, which is a detail of the committee's operating agenda. The agenda makes clear that any aspect of the bank's operations that impact ALM issues - which is essentially anything that a bank might undertake - must be addressed, for risk management purposes, at the ALCO level.

- ■ Review gap limits,  actual  gaps,  and  their  sensitivities,  together  with  any recommendations to amend the limits;
- ■ Review  the  liquidity  ratio  and  other  liquidity  metrics,  and  adherence  to regulatory and internal liquidity limits, and assess forecasted values for risk control purposes and adequacy;
- ■ Review and discuss deposit and funding trends, including deposit concentrations, programs/products, deposit promotion campaigns, and forecasts;
- ■ Review exceptions/excesses to internal and regulatory policies and limits as reported by the Head of Financial Planning and Control;
- ■ Review current allocation  of  capital  and  profit  contribution  by  business line,  and  present  regulatory  capital  adequacy  forecasts  and  requests  for Board review;
- ■ Review  the  market  environment  and  potential  impact  on  the  branch's interest-  rate risk and trading activities;
- ■ Review and approve authorised instruments and permissible hedging and position-  taking strategies for gap management, trading and customer sales;
- ■ Review and discuss recommendations to change policies, objectives or limits;
- ■ Review internal funds transfer pricing arrangements and consider whether changes are required;
- ■ Set standards and methodology for measuring and monitoring the quantitative limits on all trading activities;
- ■ In liaison with CRO's office, set and review stress testing scenarios;
- ■ Review  new  trading  activities  recommended  under  the  policy  for  New Products Services; and
- ■ Act  as  the  centre  for  excellence  for  all  ALM-  related  policy  and  governance issues.

FIGURE 9.2 Traditional ALCO operating agenda: extract from the formal ToR.

## Agenda Setting

The ALCO agenda is varied and wide-  ranging, and by definition dynamic in line with market and firm-  specific events. On the book's website we include a  sample  of  hypothetical  agendas  from  past ALCO  meetings  at  different banks. Figure 9.3 is an example of one of these agendas, from a bank ALCO meeting. Specific items must be the responsibility of named individuals, who will  present  in  accordance  with  the  agenda.  The  agenda  and  supporting documents should be circulated at least one week before the meeting date, to enable members to have sufficient time to review the contents. Where this is  not  possible,  for  example for late items or for extraordinary meetings, the emphasis should be on making the meeting documents available to the circulation list as soon as possible.

## ALCO AGENDA SAMPLE

1. Minutes of previous ALCO
2. MI Pack
3. State of the markets
4. Year-  end forecast and budget update
5. Year-  end asset and capital limits
6. Capital stress testing
7. Revised approval process, capital allocation
8. New business: Asia-  Pacific capital injection request
9. Conduit business organisation review
10. Internal funds pricing for undrawn liquidity lines
11. Risk-  weighted assets limit transfer
12. ICAAP update
13. Intra-  group limits: regulatory update
14. Revised Funds Transfer Pricing policy
15. Term funding strategy

FIGURE 9.3 ALCO agenda sample.

Items for inclusion at the next meeting should be discussed, informally or formally, with the chairperson before then being sent to the committee secretary for circulation on the agenda.

## The Group ALCO

Larger banks that operate across national boundaries and/or legal entities and subsidiaries will need to organise their governance on the basis of a central Group function and outlying regional and/or legal entity functions. This applies most crucially to the ALCO function. In Chapter 8 we presented best practice for a Group ALCO (GALCO) organisation structure in a multinational and/or multi-  entity bank.

## GALCO Agenda Setting

The agenda items for GALCO are conceptually identical to what we discussed earlier for the ALCO agenda. However, much of the operating level business will be discussed at the local ALCO level and, where they are set up, regional ALCO level. This means that much GALCO business concerns Group policy issues, or high-  level strategic decision-  making issues. A sample GALCO agenda is shown in Figure 9.4.

## For attention of GALCO

CEO

Deputy CEO

Head of Corporate Banking

Head of Portfolio Management

Head of Investment Banking

Head of Money Markets

Finance Director

Group Risk Officer

Group Head of Treasury

Deputy Group Head of Treasury

Subject

Group ALCO Agenda

## Minutes/Actions:

1. Minutes of previous GALCO
2. MI Pack
3. Macroeconomics update

## Approval items:

4. Year-end forecast and budget update
5. Year-end asset and capital limits
6. Capital stress testing
7. Revised approval process, capital allocation
8. New business: MENA capital injection request
9. Private bank business organisation review
10. Internal funds pricing for liquidity lines

## Updated items:

11. Risk-weighted assets limit transfer
12. Intra-group limits: regulatory update
13. Revised Funds Transfer Pricing policy
14. Term funding strategy

FIGURE 9.4 Sample GALCO agenda.

## EXAMPLE 9.1 ALCO BEST-  PRACTICE GUIDELINES: THE UK REGULATOR

The ALCO is the most important senior management and risk committee in a bank. It must always be run as a value-  added committee, which means it needs to have the correct composition, review up-  todate  management  information  that  reflects  accurately  the  balance sheet  position  and  risk  exposures  of  the  bank  and  be  the  centre  of robust discussion and debate. Policies and recommendations approved by ALCO will have a considerable influence on the bank's condition and future state, so it is important that all banks seek to manage their ALCO on best-  practice lines.

Having  previously  written  about  the  importance  of  the  ALCO in  his  book Bank  Asset-  Liability  Management (Choudhry,  2007), the author was particularly pleased when the UK Financial Services Authority (FSA) opined on ALCO in a publication issued in January 2011. 5   It  presented  good  practice  guidelines  that  all ALCOs should seek to adhere to, in the following areas:

- ■ the role of ALCO;
- ■ the composition and authority of ALCO;
- ■ the forward-  looking nature of the ALCO discussion and the decisions it makes;
- ■ the degree of senior management challenge during meetings, and evidence of this in the approved ALCO minutes;
- ■ regular attendance from all members.

We list the FSA's recommended guidelines here.

( continued )

5  Financial Services Authority, Finalised Guidance: Asset and Liability Management ,  January 2011. This was a 'Dear CEO' letter issued by the FSA. When the Prudential Regulatory Authority (PRA) was created in 2013 and took over from the FSA, it reissued this letter as a Supervisory Statement, SS1/13, in April 2013. There was no change to the content, but a SS carries formal weight and legislative authority, technically (although practically perhaps not) stronger than a Dear CEO letter.

## Role of the Committee

- ■ Proactively controls the business in line with the bank's strategy and objectives, and focuses on the entire balance sheet.
- ■ Ensures that risks remain within the stated risk appetite.
- ■ Considers the impact on earnings volatility of changing economic and market conditions.
- ■ Ensures that an appropriate internal funds pricing mechanism is in place that correctly charges for the cost of liquidity, incentivises the desired behaviour and is in line with the bank's strategic objectives and risk appetite.
- ■ Acts as the arbitrator in the debate and challenge process between business lines.

## Committee Membership

- ■ Attended by the CEO or Deputy CEO, and chaired by the CFO;
- ■ Includes all business group heads, the CRO, the Head of Research/ Economics and the Head of Internal Audit.

## Nature of Discussion

- ■ Is  forward-  looking in nature, focusing on the impact of future plans and strategy at the business line level;
- ■ Takes  proactive  decisions  to  manage  ALM  risks,  act  to  solve issues raised or otherwise escalating to the Executive Committee/ Board rather than simply noting or observing the risks;
- ■ Ensures issues are fully articulated and debated;
- ■ Considers recommendations from a tactical sub-  committee that excludes the CEO and other ExCo members. Where appropriate, delegates decision-  making authority to an ALCO sub-  committee;
- ■ Ensures an active dialogue and debate among committee members, and shows evidence of a strong degree of challenge;
- ■ Provides  minutes  summarising  the  extent  of  discussion  and debate, and do not only record action points.

## Management Information (the ALCO 'Pack')

- ■ Is content-  focused on future plans and strategy;
- ■ Presents  market  and  economic  outlook,  together  with  impact assessment on ALM issues pertinent to the bank;

- ■ Shows liquidity and funding metrics by currency; also provides a forecast of metrics based on current market expectations;
- ■ Provides results of stress tests under specified stress conditions;
- ■ Provides  analysis  of  interest-  rate  risk  using  modified  duration and VaR methodologies, NII/NIM sensitivity and basis risk, as part of an assessment of earnings volatility;
- ■ Reports its current funding composition, and assesses potential refinancing risk stress points, based on its funding maturities, its market funding position and the position of the market generally;
- ■ Presents liquidity stress testing scenarios of varying severity;
- ■ Presents the Contingency Funding Plan (CFP) for the bank, and regularly updates it;
- ■ Provides the required level of granularity and invites challenge from members.

The author endorses these recommendations, particularly the ones on regular attendance and management challenge.

A recommended ALCO pack template is provided on the website that accompanies this book.

## THE MODERN-  DAY ALCO

The bank crash of 2007-2008 had significant impact on every aspect of banking and finance. Possibly the most significant was the more onerous level  of  regulation  and greater scrutiny by supervisors that resulted. This had a number of effects on banking operations, and it also made the objective of 'balance sheet optimisation' more difficult to achieve, for three main reasons: first, the greater amount and cost of the key resources of capital and liquidity, which acted as a constraint; secondly, more intrusive regulation meant that banks in many jurisdictions have to ask for approval from the  supervisor  before  making  seemingly  minor  changes  to  their  business model and/or product suite; and thirdly, the greater emphasis on customer good conduct and treating customers fairly. Managing the balance sheet to ensure that it is structured as efficiently as possible, optimised so to speak, is more challenging in the post-  Basel III era.

This is the main driver of the need to move towards the more proactive  approach to ALM, the 'Strategic ALM' discipline that we discussed in Chapter 5. And the key ingredient in making strategic ALM a reality is the position and authority of the ALCO. This is why we have revised our ALCO ToR template and its position within the governance framework, so that (i) the balance sheet is owned and managed by an executive committee that has the requisite authority to ensure it is always robust and long-  term viable, and (ii) the process of implementing a strategic ALM discipline is made possible.

The recommended template ALCO ToR is included in the Chapter 9 folder in the book's companion website (see Chapter 25 for access details). An extract is shown in Figure 9.5.

## ALCO primus inter pares

More than one Board and executive committee has some kind of responsibility for oversight of a bank's balance sheet risk. Generally these would include:

- ■ Board itself;
- ■ Board risk sub-  committee;
- ■ Executive committee (CEO chaired);
- ■ Risk management committee (CRO chaired);
- ■ Credit risk committee;
- ■ ALCO.

The executive committee that is most closely concerned with balance sheet risk on a strategic and integrated basis (both sides of the balance sheet and all aspects of risk) is the asset-liability committee. More significantly, it has the bandwidth to undertake this role most effectively because that is all it does: it owns balance sheet risk and nothing else. All the other committees noted here have other facets of the bank's operations to consider (with the exception of the credit committee, which has a narrower focus on credit risk only). Thus, from the Board's perspective the most effective way to ensure above-  satisfactory and effective governance, stewardship and oversight of the  balance  sheet  is  to  provide ALCO  with  requisite  delegated  authority from the Board to enable it to carry out its mission.

For this reason, the recommend governance framework for the ALCO is that shown in Figure 9.6. There are some significant differences in this operating model compared to what was presented in the first edition of this book (it was Figure 18.5 in the first edition). An alternative structure is shown in Figure 9.7, more appropriate for banks that operate in jurisdictions that exhibit separate Board Audit and Board Risk committees.

FIGURE 9.5 Extract from author's recommended ALCO ToR.

<!-- image -->

| Chair             | Chief Financial Officer In the absence of the Chief Financial Officer, the Head of Treasury, as Deputy Chair, will act as the Chair.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                           |
|-------------------|----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| Members           | Chief Financial Officer Chief Executive Officer Chief Risk Officer Head of Treasury [For GROUP entities] Group Head of Balance Sheet Management, or alternate Head of Corporate Banking Head of Retail Banking Head of Private Banking Head of Wholesale Banking Head of Strategy [ may be attendee ]                                                                                                          |
| Attendees         | MDProducts &Marketing Head of ALM / Money Markets Head of Liquidity Risk Head of Valuation Control / Product Control Head of Financial Accounting Head of Deposits Head of Lending Head of Credit Deputy CRO Head of Model Risk Chief Economist / Head of Research Chief Operating Officer and/or Chief Technology Officer Head of Internal Audit or appointed Third Line representative Board Independent NED |
| Authority         | ALCO operates under Board-delegated authority and reports direct to the Board.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                 |
| Deputies          | If a Member is unable to attend a meeting, he/she shall appoint a deputy to attend on his/her behalf. Such deputy's attendance shall count towards the quorum and the deputy shall hold the right to vote.                                                                                                                                                                                                     |
| Quorum            | Three members, at least one of whom shall be the Chair or the Deputy Chair. If the meeting is quorate but there is no representative from the 2 nd Line of Defence, the CRO will be informed of decisions made at ALCO on meeting close.                                                                                                                                                                       |
| Meeting Frequency | Monthly and ad hoc as required by any member. Ad hoc meetings are permitted to take place via email or conference call if necessary. Rules regarding decision making and quorum remain the same as for face to face meetings.                                                                                                                                                                                  |
| Voting protocol   | Items presented for approval may be approved by simple majority vote in favour.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                |
| Secretary         | Provided by Treasury.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                          |

FIGURE 9.7 ALCO governance framework for jurisdictions with split Board Audit Committee and Risk Committees.

The  main  changes  from  the  author's  recommendations  10  years earlier are:

- ■ ALCO is now pari passu , to use the lawyer's expression, with the Executive Committee. This ensures that balance sheet resilience is given equal parity, but no greater, with profit and return on capital. It now reports direct to the Board, as the ExCo does.
- ■ To ensure that ALCO is able to exercise its oversight responsibilities over the whole balance sheet, the Credit Committee is assigned a 'dotted line' reporting line to ALCO. It is not a sub-  committee of ALCO, but this dotted line ensures that policies the Credit Committee observes such as credit concentration, overseas lending, foreign currency lending, limits on lending exposure, and so on - and which have major balance sheet impact are reviewed and challenged at ALCO.

FIGURE 9.8 Modern-  day ALCO membership and attendees.

<!-- image -->

| Members   | ● Chief Financial Officer ● Chief Executive Officer ● Chief Risk Officer ● Head of Treasury ● [For GROUP entities] Group Head of Balance Sheet Management, or alternate ● Head of Corporate Banking ● Head of Retail Banking ● Head of Private Banking ● Head of Wholesale Banking ● Head of Strategy [ may be attendee ]                                                                                                                 |
|-----------|-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| Attendees | ● MDProducts &Marketing ● Head ofALM / Money Markets ● Head of Liquidity Risk ● Head of Valuation Control / Product Control ● Head of Financial Accounting ● Head of Deposits ● Head of Lending ● Head of Credit ● Deputy CRO ● Head of Model Risk ● Chief Economist / Head of Research ● Chief Operating Officer and/or Chief Technology Officer ● Head of Internal Audit or appointed Third Line representative ● Board Independent NED |

The membership and attendee list of ALCO must be set to enable it to fulfil its mission. The recommended membership and attendee list is given in Figure 9.8.

## ALCO Main Mission

We summarise the role, objectives and responsibilities of ALCO as follows:

- ■ ALCO is a committee with Board-  delegated authority (or alternatively, operating as a sub-  committee of the Board, in which case the chair is a Board non-  executive director and not the CFO), responsible for management of the bank's balance sheet with respect to all aspects of balance sheet risk, regulatory capital requirement and liquidity.

The main responsibilities of the ALCO are:

- ■ approves policy and direction with respect to all aspects of balance sheet management;
- ■ provides strategy and direction with respect to balance sheet structure and shape;
- ■ has oversight of bank credit risk policy;
- ■ is the approval authority for new products and processes (delegated to new products committee);
- ■ guides the management of the bank's liquid asset buffer;
- ■ ensuring the bank is compliant with all regulatory requirements on liquidity and capital.

Ultimately the ALCO mission can be summarised as:

The asset-  liability committee is mandated by executive authority to act as the primary risk committee responsible for balance sheet risk management.

The ToR on this book's website contains the full detail.

Readers would have noted a number of sub-  committees incorporated into  the  organisation  charts  we  presented. These  should  be  set  up  so  as to  make the work of ALCO more effective and efficient, where they are deemed appropriate to the size, balance sheet structure and business model of the bank. The sub-  committees may include:

- ■ Balance sheet management committee;
- ■ Product pricing committee;
- ■ Liquidity and funding group;
- ■ ICLAAP committee.

Terms  of  reference  for  two  of  these  committees  are  included  in  the ALCO lecture slides on the companion website. The 'ICLAAP' committee, which  the  author  recommends  that  banks  set  up,  is  so-  called  because  it serves as the primary forum for guiding the capital adequacy and liquidity adequacy submissions processes to the regulatory authority. 6  We illustrate this in Figure 9.9.

6  The ICAAP and ILAAP (and Recovery Planning) processes and practice is discussed in Chapter 20.

<!-- image -->

## Members/attendees of the ICLAAP Committee:

Deputy CRO

Treasurer or Head of ALM

Balance Sheet Management (Finance)

Business line representative(s)

Research / Economics (scenario drafting and assumptions)

## Function:

Review and approve for recommendation the scenario storyboards and stress tests

Review and approve for recommendation the ICAAP and ILAAP simultaneously (expected

to be separate documents for the time being)

FIGURE 9.9 'ICLAAP' sub-  committee of ALCO.

## ALCO REPORTING

As befits its importance as the key bank risk committee, ALCO reporting needs to be transparent, accessible, succinct and still tell the entire story of the balance sheet at the right level of granularity.

In  Chapter  5  we  showed  an  example  of  a  macro-  level ALM  report. The ALCO will be concerned primarily with macro-  level gap and liquidity reports compiled for product and market. A variation of this report that will  be  examined  by  the ALCO  is  a  breakdown  by  income  and  margin, again separated into business lines or markets as required. In a pure commercial banking operation the revenue-  type mix will comprise the following (among others):

- ■ the  bid-offer  spread  between  borrowing  and  lending  in  the  interbank market;
- ■ corporate  lending  margin;  that  is,  the  loan  rate  over  and  above  the bank's cost of funds;
- ■ trading income;
- ■ fixed fees charged for services rendered.

The ALCO will receive an income breakdown report, split by business line. The x -  axis in such a report would show the margin level for each time period;  that  is,  it  shows  the  margin  of  the  lending  rate  over  the  cost  of funds by each time bucket. Figure 9.10 is another type of income report, which shows the volumes and income spread by a business line. The spread is shown in basis points and is an average for that time bucket (across all loans and deposits for that bucket). The volumes will be those reported in the main ALM report, but this time with the margin contribution per time period. As we might expect, the spread levels per product across time are roughly similar. They will differ more markedly by product time. The latter report is shown in Figure 9.11, which is more useful because it shows the performance of each business line. In general, the ALCO will prefer low volumes and high margin as a combination, because lower volumes consume less capital. However, some significant high-  volume business (such as interbank money market operations) operates at relatively low margin.

FIGURE 9.10 Asset profile volume and average income spread.

<!-- image -->

The income and return reports viewed by ALCO requires that it checks if  bank policy with regard to lending and money market trading is being adhered to. Essentially, these reports are providing information on the riskreturn profile of the bank. The ideal combination is the lowest possible risk for the highest possible return, although of course low-  risk business carries the lowest return. The level of trade-  off that the bank is comfortable with is what the ALCO will set in its direction and strategy. With regard to volumes and bank business, it might be thought that the optimum mix is high volume mixed with a high-  income margin. However, high-  volume business consumes the most capital, so there will be another trade-  off with regard to use of capital.

FIGURE 9.11 Business lines and average income spread.

<!-- image -->

## Additional MI Content of the Regional ALCO Pack

A bank operating in a multinational environment, in which the overseas entities  are  subsidiaries  or  associated  legal  entities  rather  than  branches, should organise its governance via regional or legal entity ALCOs. There is then a Group ALCO (GALCO) that reviews matters at Group level. Care should be taken that the governance structure is not diluted or duplicated; either a regional or legal entity ALCO arrangement is sufficient, but there is little need for both. Business best-  practice is to incorporate the legal entities that are in one defined customer region (such as MENA, GCC, Asia-  Pacific and so on) into their own regional ALCO. Of course, if a particular legal entity  in  an  overseas  region  is  a  sufficiently  large  business  concern,  then it may also have its own ALCO. There is no one definitive model, but the emphasis should be on ensuring appropriate management control, and the ability for GALCO to be abreast of all necessary MI for the overseas entities.

We illustrate the format of the inputs to a Regional ALCO here.

Over and above the standard constituents of the ALCO pack that we describe elsewhere in this chapter, we would expect to see the additional metrics discussed here. This is for a hypothetical multinational bank with its head office in Europe, but with a substantial presence in the Asia-  Pacific region. Its reporting currency is in euros, so the aggregate regional statistics are all converted into euros.

Table 9.3 is a summary of the liquid asset buffers held in each legal entity or region. These are assets that would be available in a liquidity stress event.

Figure 9.12A is a regional structural liquidity and funding metrics summary. This combines all the regional entities, both branch and subsidiary. Figure  9.12A  shows  the  funding  gap  as  the  difference  between  the  total assets and liabilities for the region, by maturity bucket (in other words, a standard ALM gap chart, but combining a number of operating entities). Figure 9.12B is the chart of total additional funding required for the region, and shows how much funding needs to be raised by maturity bucket. The first chart also shows the extent of the reliance on parent funding. This may be a focus for attention if the home and/or local regulator imposes limits on how much parent funding is allowed, or if it decides that the local entity must be funding 'self-  sufficient'. This MI is very important for banks looking to reduce overseas operations' reliance on parent funding.

Table  9.4  is  the  monthly  liquidity  metrics  monitoring  report  for  the region. The exposure to regional currencies is shown in Table 9.5.

## ALCO MI Pack: The Exam Questions

In the companion website we present a template ALCO MI pack in PowerPoint format. The structure and layout of this pack is appropriate for most commercial banks. It was designed by the author during his days as a chair of ALCO at a UK bank.

The ALCO monthly reporting pack must be able to answer 'Yes' to the following questions:

- ■ Is it succinct, accessible, and easy to read?
- ■ Is it easy to understand?

- ■ Is its main message clear on first viewing?
- ■ Does it give, within the first 10-12 slides (or pages), the latest and most complete balance sheet information in a way that enables the reader to ascertain quickly what the current balance sheet risks are? And what the expected balance sheet risks might be?
- ■ 'Does it help ALCO do its job?' 7
- ■ Does it help the Board do its job?
- ■ Is  it  accessible  enough  so  that  ALCO  members  actually  read  it  and understand it?
- ■ Is it clear and understandable to attendees as well as members, some of whom may not have a technical background in finance and risk issues?
- ■ Is  every item within it completely relevant and completely necessary? (Or, put another way, is it completely bereft of spurious or surplus-  torequirement content?)
- ■ Are  the  summary  risk  metrics  still  fit-  for-  purpose?  (And,  are  they updated regularly so as to remain relevant to the bank business model and its current and expected environment?)

It is worth reviewing the format, layout, appearance and structure of the ALCO pack at least once a year to ensure that it can still reply 'Yes' to these questions.

We show some examples of the kind of high-  level MI that is appropriate for the first few pages of an ALCO pack in Figures 9.13 to 9.18. 8

To be fair, given the pack was over 100 pages of double-  sided A4 (it was a Word file, not PowerPoint), one has to admit he had a point. The author learnt a lot from that meeting! Some years later the author was pleased to give, and receive back, a cheery 'Hello!' from this individual when passing him on London's Bishopsgate...

## CHAPTER SUMMARY

Operating from the premise that 'the balance sheet is everything' in a bank, the  management committee that is directly responsible for managing the balance sheet assumes significant importance as a governance and risk management forum. The role of the ALCO in a bank is a vital and urgent one, if one is to ensure the bank's long-  term viability.

The key tenets of the ALCO philosophy are:

- ■ ALCO must be attended by those with the requisite technical expertise, experience and ability to exercise sound judgement during stressed market conditions.
- ■ ALCO requires sufficient delegated authority if it is to undertake its role effectively. Ideally this comes from the Board direct.
- ■ An effective sub-  committee structure reporting to ALCO may be necessary in larger banks to enable ALCO to carry out its role efficiently.
- ■ Group  entities  should  ensure  an  adequate  governance  framework to  enable  standardisation  in  the  ALCO  process  across  entities  and jurisdictions.

The minimum requirement for a sound balance sheet risk management process  in  a  bank  is  an  effective  and  fit-  for-  purpose  ALCO  governance framework and structure.

## REFERENCES AND FURTHER READING

- Bardaeva,  P.  (2021), Bank  Asset-  Liability  Management  Best  Practice , Berlin: De Gruyter.
- Choudhry, M. (2007), Bank Asset-  Liability  Management ,  Singapore:  John Wiley &amp; Sons (Asia) Pte. Ltd.
- Choudhry,  M.  (2012), The  Principles  of  Banking ,  1st  edition,  Chichester:  John Wiley &amp; Sons.
- Prudential Regulatory Authority (2013), Asset and Liability Management: Suggestions for Greater Effectiveness , Supervisory Statement, LSS1/13, April.

## CHAPTER  10

## ALM Sundries: Asset Encumbrance, Hedge Accounting, IFRS 9 and EIR 1

## Abstract

Items on a bank's ALCO or Executive Committee agenda in any one month will contain a number of approval or discussion items of a technical nature. The topics in this chapter are relevant to the ALM process and are necessarily specialist in nature. An introduction is given here so that committee members and attendees are aware of them, and may research them further if necessary.

T his chapter is possibly a rather untidy one as it has ended up as a mixed collection of topics that are important for ALCO members and attendees to be aware of (hence it is in Part II) but have not been granted their own chapter, even though all of the topics are important, each warranting in fact their own book. Luckily these subjects are covered in some depth in other works, so the interested reader should research them further using specialist texts. Therefore we only introduce these topics here. That said, we reiterate that anyone attending the ExCo or ALCO would wish to have a general understanding of these subjects.

It's a bit of a pic'n'mix as the topics are not necessarily directly related. We discuss the nature and risks of asset encumbrance, how it is generated, and discuss future developments on this area as well as key implications for asset and liability management. We also consider a number of accounting related concepts, namely effective intertest rate, hedge accounting and the rules of the IFRS9 standards.

## ASSET ENCUMBRANCE

Asset encumbrance refers to the existence of bank assets subject to 'financial collateral arrangements', giving creditors or guarantors of an institution's  obligations,  temporary  legal  title  or  certain  preferential  rights  over such assets. 2

1    This chapter was co-  authored with Enrique Benito, Faculty BTRM and Natasha Bourne, Faculty BTRM.

2  In Europe, the use of financial assets as security is governed by Directive 2002/47/ EC on financial collateral arrangements and its transposition in member states.

Banks use collateral arrangements, and therefore encumber assets, in the normal course of business. The repo market is a market in secured borrowing,  and  assets  given  up  as  collateral  in  a  repo  become  encumbered. Similarly, by issuing asset-  backed securities or covered bonds, banks are able to reduce their cost of financing and diversify their funding sources, but the transaction again encumbers assets. Typical encumbered assets include cash as well as non-  cash items such as debt and equity securities, letters of credit, or lending exposures including mortgages or auto loans.

There are many different structures under which collateral can be provided. For instance, by way of 'title transfer', the 'posting party' transfers the legal and beneficial interests in the asset to the recipient. Alternatively, the posting party can grant a 'security interest' by pledging and encumbering assets, but retaining the ownership and economic benefits of the collateral. The 'secured party', who acquires the interest, can only obtain full ownership of the collateral under some predetermined events, for example, if the posting party does not repay its obligation or it defaults, allowing the secured party to seize and sell the asset if necessary. These rights and events are set out within the legal documentation of each particular transaction.

Even in the event that ownership is not transferred, it is common for the secured party to acquire rights to re-  pledge or re-  use the collateral for its own obligations, a practice known as 'rehypothecation'. This means that the secured party would become a posting party itself of the initial collateral received, but it will still have to return the same (or equivalent) assets when the original transaction matures. Rehypothecation has substantial benefits for the secured party since it avoids having to find and fund new collateral, and may result in long collateral chains as securities become re-  used several times. However, under many circumstances (for example, posting collateral to the central clearing counterparty (CCP) for OTC derivatives) rehypothecation is allowed only once for the same set of collateral.

Another important feature of encumbrance is 'overcollateralisation', which means that the collateral provided by the posting party is of higher value than the underlying exposure that the collateral is securing. This is usually undertaken by means of a 'haircut' or 'margin ratio', which refers to the proportion that is subtracted from the market value of an asset that is being used as collateral, acting as a form of protection to the secured party against potential decreases in the actual market value of the collateral during the time the lending exposure is live.

## Benefits and Risks of Asset Encumbrance

Let's illustrate the key considerations surrounding asset encumbrance (see Figure  10.1).  We  assume  two  hypothetical  commercial  banks,  Bank  A and Bank B.

Bank A's balance sheet is financed via equity capital, unsecured funding and retail deposits, and secured funding. The bank uses its trading portfolio as collateral (subject to overcollateralisation requirements) to obtain financing from Bank B.

Bank B is mitigating its credit risk and will benefit from an increased recovery rate compared to unsecured creditors should Bank A default (subject to the risk of changes in the value of the collateral). Bank B will also benefit  from  lower  regulatory  capital  requirements  since  the  overall  risk weight of its exposure to Bank A could be lower than an equivalent unsecured exposure due to the credit risk mitigation effect of collateral.

For Bank A, in turn, the arrangement results in a lower cost of funds and a more diversified funding structure.  Providing  collateral  for  certain exposures can in fact be a requirement to access certain markets or products, such as derivatives cleared through exchanges, where collateral may need to be provided to secure potential losses arising from the contracts that the bank enters.

That said, the use of collateral has generated other risks. The haircuts applied to the collateral mean that part of the assets, to which secured creditors have preferential access, are financed by unsecured creditors. It turns out that if Bank A was unable to meet its obligations because it was insolvent, its unsecured creditors and depositors would be in a worse position than they were when no encumbrance was in place. Since unsecured creditors  become 'subordinated' to secured creditors,  this  effect  is  known  as 'structural subordination'. The impact of structural subordination is exacerbated if collateral eligibility requirements result in the best quality assets being  encumbered, reducing the average quality of assets  backing  claims of unsecured creditors and resulting in lower recovery values. In addition, if Bank B reuses the collateral to obtain additional funding by exercising its rehypothecation rights, Bank A faces the possibility that the secured party may be unable to return the posted collateral, or even fail, which is known as 'rehypothecation failure'.

FIGURE 10.1 lllustrating asset encumbrance.

<!-- image -->

The risks associated with encumbering assets are generally aggravated in crisis times. Under a scenario where the value of the collateral decreases significantly, Bank B may request additional collateral from Bank A to cover the market value of the transaction. Higher haircuts are generally applied reflecting the lower value of the collateral, resulting in even higher encumbrance. Bank A may also have to provide additional margin to cover counterparty  credit  risk.  The  overall  additional  encumbrance  required  under stress is known as 'contingent encumbrance'. Given that under stress creditors  generally  prefer  to  lend  on  a  secured  basis  and  against  high  quality collateral, higher levels of contingent encumbrance may result in an adverse feedback loop until the bank runs out of eligible collateral, with no further capacity to issue secured funding and the consequential impact on structural subordination and ultimately secured creditors. These impacts may extend to deposit guarantee schemes, and the taxpayers if the bank had to be bailed out, as evidenced during the financial crisis.

This illustrates how asset encumbrance is not quite like the other ALMrelated risk metrics that are reported in an ALCO pack, because the level of encumbrance only really becomes a serious issue in the event of default. As long as the bank is viable and a going concern, asset encumbrance has less impact. For that reason, the author prefers to think of asset encumbrance as a 'gone concern' risk metric, rather than a 'going concern' one like LCR or loan-  deposit ratio. It also explains why credit rating agencies are particularly concerned with it, because they are providing a rating that is of most interest to unsecured creditors. Such lenders are keen to know how much is left 'in the tank' should their obligor go bust.

## Encumbrance in Banks' Balance Sheets

Encumbrance profiles differ across institutions. Traditional banks encumber most of their assets through issuance of covered bonds or by collateralising securities to obtain secured financing. Banks also engage in derivative activity for hedging, market making or proprietary trading, using cash or noncash assets to meet margin requirements.

Investment banks typically fund their trading activities  through  repo markets  and  thus  rely  heavily  on  secured  funding.  Banks  that  engage  in matched-  book market making activities use collateral received to finance

the cash lent in the first place by means of rehypothecation, but rehypothecation does not only occur on market-  making since any collateral received, recognised or not in the bank's balance sheet, could in principle be rehypothecated or used to meet margin requirements.

We consider below some examples of transactions that may generate asset encumbrance.

## Secured Financing Transactions

Secured  financing  transactions  include  repurchase  agreements,  securities lending and borrowing and other forms of secured lending. Although these transactions may differ in terms of mechanics, accounting treatment and legal definitions, we assume transactions in which cash is exchanged against an amount of securities for a predetermined period of time.

Repurchase agreements are generally carried out under standard legal agreements based on the SIFMA/ICMA Global Master Repurchase Agreement (GMRA). Under the GMRA, initial margin (relative to the purchase price of the repo) and haircuts (relative to the market value of the collateral) may be required in order to adjust the value of the collateral pledged to mitigate counterparty credit risk.

Margin calls requiring the posting of additional collateral can be issued if the market value of the collateral drops enough. Under a stress scenario, market participants are likely to impose higher margin requirements and haircuts and narrow the range of collateral that they are willing to accept. Changes in the quality of the collateral, credit downgrade triggers and other covenants may also result in higher collateral needs and asset encumbrance (contingent  encumbrance). As  illustrated  in  Gorton  and  Metrick  (2012), during 2007-2008 the repo-  haircut index (an average haircut for collateral use in repo transactions across various collateral classes in the USA) rose from virtually 0% to nearly 50% at the peak of the financial crisis in late 2008, and several asset classes became unacceptable for use as collateral.

The majority  of  central  banks  use  repurchase  agreements  to  lend  to financial institutions in their open market operations, but collateral eligibility criteria and the haircuts applied differ significantly. During the financial crisis, most central banks widened their lists of eligible collateral, but the conservative haircuts and valuations applied generally resulted in increased encumbrance.  Overall  encumbrance  has  also  been  aggravated  by  central banks' recent interventions such as the ECB's Long Term Refinancing Operations  (LTRO),  especially  as  banks  in  the  eurozone  peripheral  countries became more reliant on the ECB's funding.

## Derivatives

Encumbrance related to derivative trades generally arises from the need to post initial margin in the form of cash or high-  quality liquid securities, and subsequently variation margin (VM) during the life of the transaction. For centrally cleared trades (trades cleared through a Central Clearing Counterparty or CCP), an amount of initial margin (IM) reflective of the riskiness of the bank itself is collected. These margin requirements are calculated according to methodologies based on particular confidence levels of the estimated distribution of future exposures, and depend in turn on the particular characteristics of the derivative, such as volatility, jump-  to-  default risk, and possible liquidation procedures, among others.

In the case of bilateral OTC trades, the exchange of margin is generally discretionary, but it is not uncommon to exchange margin by annexing a Credit Support Annex (CSA) to the ISDA Master Agreement. The type of collateral used in the bilateral market is predominantly cash, but counterparties also use non-  cash instruments to meet requirements. As counterparties and regulators adopt the new principles and requirements developed by the Basel Committee and IOSCO, and comply with the European Market Infrastructure Regulation (EMIR), requiring the exchange of initial margin, in the form of highly liquid collateral subject to appropriate haircuts, one would expect to see additional encumbrance levels and a subsequent impact on contingent encumbrance.

## Securitisations and Covered Bonds

Both  asset-  backed  securities  (ABS)  and  covered  bonds  are  used  to  raise liquidity and funding from illiquid loans and exposures. To do so, funding notes are issued and sold to investors with income payments derived from a pool of underlying assets, generally in the form of loan exposures.

The  encumbrance  levels  arising  from  these  transactions  will  again depend on the accounting treatment, legal definitions and mechanics of the arrangements. The underlying assets in an ABS are generally pooled and transferred to a special purpose vehicle (SPV), isolated from the originator. There are instances, however, where accounting criteria may result in the recognition of the underlying assets on the balance sheet assets, resulting in additional encumbrance.

Collateralised mortgage debt can be issued in the form of MBS or covered  bonds. As  opposed  to  MBS,  the  mechanics  of  covered  bonds  imply that the underlying pool of assets and issued notes remain on the issuer's balance sheet from origination. The reliance on covered bonds or MBS to fund mortgage lending tends to be influenced by local market practices and legislation. Nordic countries, Germany and Spain have a long tradition of covered bond issuance. In the USA, by contrast, mortgage funding is primarily based on MBS.

In addition, MBS, ABS and covered bonds are generally subject to additional credit enhancement techniques that may result in increased encumbrance, such as over-  collateralisation requirements or collateral accounts.

## Measuring Asset Encumbrance

High levels of asset encumbrance may reduce a bank's ability to obtain additional  funding,  or  require  it  to  pay  more  for  funding,  especially  if  there is limited transparency, given the uncertainty faced by unsecured creditors when pricing unsecured debt. Therefore it is important that ALCO has a clear picture of the bank's encumbrance level.

As is common in finance generally, more than one measure can be used to assess encumbrance levels. These are variations of an 'asset encumbrance ratio', which is calculated as the ratio of encumbered assets to total assets. These types of measures have some drawbacks. Some assets may not be realisable under insolvency, and others, despite being unencumbered, may not be available for encumbrance due to economic or legal restrictions. In addition,  encumbrance ratios generally differ  significantly  based  on  the  bank business model, and some institutions may show naturally high levels of the ratio due to their particular business activity. In addition, these ratios do not provide information on the structural subordination of unsecured creditors resulting from overcollateralisation.

From the liability side, metrics generally focus on evaluating the proportion of secured to unsecured funding. These ratios generally do not reflect the amount of overcollateralisation of secured funding liabilities and ignore encumbrance  arising  from  derivatives  transactions. 3   In  addition,  if  repo funding is a significant part of the balance sheet, liability based measures can overestimate actual encumbrance levels if offsetting reverse repo transactions are not taken into account.

Table 10.1 shows the different asset encumbrance ratios that can be reported for a bank.

## Asset Encumbrance and the Regulatory Response

The Basel III liquidity metrics Liquidity Coverage Ratio (LCR) and Net Stable Funding Ratio (NSFR) favour secured funding and provide preferential

3  As indicated in CGFS (2013), estimating asset encumbrance using this approach may lead to downward biases.

## TABLE 10.1 Asset encumbrance ratios.

Encumbered Assets / Total Assets

Encumbered assets and off-  balance sheet collateral / Total assets and off-  balance sheet collateral

Encumbered Assets / Assets available for encumbrance

Unsecured liabilities / Total unencumbered assets

Unsecured liabilities / Assets available for encumbrance

Unsecured debt excluding retail deposits / Assets available for encumbrance

treatment for both issuance and holdings of covered bonds. The EU's bank resolution framework includes 'bail-  in' powers, which allow regulators to convert unsecured debt to loss-  absorbing capital in case of insolvency, making it more likely that unsecured creditors suffer losses if a large bank fails. The depositor preference rules will also result in lower recovery values for unsecured creditors.

In the EU and UK, banks have become more reliant on central bank funding and the asset purchase programmes that are being implemented as part of the Quantitative Easing (QE) stimulus. As this has raised encumbrance levels, some regulatory authorities have set limits on asset encumbrance, for example, by limiting the combined value of assets that can be used to secure covered bonds. Australia, Canada and Singapore have all set limits on asset encumbrance, and the Netherlands, Norway and the United Kingdom have taken a case-  by-  case approach setting threshold values for covered bond issues and collateral upgrade transactions for individual institutions (Houben and Slingenberg, 2013).

## I m p l i c a t i o n s   f o r   A s s e t -   L i a b i l i t y   M a n a g e m e n t

A bank's ALM policy should include the treatment and appetite for asset encumbrance of the bank. This will include setting encumbrance limits. The policy should address the following issues:

## Definition and identification

- ■ Clarify definitional aspects of asset encumbrance as relevant to the institution's balance sheet and business model.
- ■ Identify the key sources of asset encumbrance as well as encumbered and unencumbered (but encumberable) assets.

## Development and integration

- ■ Develop policies,  procedures  and  controls  to  ensure  that  encumbrance related risks are adequately identified, monitored and managed under both normal and stress conditions.

- ■ Clarify ownership and governing aspects, ensuring that the firm's management and governing bodies are involved in the development and approval processes as needed.
- ■ Identify key encumbrance metrics relevant to the institution's business model, calibrate warm and hot zones and develop Management Information (MI) reports.
- ■ Integrate into the firm's existing risk management framework carefully considering interactions with credit, liquidity and market risk policies and procedures, contingency planning and FTP, collateral management, new product introduction, stress testing, and so on.
- ■ Embed within the firm's risk culture.

## Measurement and monitoring

- ■ Implement a monitoring framework that provides timely information to the management and the relevant management bodies on key aspects of asset encumbrance, such as the level, evolution and types of encumbered and unencumbered assets as well as the firm's key sources of asset encumbrance.
- ■ Undertake  stress  testing,  focusing  on  the  amount,  evolution  and types of contingent encumbrance, and considering the firm's business and funding model as well as developments in funding markets and the macroeconomic situation.

## Active management

- ■ Actively manage the firm's asset encumbrance position in accordance with the established policies and procedures.
- ■ Reassess the validity of the framework.

It is important that adequate governance and controls arrangements are put in place and that the overall framework is integrated within the existing risk management and ALM frameworks. Importantly, data and governance challenges need to be addressed early in order to meet the new requirements, provide consistency between different instances of data provision, and ensure a smooth integration with the firm's liquidity and ALM risk framework.

## HEDGE ACCOUNTING 4

In  earlier  chapters  we  discussed  the  application  of 'hedging'  techniques by banks to minimise risk arising from changes in market factors, thereby minimising the chance of negative impact on the balance sheet. A common tool to use for such hedging is the financial derivative contract. So far so good. There is just one problem with this approach (actually there is more than  one  problem,  although 'complication'  is  a  more  accurate  adjective to use here), and that is that the item being hedged (the loan or deposit) is accounted for under different principles than the item doing the hedging. The loan or deposit is not 'fair valued' in the accounts every day or every month, whereas the derivative is. So an item valued at par throughout is hedged with an item whose value changes every day. Not fun for the daily P&amp;L optics.

4  The illustrations in this section are authored by Juan Ramirez, Faculty BTRM, and are used with permission.

An  accounting  process  known  as  'hedge  accounting'  gets  around this process.

## Process of Hedge Accounting

The objective of hedge accounting is to represent the effect of risk management  activities  appropriately  within  the  financial  statements  through modifying the normal basis for recognition of gains / (losses) of the item giving rise to the risk (hedged item) and the instrument being used to hedge that risk (hedged instrument). Figure 10.2 shows the relevant accounting standards.

Applying the hedge accounting produce will remove the P&amp;L volatility arising from implementing hedges for risk management purposes, as shown

FIGURE 10.2 Relevant IFRS standards.

<!-- image -->

Source: Used with permission from Juan Ramirez, Faculty BTRM.

FIGURE 10.3 Impact of hedge accounting process on P&amp;L volatility. Source: Used with permission from Juan Ramirez, Faculty BTRM.

<!-- image -->

in  Figure  10.3.  To  apply  the  process,  certain  criteria  must  be  followed, which include:

- ■ Formal designation and policy documentation to be in place at the time of hedge inception, describing:
- ■ risk management objective and strategy;
- ■ hedging instrument;
- ■ hedged item;
- ■ nature of risk being hedged;
- ■ hedge effectiveness measurement methodology.
- ■ The hedge relationship must consist of only eligible hedging instruments and eligible hedged items.
- ■ Hedge effectiveness requirements, including that:
- ■ effectiveness can be reliably measured;
- ■ the hedge is expected to be highly effective (prospective effectiveness);
- ■ retrospective effectiveness testing must be performed on a regular basis and is determined to be highly effective, within an 80-125% range.

Without these in place the external auditor will not be able to sign off on the hedge accounting process.

There  is  a  concept  of 'micro  hedge  accounting'  and 'macro  hedge accounting', as illustrated in Figure 10.4. Within this, there are three types of hedges, as shown in Figure 10.5. Figure 10.6 summarises the contents of hedge accounting documentation.

For more detail on this process readers should review the slide deck on hedge accounting that is included on the companion website.
