## SCENARIO ANALYSIS: ECONOMIC DOWNTURN STRESS TEST REPORT

As part of its stress testing a bank will undertake periodic stress testing that considers the impact of a major economic downturn. An example of such a stress test report is included on the companion website, and is a template that can be adapted for use at a commercial bank.

The purpose of such a stress test is to assess the bank's likelihood of surviving as a going concern in the event of a severe economic downturn. The object of the test is to determine the impact of the downturn on three aspects of the bank's performance:

- ■ the profit and loss position;
- ■ the regulatory capital position;
- ■ the liquidity position.

FIGURE 20.3 Example of stress test results, yield curve sensitivities.

<!-- image -->

If the results of the report suggest it, action can be taken to alter the profile and sensitivity of the balance sheet to mitigate the impact of any crash. This  may  take  the  form  of  raising  additional  capital  and/or  contingency funding arrangements, or hedges to lower asset exposure.

In the template report provided on the website, two iterations of the stress scenario are tested and the results reported. The executive summary of

## Economic Downturn: Executive Summary

This document sets out the parameters, methods and results used in ascertaining a bank's ability to withstand a severe economic downturn in terms of effect on capital, P&amp;L and liquidity.

The risks that have been stressed include interest-  rate risk, FX risk, credit risk and liquidity risk. Operational risk has not been stressed - we assume the bank has a standardised approach and a small loss history with infrequent occurrences of relatively small size. Other risks - for example, legal, pension, reputational and so on - have not been stressed as these have been insignificant historically.

This macroeconomic scenario has been run in two iterations with slightly varying assumptions. The results of the stress scenarios are as follows:

|                                                     | Unstressed    | Iteration 1   | Iteration 2   |
|-----------------------------------------------------|---------------|---------------|---------------|
| Effect on P&L                                       |               |               |               |
| Market risk                                         |               | -2,547,621    | -61,838,086   |
| Credit risk                                         |               | -45,013,826   | -60,018,435   |
| Total                                               |               | -47,561,447   | -121,856,521  |
| Effect on capital                                   |               |               |               |
| Risk-weighted assets                                | 3,281,699,355 | 3,236,599,547 | 3,193,699,910 |
| Capital                                             | 589,900,000   | 537,976,090   | 468,043,479   |
| Capital adequacy ratio                              | 17.98%        | 16.62%        | 14.66%        |
| Effect on liquidity                                 |               |               |               |
| Liquidity ratio: sight                              | 21.83%        | 19.41%        | 18.58%        |
| Regulatory liq. ratio: sight to 8 days (limit 0%)   | 13.38%        | 11.05%        | 10.36%        |
| Regulatory liq. ratio: sight to 1 month (limit -5%) | 4.93%         | 2.69%         | 2.14%         |

FIGURE 20.4 Economic downturn stress test report: Executive summary.

The results suggest that:

- ■ Losses appear most sensitive to changes in bond spreads, while losses due to interest-  rate and FX rate changes are more modest.
- ■ The bank is able to meet the minimum regulatory capital adequacy ratio of 15% without any management action in the unadjusted stress scenario (iteration 1), but NOT in the adjusted stress scenario (iteration 2).
- ■ The bank would not need to seek additional funding in order to meet regulatory liquidity ratios. (In both iterations the impact of stress on liquidity did not result in breach of regulatory ratios, but in all cases the ratio deteriorated significantly.)

Thus it would appear that the bank is well placed to survive a severe economic downturn, but that some preparatory capital planning is necessary to ensure that the regulatory capital adequacy ratio can withstand large mark-  to-  market losses. In addition, the internal liquidity limits should reflect the sharp deterioration in liquidity possible in a downturn. This should be noted when compiling the Contingency Funding Plan.

FIGURE 20.4 ( continued )

this report is shown in Figure 20.4. We see from that in the second iteration of the stress test, the impact on the bank is such that its regulatory capital adequacy ratio falls below (for this bank) the required level of 15%. It is the Board's decision as to whether further capital should be raised to mitigate this risk impact. The experience of the crash of 2007-2009 has shown that for a bank to maintain the confidence of the market and remain as a going concern, it is important that capital levels remain above minimum regulatory requirements, even after the worst effects of capital losses have been felt. The conclusion from this stress test, then, is that the bank should increase its capital reserves, either via a straight equity capital raising or by issuing contingent capital debt.

## LI Q U I D I T Y   S T R E S S   T E S T   R E P O R T S

The purpose of liquidity stress testing is to ascertain the extent of funding difficulties for the bank in the event of idiosyncratic or market-  wide stress. Stress test output results should help senior management to understand the liquidity position of the bank, enabling them to take mitigating action if deemed necessary.

The primary stress  test  output  is  the  cash  flow  survival  report. This was shown earlier in the chapter; in Figure 20.5 we illustrate an example of a report under BAU conditions and one after the mitigating actions have been taken (such as liquidating securities, and accessing contingency funding sources).

The second chart shows the stressed cumulative cash flow forecast, taking  into  account  the  immediate  sale  or  repo  of  marketable  securities.  In this  example,  the  standard  regulator-  specified  stresses  have  been  applied: wholesale funding, retail liquidity, intra-  day liquidity (3- and 5-  day stresses), cross-  currency  liquidity,  intra-  group  liquidity,  off-  balance  sheet  liquidity,  marketable  assets,  non-  marketable  assets,  and  funding  concentration. Observed  behavioural  forecasting  (OBF),  which  refers  to  the  treatment of liabilities for 'stickiness', has been applied in the following way: noninterest-  bearing liabilities (100% every 3 days), current accounts (-5.66% every 36 days), retail time deposits (-0.41% every 344 days), capital (100% every 3 days), cash and equivalents (100% every 3 days), fiscal liabilities (100% every 3 days), repo liabilities (in this example there are no repos), other time deposits (-0.03% every 47 days) and foreign exchange (100% every 30 days). No OBF has been applied to corporate deposits, government time deposits, intra-  group time deposits and inter-  bank time deposits.

In this example, the survival period has been extended from 49 days to 93 days after taking into account the impact of the stress.

For senior management, a one-  slide summary of this test result is illustrated in Figure 20.6.

<!-- image -->

<!-- image -->

FIGURE 20.6 Senior management summary report, one-  slide PowerPoint.

<!-- image -->

A line-  by-  line stress test result report should be produced on a quarterly basis, or as required by the regulator. We provide an example on the companion website. This shows the results of individual shocks on the liquidity ratio, and the probability of each result occurring. The following categories are included:

- ■ reduction in liquid assets;
- ■ decrease in liabilities;
- ■ FX mismatch;
- ■ combined shocks.

This report would be produced as part of routine stress testing, undertaken either by Treasury or Risk Management.

## I N T E R N A L   C A P I TA L   A D E Q U A C Y   A S S E S S M E N T PROCESS (ICAAP)

Under the Basel rules on capital adequacy and EU Capital Requirements Regulation and Directives, banks are required to undertake a regular assessment of the amounts, types and distribution of capital that they consider adequate to cover the level and nature of risks they are exposed to.

This assessment is called the ICAAP and should cover the major sources of risk to a firm's ability to meet its liabilities as they fall due and incorporate both stress testing and scenario analysis. The ICAAP should be the responsibility of the governing body, form an integral part of the management process and decision-  making culture and should be updated at least annually.

We consider each of the steps in the ICAAP process in turn.

## I C A A P   E n d -   t o -   End Process

Figure 20.7 illustrates a generic ICAAP process for banks operating in the UK and regulated by the Prudential Regulation Authority (PRA).

## Balance Sheet Start Date

In large organisations, in particular, it is important to agree upon a balance sheet start date, which will be used as a basis for all modelling activity. As the ICAAP can take a number of months to complete, there is a risk that different business units will use varying dates, which could lead to a degree of inconsistency in the overall capital assessment.

Time

FIGURE 20.7 Indicative ICAAP end-  to-  end process.

<!-- image -->

## Assessment of Material Risks

The first step is to identify all of the risks facing a bank. This will be part of the risk management framework (see Chapter 18) and known as the Risk Map or Risk Register. An example is shown in Figure 20.8. As we noted in the earlier chapter, it will list all material risks facing the organisation and how they are measured, managed and mitigated. Those risks deemed to be immaterial still need to be documented with reasons explaining why this is the case.

Key to this process is the Risk Appetite Statement, a Board level document quantifying the level of a particular risk that an organisation is willing to take. Typically, the Risk Appetite is expressed in terms of a risk limit, but under the ICAAP, it is useful for this to be quantified in terms of capital/ earnings volatility as well.

## Selection of Stress Scenarios

In the UK the PRA publishes a macroeconomic scenario for firms to use in their ICAAP modelling.

The key practical benefits of this approach are:

- ■ It  helps  banks  to  conduct  stress  tests  of  appropriate  severity.  Left  to their own devices, they tend to underestimate the probability of adverse outcomes and the potential crystallisation of 'tail risks'.
- ■ It enables bench-  marking of outputs across the industry.
- ■ It helps to promote senior management engagement in the stress testing process.

FIGURE 20.8 Risk  Register  extract.

| Risk                          | Definition                                                                                                    | Indicative Appetite Statement                                                                                                 |
|-------------------------------|---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| Credit Risk: Customer Lending | Unexpected losses from borrowers failing to meet their obligations as they fall due                           | X% of customer lending secured by land/ buildings                                                                             |
| Credit Risk: Treasury         | Unexpected losses from Treasury counterparties failing to meet their obligations as they fall due             | All counterparties must be rated at XX or above                                                                               |
| Concentration Risk            | Risk of loss from over exposure to a particular group of counterparties                                       | Customer lending caps to sectors                                                                                              |
| Liquidity Risk                | The bank is unable to meet its deposit liabilities as and when they fall due                                  | Maintain sufficient liquid assets to satisfy an idiosyncratic stress for 90 days                                              |
| Interest Rate Risk            | Risk of loss through movement in interest rates                                                               | Limit on re-pricing mismatch                                                                                                  |
| Pension Risk                  | The risk that the bank will be unable to meet its pension obligations                                         | Maintain sufficient surplus capital to cover scheme deficits                                                                  |
| Reputation Risk               | Risk that the bank's business activities result in a loss of trust from bank customers and other stakeholders | Minimum reputational quality from a variety of stakeholder groups                                                             |
| Conduct Risk                  | Business activities result in poor outcomes for customers                                                     | Mortgage applications will only be accepted from Prudential Regulation Authority/Financial Conduct Authority registered firms |
| Regulatory Standing           | The risk that the bank's standing with regulatory bodies deteriorates to the extent that it harms business    | Bank's activities will operate to avoid breaches of the regulatory framework                                                  |
| Operational Risk              | The risk of loss from inadequate or failed internal controls, human error or external events                  | Management is not willing to accept any single loss greater than £X, or combination of y losses in a quarter in excess of £Z  |

On its own, this is not sufficient though, as banks must develop their own  scenarios  to  complement  the  one  provided  by  the  regulator.  Firms should focus on their key vulnerabilities and, in common with the approach taken by regulators, aim to select scenarios that are both severe yet plausible. This is illustrated in Figure 20.9, which provides a graphical representation of the key assumptions behind the PRA's 2019 Supervisory Stress Scenario.

## ICAAP Modelling and Risk Mitigation

Once the base balance sheet date, the spectrum of risks facing the organisation and a range of stress scenarios have been agreed, banks are in a position to build models to assess the likely impact of each scenario on both earnings and the capital position.

In most instances, the gross stress outcome from running the models will result in a capital and income position that is below the base case forecast. If the capital position is lower than the bank's minimum capital ratio, it will need to think about the range of mitigating actions that could be taken.

Examples of mitigants could include reducing the risk profile by hedging, cutting the level of risky activity (e.g., lending to a particular market) or exiting business lines. An illustration of the potential impact of risk mitigation activity is provided in Figure 20.10. An actual output example from a UK commercial bank, showing that there is surplus capital after the impact of the 'anchor' stress scenario, is shown in Figure 20.11.

Figure 20.12 shows the interplay of the bank's risk metrics and warning  indicators,  stress  test  impact  on  balance  sheet  capital  and  management actions.

## I C A A P   C o m p o n e n t s

Each bank will devise its own approach, but a set of specimen table of contents would include:

- ■ business plan and strategy;
- ■ description of risk governance;
- ■ Statement of Risk Appetite, including a link to risk limits and strategy;
- ■ comparison with risk-  bearing capacity;
- ■ assessment of material risks;
- ■ description of stress scenarios;
- ■ impact of stress scenarios on material risks and the outcome for capital/earnings;
- ■ available mitigants;
- ■ reverse stress testing;

## Quarterly real GDP growth

House Price Index

<!-- image -->

FIGURE 20.9 PRA 2019 Supervisory Stress Scenario.

<!-- image -->

## Unemployment rate

<!-- image -->

Base rate

<!-- image -->

FIGURE 20.10 Risk mitigation: gross versus 'net' scenario outcome.

<!-- image -->

- ■ capital allocation process and policies;
- ■ use test - how the ICAAP analysis is embedded in the day to day business process.

Some regulators have published their suggested table of contents structure for banks to follow.

## ICAAP Business Benefits

The main beneficiary of the ICAAP is the bank that has prepared the document, as it delivers a greater understanding of the organisation's ability to withstand shocks. In summary, an ICAAP enhances a bank's ability to gauge how much capital flexibility exists to support risk appetite and acquisition strategy,  it  enables  understanding  of  capital  requirements  under  different economic  scenarios  and  stress  tests  and  builds  and  supports  the  linkage between risk and capital and ties performance to both. From the perspective of key external stakeholders, the ICAAP positions a bank to optimise the outcome of a regulator's review of capital requirements, potentially freeing up the maximum amount of capital to support strategic flexibility and strengthens a bank's position to respond to an increasing focus on capital by both rating agencies and analysts.

<!-- image -->

## Total Capital surplus

- Under stress, the bank continues to maintain a total capital surplus as available capital is sufficient to meet increases inrequired capital, although surplus is significantly less than under base case assumptions (£660m compared to £1,258m under base assumptions in 2018)
- The lowest capital surplus is £414m in 2014, rising to £660m in 2018

## Capital Ratios

- During the forecast period the CT1 ratio remains app. 12%, although is significantly below base case forecasts (4% lower than base case in 2018)

FIGURE 20.11 Stress test output after running market-  wide  'anchor' stress scenario, showing capital surplus at end of stress.

## Stress Test Visualisation

EWI = Early warning indicator

<!-- image -->

FIGURE 20.12 Stress test visualisation.

Source: Miranda Love, Faculty BTRM. Used with permission.

## The Supervisory Review and Evaluation Process (SREP)

In banks in the UK and EU, the SREP is a process by which the PRA:

- ■ reviews the arrangements, strategies, processes and mechanisms implemented by a bank to comply with PRA rules/ CRR IV/CRD IV;
- ■ evaluates the risks to which a firm is exposed;
- ■ assesses the risk that the firm poses to the financial system;
- ■ evaluates the further risks revealed by stress testing.

The review conducted by the regulator is much more detailed than a review of the ICAAP document. It will also include assessments of the governance  arrangements  of  the  firm,  the  involvement  of  the  firm's  governing  body in the ICAAP production and the corporate culture and values of the firm.

On the basis of the SREP, the PRA will determine whether the arrangements implemented by a bank and the capital held provide sound management and adequate coverage of risks. If necessary, the PRA will require the firm to take appropriate actions to address any future potential failure to meet its prudential regulatory requirements.

Although it may be called by a different name, an equivalent process is adopted by all member countries of the Basel Committee.

## Individual Capital Guidance and the Regulator Pillar 2B Buffer

Following the SREP, the regulator will give banks Individual Capital Guidance setting out the amount and quality of capital that it believes the firm should hold (Pillar 2A), in addition to the capital it must hold to comply with the CRR (Pillar 1). The advice to firms will always be on a consolidated basis and may also be on an individual basis to subsidiaries. The regulator may also advise firms of a specific supervisor's Buffer (Pillar 2B) that should be held to cover capital requirements in a real world stress event, after allowing for realistic management actions. A bank that does not meet its  regulatory  Buffer  can  expect  enhanced  supervisory  action  and  should prepare a capital restoration plan.

## Practical Issues and Best-  Practice ICAAP Preparation

We have already noted that the main beneficiary of the ICAAP is a bank's management as the process will reveal vital information about the level and types of risk the organisation is exposed to. As a result, it is essential that the ICAAP is not viewed as a regulatory tick-  box exercise.

The end-  to-  end ICAAP process can take around 8-12 months to complete in large organisations. A key challenge is the continuing relevance of a firm's ICAAP as the business will develop during the course of ICAAP preparation and PRA review/feedback, meaning that the document produced is quickly out of date.

When assessing their material risks ,  banks should seek to ensure that the modelling approach is appropriate for the type of risk being evaluated. Also, as well as capturing material risks in risk maps, risk appetite statements and so on, it is important to document any risk classified as 'nonmaterial' with reasons.

ICAAP inputs can originate from many different parts of a bank. If the nature of the agreed stress  scenarios is  not  well  understood by all stakeholders at the outset, then inconsistencies will arise in ICAAP outputs. For example, Business Unit A deduces that a particular stress scenario has positive implications for lending volumes, whereas Business Unit B, engaged in related activities, concludes the opposite. Workshops should be arranged by the central project team in the early phases of the project to discuss the stress scenarios with all stakeholders and ensure that there is a common understanding. Also, a broad range of internal stakeholders should be involved in the development of plausible but severe scenarios to stress test the key vulnerabilities of the firm.

A good data history is  of  paramount  importance. Weaknesses in the availability or quality of historic data is likely to adversely affect the predictive capability of models and lead to greater subjectivity in the production of stress testing results. A bank with poor data history, inaccurate entries and missing values will have to allocate time and resources to developing alternative data sources, data cleansing and subjective overlays, which will divert attention away from the development of models with a good predictive capability.

Banks must check that their list of risk mitigation actions ('management actions') is reviewed against the background of the stress scenario that is being tested to ensure they are still valid. For example, many organisations rely upon disposal of discretionary trading portfolios to free-  up balance sheet resources in a stress situation, but this approach would not have worked during the 2007-2008 financial crisis as there was a flight to quality and no market whatsoever in many instruments.

If the ICAAP is viewed as a tick-  box exercise to satisfy a set of regulatory rules, its preparation will most likely be under-  resourced and, as a consequence, little will be learnt about the risks to which the firm is exposed. A strong central project team is  required,  made  up  of  both  project  management resources and subject matter experts (SMEs). The project managers  are  responsible for planning the exercise, managing its completion to a  tight  timeline  and  putting  necessary  governance arrangements in place. SMEs, on the other hand, assess the full range of risks to which the firm is exposed, ensure that the modelled answers are realistic and check the handoffs between functions. The level of effort involved should match that given to the annual planning cycle and a full resource commitment is required by all stakeholders to the process.

ICAAP  preparation  requires cross-  functional  collaboration ,  which  is not always easy to achieve in large organisations. Senior management must agree where the stress testing function sits in the organisational structure and a clearly  defined  operating  model  (reviewed  by  Internal Audit).  Figure 20.13 illustrates how the different functions within the bank must work together closely and efficiently when working on the ICAAP process

In terms of governance, a single owner of the ICAAP will help to drive a consistent comprehensive approach (who may come from Risk, Treasury or Finance functions). For optimum results, key stakeholders in business units, Risk, Finance, Treasury, Strategy, Economics and so on, must be involved in its construction. Both senior management and the Board should be actively involved  in  the  review  and  challenge  process  and  there  should  be  a  formal sign-  off.

FIGURE 20.13 Different functions of a bank must work together closely to produce an effective value-added ICAAP.

<!-- image -->

According to the PRA, 'the ICAAP should be an integral part of the firm's management process and decision-  making culture'. This ' use test ' could, for example, be achieved by capturing the outputs from the ICAAP process in the setting of risk limits, pricing and/or capital allocation.

In an ideal world, all aspects of the business's financial plan and stress testing  activity  would  be  undertaken  in  one integrated  model (base  case economic scenario, base case business financial plan, capital plan and stress modelling of each key risk faced by the firm). In reality, though, these tend to be modelled as separate components, which means that care is required to ensure that the inter-  relationships between the components are explored. There will inevitably be a degree of subjectivity in the production of the final result.

If  a Pillar  2A  or  2B  overlay is  applied  as  part  of  Individual  Capital Guidance, banks must decide how, if at all, it should be allocated to business lines . If the level of the regulatory overlay differs from the bank's internal calculations of the level of capital required, the challenge is increased. If this process is not carefully managed, gaps can arise between the business unit view of the level of capital required, on which incentive schemes are based and the aggregate view.

It is important to recognise that ICAAP/SREP/ICG is only part of the capital  story.  In  the  US,  the  comparable  process,  CCAR  (Comprehensive Capital Analysis and Review) also captures Recovery and Resolution Planning to deliver a fully integrated 'end-  to-  end' capital assessment, which is updated by firms annually. In the UK, the PRA has also introduced Recovery and Resolution Planning, but it is separate to the ICAAP.

## I N T E R N A L   L I Q U I D I T Y   A D E Q U A C Y   A S S E S S M E N T   ( I L A A P )

We consider now the regulatory aspect of liquidity risk management and the process of the 'individual liquidity adequacy assessment' or ILAAP.

## Li q u i d i t y   S t r e s s   Te s t i n g

The liquidity stress testing process (illustrated in Figure 20.14) involves the modelling of assumptions on how assets and liabilities of a bank ( inventory ) will behave in various stress scenarios , to produce an output for review and action by senior management.

## Inventory

The inventory is the composition and maturity profile of the balance sheet (including off balance sheet exposures) on a given date - a basic building block for all modelling activity.

## Stress Scenarios

The calculation of the LCR is based upon a stress scenario set by the regulatory authorities. In contrast, the scenarios used for liquidity stress testing should be generated by banks and include a range of idiosyncratic, market and macroeconomic stresses. These should be severe, but plausible and focus upon key vulnerabilities. Examples could include: a sustained period of  systems  failure,  a  vulnerability  to  previously  liquid  markets  becoming illiquid (e.g., commercial paper or securitisation), heavy reliance on a particular sector for funding which becomes no longer available (local authorities in a credit downgrade scenario).

FIGURE 20.14 End-  to-  end liquidity stress testing process.

<!-- image -->

## Assumptions

Assumptions about the response of assets and liabilities to a given stress scenario are based on their historic behaviour in both normal and stressed conditions. The level of sophistication adopted will depend upon the amount of historic data available to support the modelling process. For example, if there is little or no history, assumptions might be developed at a product level only. Alternatively, if the product data can be broken down by customer type and cohort (by account opening date), a more advanced modelling approach may be adopted.

In forming behavioural assumptions, banks should consider the likely consequences of the stress scenario for the major sources of risk they face, which include:

- ■ retail funding risk;
- ■ wholesale secured and unsecured funding risk;
- ■ correlation and concentration of funding;
- ■ additional contingent off-  balance sheet exposures;
- ■ funding tenors;
- ■ intraday liquidity risk;
- ■ deterioration in the firm's credit rating;
- ■ foreign exchange convertibility and access to foreign exchange markets;
- ■ ability to transfer liquidity across entities, sectors and countries;
- ■ future balance sheet growth, impact on a firm's reputation and franchise;
- ■ marketable and non-  marketable asset risk; and
- ■ internalisation risk (which relates to the potential close-  out of customer short-  positions leading to outflows).

## Output

A key output from the stress testing process is the calculation of a cash-  flow survival period. This details how many days a bank's sources of liquidity (liquid assets, cash inflows and drawdown of contingent liquidity facilities) will  be  able  to  offset  the  outflows  that  are  assumed  in  a  stress  scenario. Banks will set a minimum acceptable survival period as part of their Liquidity Risk Appetite Statements.

Figure 20.15 is an example of the stress test output for a specified stress scenario,  showing  the  surplus  liquidity  available  at  the  end  of  the  stress period. In this scenario, the bank has implemented two management actions (labelled 'A' and 'B') during the stress period.

Figure  20.16  illustrates  a  summary  of  the  output  results  for  a  UK regional bank. This table would be useful as a high-  level report of the ILAAP results, and might be presented at the start of a Board review and challenge of the draft report. This exhibit presents the stress test output results for each type of stress scenario, which makes it ideal for high-  level review. The reference to 'OLAR' is the overall liquidity adequacy rule of the UK PRA. The concept of the OLAR is essentially that a bank must maintain sufficient liquidity  reserves  to  withstand  a  stress  event  that  is  in  line  with  its  own stated appetite for liquidity risk. Hence the '92 days' stated in the exhibit is user-  specified. 3

Note the description of the three types of stress test required to be applied in an ILAAP process, which is also valuable background for the high-  level reviewer and has been made relevant specifically for this institution.

## Review

The output of the stress testing process should be shared with the ALCO and be included in periodic updates to the Board. If the cash-  flow survival period exceeds the bank's risk appetite, then no further action is necessary, other than continuously reviewing the stress testing process and investing in areas like data collection to improve the quality of outputs over time. Alternatively, if the cash-  flow survival period is less than the risk appetite, then some form of corrective action is required, such as:

- ■ reduce exposures to certain products and markets;
- ■ reduce limits applied to contractual outflows (i.e., the amount of maturity transformation undertaken by the bank);
- ■ review  strategies  to  address  liquidity  shortfalls  in  the  Contingency Funding Plan.

The Contingency Funding Plan is a statement setting out a bank's strategies for addressing liquidity shortfalls in emergency situations; it will demonstrate how a bank will survive a given stress scenario. Quick, decisive, pre-  planned action is important in times of great uncertainty when confidence is low and may make the difference between bank survival and failure.

ILAAP stress test assumptions

| Stress description:   |                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                 |
|-----------------------|---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| Firm specific         | Severe stress event caused by prudential and/or reputational issue. Significant numbers of retail customers lose confidence in the bank and seek to withdraw balances, though the period of retail stress is relatively short lived (assumed in month), after which customers are re-assured by the bank continuing to function and by presence of the government deposit guarantee scheme. There is almost no recent data to model the effect of a retail stress, apart from the case of Northern Rock. Anecdotal comment suggests that that firm lost approximately 15% of its retail balances during the stress period. The benefit of the deposit guarantee system was not widely understood then, however. Given much better understanding of the system now, it is assumed that retail withdrawals would be about half those experienced in that event. No wholesale funds are assumed to renew during the stress period. |
| Market wide           | Market-wide disruption that is not seen to carry negative connotations for small regional banks. There is no apparent retail funding stress and once it becomes clear that the bank is not adversley affected, additional retail inflows would be expected. Wholesale counterparties with whom the bank transacts may take a precuationary stance and therefore we assume that wholesale funding does not renew. If economic activity generally or financial services activity specifically are adversely affected, there may be less loan renewal and remortgage activity and so redemptions and prepayments may reduce.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                       |
| Combined              | Market-wide disruption that is seen to adversely affect the bank, although we assume that the stress is not as severe as under the Firm Specific assumption. There are some retail withdrawals in the first month (assumed at about half the rate seen in the Firm Specific stress) and again the situation stabilises thereafter. Loan renewal and remortgage activity (and therefore mortgage redemptions) are assumed to be lower.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                           |

FIGURE 20.16 ( continued ).

## I L A A P   D r a f t i n g

The stress test results are drafted into the Individual Liquidity Adequacy Assessment (ILAAP) which should incorporate:

- ■ a clearly articulated risk appetite defining the duration and type of stress the firm aims to survive;
- ■ a range of stress scenarios focusing upon key vulnerabilities of the firm;
- ■ the results of stress tests; and
- ■ those measures set out in the Contingency Funding Plan that it would implement.

The ILAAP should also be:

- ■ recorded in a document approved by the Board;
- ■ proportionate to the nature, scale and complexity of a bank's activities; and
- ■ updated  annually  or  more  frequently  if  the  business  model  of  the firm changes.

Although there is no pre-  prescribed format for an ILAAP, the headings shown in Figure 20.17 are recommended by the author. Above all, banks must ensure that their ILAAPs inform decision   making and risk management and are not seen to be just a compliance exercise.

## Liquidity  Supervisory  Review  and  Evaluation  Process (L-  SREP)

In the UK, this is the review of the ILAA by the regulator. It is a broad assessment which will also capture items like:

- ■ whether the institution has an appropriate framework and IT system for identifying liquidity risk;
- ■ whether the governance framework around the liquidity risk management process is sufficient;
- ■ whether there is an adequate transfer pricing mechanism for liquidity;
- ■ whether there are adequate controls over the liquid asset buffer;
- ■ whether the institution defines and communicates its liquidity risk strategy and tolerance;
- ■ whether there is a comprehensive internal limit and control framework for liquidity risk management.

- 1 Executive Summary
- 2 Firm business model, governance structure and strategy
- 3 Firm risk management framework
- 4 Forecast balance sheet
- 5 Funding profile and plan
- 6 Liquidity risk definition and ownership
- 7 Liquidity risk management framework]
- 8 Liquidity risk appetite
- 9 ILAAP Coverage
- 10 Stress scenarios
- 11 Stress testing results (covering all identified material liquidity and funding risks)
- 12 Pillar 2 Liquidity
- 13 Liquidity risk assessment
- 14 Liquidity risk mitigants and specific management actions tables
- 15 High quality liquid assets policy and portfolio
- 16 Other sources of funding / mitigants
- 17 Reverse Stress Test
- 17 ILAAP challenge and internal approval process
- 18 Use of ILAAP in the firm Appendices
- A1 Reference to LCP
- A2 Assumptions used in stress testing
- A3 FTP Policy
- A4 Deposit pricing policy and model

FIGURE 20.17 Author's recommended ILAAP Table of Contents.

## Individual Liquidity Guidance

Following the L-  SREP, the PRA (in the UK) will give Individual Liquidity Guidance to banks. Typically, this will cover whether the quantity and quality of liquid assets held by the bank is sufficient, whether the firm's funding profile is appropriate and any further qualitative arrangements the firm should undertake to mitigate its liquidity risk. Note, the quantitative guidance extends beyond the liquidity buffer the firm is required to maintain under the LCR and will cover liquidity risks to which the firm is exposed, but which are not captured by the LCR.

A  similar  process  exists  in  other  jurisdictions  subject  to  the  Basel regulations.

## REVERSE STRESS TESTING: DEFINITION, APPROACH AND RELEVANCE

Reverse Stress Testing (RST) is generally defined as the analysis of scenarios that render the business model unviable. While both traditional stress testing and RST involve scenarios and scenario-  related impacts, they differ in two key aspects: the first difference relates to the direction :  in  traditional stress tests, banks start with defining a (macro-  financial) scenario and then assess its impact on their business, typically, in terms of earnings, capital and liquidity. RST starts with defining the outcome followed by reverse-  engineering scenarios that, should they unfold, lead to the specified result. The second difference relates to the severity of the stress: RST can go further into the tail end of the probability distribution compared to other risk measures. RST scenarios are often designed to be so severe that they 'break the bank'.

In some jurisdictions the regulator demands that the bank incorporate at least one RST in its ICAAP (and ILAAP if there is one), hence there is no choice in the matter.

## Approach

We suggest a qualitative approach that consists of the six steps shown in Figure 20.18.

<!-- image -->

FIGURE 20.18

Steps in the RST approach.

## Step 1: Definition of Failure Points

The  first  step  is  the  definition  of  so-  called 'failure  points',  defined,  for example, as points at which a bank's business model becomes unviable. We distinguish three perspectives: firstly, a failure from an earnings perspective, secondly, from a capital perspective, and thirdly from a liquidity perspective. While there may be interlinkages, it is important to consider these as separate dimensions.

## Step 2: Vulnerability Analysis and Risk Inventory

The purpose of the vulnerability analysis is to identify the primary material risks to which the bank is exposed through the business it conducts and the

clients it serves. The output of Step 2 is a risk inventory and a corresponding classification, for example, as shown in Figure 20.19 by failure type and time horizon.

## Step 3: Generic Storyboards

The storyboard encompasses the development of scenario narratives (from status quo to the point where the business model becomes unviable). The framework proposes to obtain a big picture perspective first (and not to get lost in detail by many possible storyboards). By drawing from the classification in Figure 20.19, eight groups of generic storyboards can be derived. The next step is to develop scenarios for each of the eight case groups.

## Step 4: Scenario Design and Parametrisation

The next step in the framework is to specify the building blocks of the storyboard, that is, to calibrate each building block. This step will provide further insight into the range of risk factors contributing to the scenario, their financial impacts, and how they would change under stress. The calibration may, but is not required to, rely on guidance derived from standard scenario analysis, such as historically observed or regulatory-  imposed scenarios. This is followed by the next step, plausibility checks and management actions.

## Step 5: Plausibility Checks and Management Actions

With regard to plausibility checks, the calibration should be carefully sensechecked and, where possible, 'back-  tested' against existing stress events. If the likelihood of an RST scenario, and hence risk of business failure, is deemed to be unacceptably high, the bank must take mitigating actions. The open-  ended nature of RST means that appropriate mitigating management actions should depend very much on the type of the scenario identified, and its constituent material risk drivers. In general however, it is not necessarily the case that any management actions are apparent, given the nature of the severity of the scenario impact in an RST.

## Step 6: Monitoring and Reporting

RST scenarios and vulnerabilities should be monitored regularly to trigger management discussions and actions. Over time users will observe changes of likelihood levels. If - for a given RST scenario - overall scenario likelihood levels increase,  managers  should  propose  formal  mitigating  actions and escalate to the relevant governance body. Scenario monitoring may be conducted primarily through indicators linked to their constituent risk factors. Finally, the overall RST owner, usually a working group or a committee, should be responsible for consolidating the RST results for presentation to the governing bodies. Reports should be produced at least annually to achieve awareness of the gained insight from both the findings as well as from running the process itself.

FIGURE 20.19 RST scenarios by failure type and time horizon.

<!-- image -->

|               | Liquidity failure                                                                      | Capital failure                                                                           | Capital induced liquidity failure                                                                                                         | Earnings failure                                                                                                  |
|---------------|----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| Instantaneous | Case 1a Severe risk event results in liquidity failure (impact on capital is minor)    | Case 2a Sudden exhaustion of capital basis                                                | Case 3a Sudden deterioration of capital base raises substantial doubt about future viability of business, leading to liquidity failure    | Case 4a Sudden changes resulting in negative PTI impact for the foreseeable future making business model unviable |
| Bleeding out  | Case 1b Series of risk events result in liquidity failure (impact on capital is minor) | Case 2b Gradual exhaustion of capital basis, emergency liquidity facility still available | Case 3b Continued deterioration of capital base raises substantial doubt about future viability of business, leading to liquidity failure | Case 4b Continued changes resulting in negative PTI impact making business model unviable                         |

## Relevance for Bank Risk Management

We have focused on the following points:

1. Regulatory requirements: Regulators across the globe expect banks to complement sensitivity analysis and scenario analysis with RST.
2. Strategic risks: RST can help to identify strategic risks which may materialise prior to a capital and/or liquidity failure.
3. Severity: RST allows to capture high severity events not covered by sensitivity analysis and scenario analysis. For example, banks' ICAAPs are sometimes criticised for not being severe enough, for instance due to cognitive  biases  and  disaster  myopia. As  noted,  RST  can  be  used  to 'backtest' the existing scenario inventory for relevance and severity.
4. Recovery  and  Resolution  Planning  (RRP): There  are  various  links between RST and RRP. For example, RST work may be seen as a special case of Recovery Planning. Or, it may inform how banks arrive at points that trigger the Resolution Planning.
5. Risk integration: RST can help to better understand the dynamic interplay of risks, capital and liquidity over time, including feedback loops, non-  linear  effects  and  combinations  and  (inter)dependencies  between risk types. Consequently, it can help to identify so-  called hidden risks (not picked up during the traditional stress tests) and gather insights into scenarios that involve combinations of solvency and liquidity stresses where traditional modelling may fail to capture complex aspects from real situations.
6. RST can provide new insights by looking at individual sensitivities in reverse, such as: which liquidity risk factors need to change, by how much, to reduce the liquidity buffer below a given amount, for example, US$10 billion (failure point)? Or which size of shocks of one or multiple currencies cause a reduction of CET1 ratio by, let's say, 15 bps? Or what size of unexpected outflows of non-  maturing deposits could result in a loss of US$1 million (failure point), for example, due to the need to unwind hedges at a loss that are no longer matched?

FIGURE 20.20 Building blocks of RST.

<!-- image -->

In banking, RST is not as developed as are other aspects of risk management. Approaches and even the sequence of steps frequently differ. However, RST can offer a new world of discussions, even more so if the individual dimensions summarised in Figure 20.20 are considered as a morphological box and combined in multiple ways. For example, assume that a bank's Board comments that its RST scenarios are too remote. The risk manager could lower the loss threshold to a few million dollars to discuss scenarios that do not kill the bank but may test the Board's risk acceptance for a particular portfolio. This is what we call 'mild RST'. It is also a useful exercise in getting the Board to engage with risk exposure and risk appetite under stable and stressed economic circumstances.

Overall,  RST  may  be  best  applied  as  a  thinking  tool  (in  many  ways similar to reverse brainstorming as a creativity tool).

## CHAPTER SUMMARY

The primary beneficiary of a bank's stress testing process is the bank's Board of  Directors  and  its  other  stakeholders.  In  other  words,  there  is  value  in undertaking 'what if?' type scenarios on a regular basis to understand how the bank would be impacted under stressed conditions.

That said, the capital and liquidity adequacy processes that banks are obliged to implement and report on mean that there is no choice in the matter, it is a formal requirement.

The ICAAP is an annual process to determine the impact on a bank's capital  position  in  a  stressed  market  environment.  The  objective  is  to demonstrate that the bank has sufficient capital to remain a going concern in the event of the assumed scenarios playing out in reality. A similar process is undertaken, annually in most jurisdictions, for liquidity adequacy.

The RST is required in some jurisdictions to show what extreme conditions it would require for the bank to fail.

## REFERENCES

Choudhry, M. (1999), Introduction to Value-  at-  Risk .  London:  Securities  Institute Publishing.

Eichhorn, M., et al. (2021), Reverse Stress Testing in Banking , Berlin: De Gruyter.

## Recovery Planning and Resolution

## Abstract

Recovery planning and resolution planning are regulatory requirements that follow Basel Committee guidance. We introduce the key elements of recovery planning, which may be viewed as an extension of the ICAAP and ILAAP processes under more severe stressed conditions, and the key requirements of resolution planning.

## RECOVERY PLANNING AND RESOLUTION PLANNING 1

In  this  chapter,  we  will  examine  the  Recovery  and  Resolution  Planning (RRP) requirements that have been established by global regulators since the 2008 financial crisis.

The purpose of RRP is to establish a global framework to deal effectively with banks that are on the brink of failure or have reached the point of  non-  viability.  It  is  a  mechanism  that  evolved  from  the  financial  crisis, which highlighted how difficult in a time of stress it was to work through a series of initiatives that could result in the credit institution recovering from the stress or resolving it in a timely fashion. In essence it is targeted to avoid 'too big to fail' bank bail-  outs.

RRP is mainly aimed at large banks and larger investment firms that are subject to the Capital Requirements Regime (CRR) in Europe and the Dodd-  Frank Act in the US and where failure can have a significant impact on the domestic or the global economy, but in some jurisdictions smaller institutions are also in scope. In the UK, for instance, all banks are required to submit a recovery plan.

Please also note the PowerPoint slide deck in the folder for Chapter 21, on this book's companion website, which discusses both recovery planning and resolution planning in more detail.

## What Is RRP?

RRP is a core requirement of the regulatory authorities in Europe and the US and is a supervisory tool used by the Bank of England in the UK. There

1  Special thanks to Bruce Walker, former Faculty BTRM, for review comments on an earlier draft of this chapter.

## CHAPTER  21 Planning

is  a  regulatory  requirement  to  have  completed  one  as  part  of  succession planning ('Living Wills').

There is a slightly different approach for large and systemically important banks compared to smaller banks.

The plan itself is not shared with the market, given the sensitive nature of the information contained within it, much like an ILAAP document. It will cover areas such as the size and structure of some banks and take into account  any  cross-  border  jurisdictional  arrangements  that  may  already exist. It will also identify any critical economic functions which the organisation undertakes, such as payments, settlements and deposit taking.

A key challenge to this requirement is that RRP requires new thinking in how to structure debt in an organisation, considering that all banks do not have the same operating business model. A one-  size-  fits-  all approach does not work so bespoke solutions are needed.

## Key Attributes of the RRP Framework

In 2011, the Financial Stability Board (FSB) drew up the Key Attributes of Effective Resolution Regimes for Financial Institutions (FIs), the objective being to allow the resolution of FIs

without severe systemic disruption and without exposing taxpayers to loss,  while  protecting  vital  economic  functions  through  mechanisms which make it possible for shareholders and unsecured and uninsured creditors  to  absorb  losses  in  a  manner  that  respects  the  hierarchy  of claims in liquidation .

The key principles of an effective regime are:

- ■ ensure continuity of systemically important financial services;
- ■ protect insured depositors / investors;
- ■ allocate losses to firm owners and uninsured creditors;
- ■ do not rely on public solvency support;
- ■ avoid unnecessary destruction of value;
- ■ provide speed, transparency and predictability;
- ■ mandate in law for information exchange and cooperation;
- ■ ensure non-  viable firms exit market in an orderly way;
- ■ be credible.

The FSB broke down the essential features for resolution regimes across all jurisdictions. The scope of this was to ensure that any bank, which could be systemically significant or critical if it failed, would be captured under this framework - by definition, all G-  SIFIs and SIFIs.

The framework outlined a series of powers that were given to the relevant regulators to ensure a fast and effective transition. These included:

- ■ remove and replace senior management;
- ■ appoint an administrator;
- ■ operate and resolve the bank;
- ■ ensure continuity of essential services and functions through other companies in the same group;
- ■ override rights of shareholders;
- ■ transfer or sell assets and liabilities;
- ■ establish a temporary bridge institution to control critical functions and viable operations;
- ■ establish a separate asset management vehicle;
- ■ carry out bail-  in;
- ■ temporarily stay the exercise of early termination rights;
- ■ impose a moratorium on payments to unsecured creditors and customers;
- ■ effect  the  closure  and  orderly  wind  down  of  the  whole  or  part  of  a failing bank.

The framework would also provide clear specific guidance on set-  off, netting,  collateralisation  and  segregation  of  client  assets  that  would  be enforceable during a crisis or resolution of banks and should not hamper the effective resolution.

There  were  some  specific  safeguards  that  were  introduced  into  the framework  to  address  particular  legal  issues  that  had  been  identified  in previously attempted recovery and resolution exercises during the financial crisis. These were:

- ■ respect for the creditor hierarchy and 'no creditor worse off' principle;
- ■ legal remedies and judicial action.

Other principles included how to deal with the funding of firms that had entered the resolution stage. These included:

- ■ the objective is to avoid public ownership or bail-  outs;
- ■ jurisdictions should have privately funded deposit insurance schemes / resolution funds;
- ■ in  extremis authorities  are  allowed  to  provide  temporary  funding  to achieve an orderly position.

A legal framework was established to deal with cross-  border cooperation  amongst  the  regulatory  community  given  that  the  attention  was  on large institutions that would span several jurisdictions. These were:

- ■ a coordinated and cooperative approach is encouraged among different jurisdictions;
- ■ a resolution authority should have powers over local branches to act in conjunction with a foreign authority or on its own initiative;
- ■ confidentiality requirements and statutory safeguards for protection of information received from foreign authorities.

As part of the framework, a resolvability assessment is required (for at least G-  SIFIs) to evaluate the feasibility of resolution strategies. This should focus  on  the  credibility  of  the  strategies  outlined  by  the  institutions  and should assess the impact of these strategies on the financial system and overall economy. This assessment should include:

- ■ coordination of critical financial services;
- ■ intra-  group exposures;
- ■ capacity of the bank to deliver accurate and timely information;
- ■ robustness of cross-  border cooperation and information sharing agreements.

## What Should Be Contained Within a Bank's RRP Document?

A bank's recovery plan should include:

- ■ credible options to deal with both idiosyncratic and market-  wide stress;
- ■ credible options for capital shortfalls and liquidity pressures;
- ■ a  process  to  provide  timely  implementation  of  recovery  options  in  a range of stress situations.

A bank's resolution plan should include:

- ■ a detailed assessment of the financial and economic functions for which continuity is critical;
- ■ suitable  resolution  options  to  preserve  those  functions  or  wind  them down in an orderly manner;
- ■ data  on  the  bank's  business  operations,  structures  and  systemically important functions;
- ■ potential barriers to effective resolution and  actions to mitigate the barriers;
- ■ actions to protect insured depositors and insurance policy holders to ensure rapid return of assets;
- ■ options for exit from the resolution process.

Recovery  and  resolution  plans  are  to  be  updated  regularly  (at  least annually)  or  on  material  changes  to  a  bank's  business  or  structure  and agreed with the bank's regulator.

## Regimes and Powers

To better understand the requirements that banks are facing, let's examine the two RRP regimes that are in existence today across the EU and the US which both address the same issues but in different ways. A summary of the two approaches is given in Figure 21.1.

## The European Approach

It  applies  to  all  institutions  and  contains  three  main  pillars  consistently applied across the eurozone:

- ■ Preparation and prevention
- ■ Early intervention
- ■ Resolution.

FIGURE 21.1 Key differences between EU and US recovery and resolution regimes.

|                      | EU                                                                                                        | US                                                                                                        |
|----------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| Relevant Legislation | Bank Resolution &Recovery Directive, July 2014                                                            | Title I and Title II of the Dodd-Frank Act, July 2011                                                     |
| In force             | Main legislation Jan 2015 (with bail-in measures introduced January 2016)                                 | July 2011                                                                                                 |
| Scope                | All credit institutions and investment firms                                                              | Only large and complex banks (consolidated assets of US$50bn or over)                                     |
| Resolution Authority | New Resolution Board comprised of national and European authorities                                       | Existing Federal Deposit Insurance Corp                                                                   |
| Recovery Plan        | Annual review, update and submission to resolution authority / supervisor                                 | Not required                                                                                              |
| Resolution Plan      | Annual review and update with resolution authority ownership                                              | Annual review, update and submission to resolution authority, bank ownership                              |
| Triggers             | Failing or likely to fail, protection of public interest and financial stability, no private alternatives | Failing or likely to fail, protection of public interest and financial stability, no private alternatives |
| Resolution Funds     | Covers losses after bail-in liabilities, used for liquidity and recapitalisation                          | Funds bridge company, no recapitalisation                                                                 |

The resolution authorities are the existing authorities / central banks that currently oversee the banks.

Recovery planning is an important early warning safeguard and resolution planning is undertaken and owned by the authority (but with input from the institution). The European model considers either Single or Multiple Points of Entry, which means that the banking institution can be resolved through various means.

The resolution tools and powers available are:

- ■ sale of business;
- ■ bridge institution;
- ■ asset separation;
- ■ bail-  in.

As a means to address the issues of cross border resolution, a Resolution College of Regulators is established to discuss cross-  border issues in more detail pertaining to each institution independently.

## The US Approach

The US approach is a little different and was established much earlier on through the Dodd-Frank legislation that was born very soon after the end of the financial crisis in 2011.

Traditionally liquidation was the US route for resolving bank failure but G-  SIFIs posed a bigger threat given their linkage across multiple jurisdictions which were not all in control of the US authorities.

Smaller banks will still be dealt with by sale of assets (where possible) and liquidation.

The Dodd-Frank Act brought in a new regime for G-  SIFIs and institutions that had a balance sheet in excess of US$50 billion. This new regime introduced four levels of greater scrutiny:

- ■ heightened supervisory review;
- ■ initial remediation;
- ■ recovery;
- ■ recommended resolution.

It determined that the Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation (FDIC) was to be the resolution authority and ensured that it had increased powers to deal with this new responsibility.

It also created the Orderly Liquidation Fund, which was to be used once the FDIC had put a firm into receivership.

The US regime preferred single point of entry to the European approach of multiple points of entry.

Under the Dodd-Frank Act, the following tools were available to the resolution authorities:

- ■ Sell the business -
- ■ without consent of shareholders;
- ■ Without usual procedural requirements;
- ■ transparently and on commercial terms.;
- ■ in parts where it achieves the best result and protects the public.
- ■ Bridge bank -
- ■ transfer the business to a bridge institution in public ownership;
- ■ resolution authorities have full discretion on bridge entity, management and risk profile;
- ■ objective is to house the failed bank whilst making it sellable.
- ■ Asset separation -
- ■ separate  the  toxic  assets  to  maximise  liquidation  proceeds,  protect markets or allow a bridge bank to function;
- ■ usually  operates  in  conjunction  with  another  resolution  tool  (e.g. bridge bank).

## Bail-  I n   P r o c e s s

The bail-  in process ensures that the bank owners and unsecured creditors contribute to failed banks first following the traditional capital hierarchy as described in Figure 21.2. The bank's owners and creditors are written down or converted into equity to recapitalise the bank.

FIGURE 21.2 The bail-  in process - creditor waterfall. © Bruce Walker. Used with permission.

<!-- image -->

The resolution authority can exclude liabilities from bail-  in in certain circumstances, and most have chosen to exclude retail deposits from the bail in hierarchy.

## Resolution Tools Explained Numerically

Figure 21.3 is a numeric representation of what would happen if a bail process were to be triggered. It follows four key states: pre-  bail in showing the structure of the balance sheet; the loss absorption showing how the loss  is  written  off;  the  recapitalisation  state  showing  what  liabilities  are converted to equity to allow the bank to continue operating; and the final state as a post-  bail-  in entity with a smaller balance sheet as a result of the write down.

In this example, the bank faces a loss of £10 which forces it into resolution territory, as it no longer has any capital to support the asset base. The capital of £9 is written off as well as £1 of sub-  debt to match off the £10 loss. In the recapitalisation phase, the next layer in the credit waterfall is used to recapitalise the bank, which is done through converting the remaining £2 of sub-  debt and using a further £7 of senior debt. This translates into an entity post-  bail in having a balance sheet sized at £90 (the remaining good assets on the balance sheet) supported by liabilities and equity totalling £90, which includes an equity component of £9. We note from Figure 21.3 that the Insured Depositors throughout this exercise are left untouched and that the liabilities are written off in the order as per Figure 21.2.

FIGURE 21.3 Bail-in process explained numerically.

<!-- image -->

## The Importance of Capital Structures and Loss Absorbing Capital

There are implications for how banks fund and capitalise themselves with regard to the new Basel III capital rules and regulators' stress tests. Clear considerations need to be given with respect to:

- ■ The  use  of  contingent  convertibles  (Co-  Cos)  in capital structure given that their conversation to equity is dependent on certain events materialising.
- ■ Where debt is held - single point of entry versus multiple points of entry.
- ■ Resolution fund rules of when to support:
- ■ US after 10% of consolidated assets wiped out;
- ■ EU after 8% of total liabilities bailed in.
- ■ Impact  of  EU  Minimum  Requirement  of  Eligible  Liabilities  (MREL) ratio above capital ratios.
- ■ Ratings impact of where debt is held.
- ■ Solvent wind down and ring fencing of retail banks.

## CONCLUSION

RRP is an important part of the post-  financial crisis rules set that was developed to address certain failings in some institutions and to protect the taxpayer from having to come to the rescue of the banking sector.

It is important that Recovery and Resolution Plans should include:

- ■ key recovery and resolution strategies and a plan to implement them;
- ■ clear options for both recovery and resolution;
- ■ solid and robust analysis to support the strategies for recovery;
- ■ early  warning  triggers  for  implementation  of  both  recovery  and resolution;
- ■ preparatory actions for effective and timely implementation of the plans;
- ■ potential material impediments to execution of the plans;
- ■ clear responsibility for preparatory actions, executing the plan and corrective measures if necessary.

To conclude, an effective Resolution and Recovery requires the following from both sides of the regulatory framework:

## Regulator

- ■ Clearly designed regulatory framework
- ■ Oversight of bank recovery and resolution plans

- ■ Sufficient legal powers to do what's necessary
- ■ International coordination where required

## Bank

- ■ A detailed due diligence and full breakdown of the bank's structure and balance sheet
- ■ Effective internal policies in place to support recovery and resolution planning
- ■ A full understanding at senior level of detailed recovery plans
- ■ Clear lines of communication and allocation of responsibility

The next big bank failure will show if the resolution legislation actually works in practice.

## CHAPTER SUMMARY

There are complex regulatory rules governing the recovery planning and resolution plan processes that banks must follow. There are also detailed differences between the approach taken in the USA, EU and UK, as well as other jurisdictions.

Recovery planning is ultimately a 'going concern' process as the bank will be demonstrating the actions it can take, feasibly, in a highly stressed condition and remain viable by restoring its KRIs to green.

The Resolution Plan is a 'gone concern' process as it states what the bank will do to facilitate as seamless and trouble free a winding up (or other resolution solution) as possible once the bank has already been deemed to be a gone (that is, failed and unviable) firm.

## REFERENCE

Prudential Regulation Authority (2020), Recovery Planning , Supervisory Statement SS9/17.

## Abstract

Banks today are following a strategy in line with a more sustainable approach to commerce, and also in reaction to the impact of climate change on the economy. Regulation is also requiring banks to adopt a more 'green' approach to originating customer business. We approach the topic from the perspective of the balance sheet manager at a bank, and the perspective of ALCO.

A s Chapters 1 and 18 showed, banks have always been exposed to a large number of varied risks incurred in undertaking the ordinary course of business. To these we must add environmental risk and climate change risk. Unsurprisingly, the Basel Committee as well as national regulatory authorities  have  published  guidance  on  this  topic  that  banks  are  obliged  to  be compliant with; that said, in the field of sustainable banking and environmentally friendly banking there are also areas where a bank may wish to follow its own course of action, not influenced by supervisor edit.

## SUSTAINABLE BANKING AND GREEN BANKING

Green banking is a recent development in banking, and as with much in finance, there is more than one way to define it.

An internet search defined it as:

any form of banking from which the country and nation gets environmental benefits. A conventional bank becomes a green bank by directing its core operations towards the betterment of the environment. . . . It means developing inclusive banking strategies which will  ensure  substantial  economic  development  and  promoting environmental-  friendly practices as well. 1

1  https://www.greengrowthknowledge.org/research/green-  banking-  going-  green

## Sustainable Banking and Climate

## CHAPTER  22 Change Risk Management

The author's definition is perhaps less utopian and recognises that banks cannot, on their own, 'change the world':

Green banking is undertaking banking (comprised of deposit taking and lending activity), that takes into account the 'Environmental and Social' impact of so doing and factors this impact into its overall business decision making.

Barua (2021) has suggested the following as a template for principles of green banking:

| Management/Board-level principles        | Operational level principles                             |
|------------------------------------------|----------------------------------------------------------|
| (1) Long-term orientation                | (6) Value environmentally responsi- ble clients          |
| (2) Planet and people before profit      | (7) Environmental risk based project valuation           |
| (3) Fitting into vision and strategy     | (8) Favourable fund pricing                              |
| (4) Continuous management priority       | (9) Adopt formal approach                                |
| (5) Resource commitments are investments | (10) Responsible environmental accounting and disclosure |

## Green Banking Principles

Let's consider these in turn.

## Principle 1: This Is a Long-  Term Policy

Transitioning to green banking is a long-  term process. It requires a mindset change that reduces the importance of the short-  term profit motive and places more emphasis on 'sustainable' business in the long run. The rationale is that the value of this potential benefit in the long run outweighs the benefit that they expect to generate in the short run. A long-  term focus is necessary because much of the green initiatives in banking undertaken either voluntarily or by regulation takes time to deliver results. For example, the introduction of a new green credit or savings product could take time to become well perceived and well known to target customers; bank management and employees may take time to adapt psychologically and morally.

Hence,  preparing  green  banking  policy  and  strategy  documents  and implementing them in business requires a long-  term approach.

## Principle 2: Planet and People Equal to, if not Before, Profit

The accepted view in 2021 is that a focus solely on 'profit' or return on capital may not be sustainable in the long run. As banks are a key segment of society overall, banks should act more responsibly and ethically than, say, other corporate entities (from an indirect angle, when compared with, say, hydrocarbons extraction, energy generating or mobile phone producing corporates). If the local, and global, environment, are maintained in a manner that is sustainable, this helps ensure society and people are also sustainable, which in turn ensures sustainability for commercial business.

Realising  this  requires  incorporation  in  the  principles  of  the  bank stakeholders,  including  senior  management  executives  and  the  regulators of banks. Placing this objective in line with, if not ahead of RoC should be viewed as a long-  run benefit for both banks and society.

## Principle 3: Fitting into Vision and Strategy

To be genuinely impactful (as opposed to mere 'virtue signalling') banks have to incorporate green banking and environment-  friendly banking concepts into their vision, mission, strategy and customer-  level operations. This ensures  the  whole  firm  becomes  an  environmentally  responsible  entity; for instance:

- ■ Corporate  policy  and  strategy  documents  should  include  green  principles  as  a  primary  mandate  to  follow  and  be  communicated  to  all stakeholders
- ■ Board, management, executives, and the organisation as a whole, should implement a comprehensive environment-  friendly banking policy and practices.

Potentially, this may require that all employees be given orientation and training on this common 'green vision' commitment so that they can practise it in full in their 'day job'. This has practical impact at its greatest in the 1st LoD.

## Principle 4: Continuous Management Priority

A potential obstacle in implementing green practices is loss of continuing priority by the management and the board. Tactical level internal and external activities of a bank need to maintain the focus on environmental protection - it is a continuous and continuing process. In other words, the green approach to bank origination would become the 'new normal' model for regular banking business.

## Principle 5: Resource Commitments Viewed as Capital Investment

Some studies suggest that green banking frameworks have not been adopted in full due to the perception that dedicating physical, financial and human resources for this has no visible financial or non-  financial outcome (Rahman and Barua, 2016).

'Greening' bank practices require substantial financial investment and engagement of human resources; for example, altering bank infrastructures such as head office and branch offices to make them environment-  friendly, introducing technology-  based platforms for the banking process to reduce paper and human involvement, and training on green banking for all bank employees including those who are responsible for delivering green banking services to customers and those who need to perform in-  house operations such as ensuring regulatory compliance. These investments often could be substantially large and go beyond the means of small-  scale banks.

## Principle 6: Value Environmentally Responsible Clients

Banks  deal  with  client  groups  of  diverse  background  and  industry.  Ideally, adopting  an  environment-  friendly  business  practice  means  valuing  environmentally responsible customers higher and therefore delivering 'preferential' services to clients who act or intend to act responsibly to the environment; for example, on interest rates charged or paid, or providing premium or privilege service. This is not a trivial policy decision. (Think not just of coal mining companies but also airlines, bitcoin electricity suppliers, rare earth metals extraction, tourist industry, meat processing plants - pretty much every corporate entity has some level of impact.) To be genuinely impactful, as opposed to 'virtue signalling', requires a major appraisal of the types of customers that a bank wishes to conduct business with. There is also not a universal agreement on which industrial sectors contribute more or less to environmental degradation.

## Principle 7: Environmental Risk-  Based Project Valuation

When assessing  and  appraising  any  project,  firm  or  asset,  banks  should assess  its  involvement  with  the  environment,  and  the  nature  and  magnitude of damage the project may create to the environment.
